section_title: One customer, about two-thirds of revenue
body_text: Microsoft was roughly 67% of FY2025 revenue, and no other customer exceeded 10%. That concentration is disclosed plainly in the 10-K, yet Microsoft was named zero times on the Q1 2026 earnings call - the single most important risk to the business documented where required but not volunteered on the call. Committed contracts represent over 98% of revenue, so nearly all income is the backlog converting. Diversification is underway but early: the company signed a master services agreement with OpenAI in May 2025 and names Meta as another significant customer, but FY2025 revenue was still overwhelmingly Microsoft. Several of the largest customers are also competitors - Amazon, Google, Microsoft, and Oracle each rent capacity - and Microsoft is at once the biggest customer and, through Azure, a direct rival.
qualifier: from the 10-K
vault_ref: CRWV company page / customer concentration

section_title: The colocation crossover
body_text: In the quarter ended March 2026, Core Scientific's colocation revenue reached $77.5 million and overtook Bitcoin self-mining revenue ($30.1 million) for the first time - the crossover the company had been building toward. GAAP colocation gross margin jumped to 56.7%, up from around 30% in 2025, as billable megawatts ramped. At 243 megawatts billing, that equates to more than $350 million of annualized colocation revenue under GAAP. Under the accounting, revenue from the CoreWeave contract is recognized on a straight-line basis over the twelve-year lease term, which effectively pulls the contract's escalators forward. Management separately raised its cash-gross-profit target for the CoreWeave contract to 80-85%, up from an original 75-80% - but that is a non-GAAP management target, kept distinct from the realized 56.7% GAAP figure.
qualifier: from the quarterly filing and earnings call
vault_ref: CORZ company page / colocation crossover + run-rate

section_title: Analyst estimates
body_text: Sell-side analysts estimate that the next architecture roughly doubles memory bandwidth again. This is an analyst estimate, not a company figure. Prior estimates of this kind have been directionally right before, and early on timing before.
qualifier: estimate
vault_ref: NVDA / sell-side estimates

section_title: Lead times
body_text: Industry survey data from mid-2025 put lead times for large power transformers at roughly 128 weeks - about two and a half years - with generator step-up units at roughly 144 weeks and custom large orders quoted at over four years. The historical baseline was about 50 weeks. Even the small pad-mount transformers that connect individual sites stretched from 8-16 weeks to 50-plus at the peak. Behind the queue is a demand shock: orders for substation-class transformers are up roughly 116% since 2019 and generator step-up demand is up 274%. Prices have followed - up roughly 77% since 2019 by one estimate, and the International Energy Agency puts some transformer prices at up to 2.6 times pre-pandemic levels in real terms. These are survey and agency figures, not audited numbers.
qualifier: industry survey and agency data
vault_ref: Transformer research page / lead times and pricing

section_title: Three makers, sold out
body_text: Exactly three companies manufacture high-bandwidth memory - the stacked DRAM every AI accelerator needs - at scale: SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron. Market-share estimates put SK Hynix at roughly 50-62%, Samsung at 17-35%, and Micron at 11-21%. Roughly 90% of NVIDIA's high-bandwidth memory is estimated to come from SK Hynix alone - one supplier behind most of the flagship AI chips. Industry capacity has been described as sold out through 2026 across all three makers, and no fourth maker has reached scale production. Two of the three are domiciled in South Korea, concentrating a critical layer of the AI supply chain in a single country.
qualifier: market-share estimates from independent research
vault_ref: HBM research page / supplier concentration

section_title: Lending against chips, lending against sheds
body_text: The collateral experiments are the frontier. CoreWeave carries $25.1 billion of total debt including 9.75%-coupon unsecured notes - and the landmark: an $8.5 billion facility rated A3 by Moody's, the first investment-grade GPU-backed financing, non-recourse and secured by a single-purpose subsidiary plus one customer contract. The ~340 basis-point gap between its secured and unsecured borrowing says the quiet part: the A3 rates the contract, not the company - S&P's recovery modeling puts loss-given-default on the unsecured debt at 70-90%. One layer down, Core Scientific ran the inverse experiment: a $3.3 billion project bond at 7.75%, secured not by chips but by the host real estate and 12-year lease cash flows - from a tenant that is 100% of its colocation revenue and whose all-stock offer to acquire it was rejected by shareholders. Two collateral philosophies, one common feature: both securitize a single counterparty's promise. The bondholders are, functionally, underwriting one customer relationship each.
qualifier: from filings, rating actions, and bond documents
vault_ref: Risk-map research page / GPU and host collateral structures

section_title: The sole Western producer
body_text: HALEU - the higher-enrichment uranium fuel most next-generation reactors need - is produced at scale by exactly one licensed Western facility: Centrus Energy's centrifuge cascade in Piketon, Ohio. Its first production phase was completed in June 2025 at roughly 900 kg per year, and more than 1.6 metric tons had been delivered to the US Department of Energy cumulatively through the first quarter of 2026 - up more than 60% in a single quarter. The next phase, a 60-machine cascade targeting about 6 metric tons per year, has its first new cascade expected online in 2029, anchored by a $900 million government task order awarded in January 2026 that could reach $1.07 billion with options. The company's stated full-scale ambition is 12 metric tons per year, with its CEO describing it as currently the only production-ready option for the national security establishment.
qualifier: from filings and the earnings call
vault_ref: HALEU research page / supply position

section_title: The demand gap
body_text: Official projections put HALEU demand near 40 metric tons per year by 2030 and 50 by 2035. Against today's actual production capacity of roughly 900 kg per year, that is a gap of roughly 44x by 2030. Even if the sole producer's full planned build-out to 12 metric tons per year is completed on schedule, the gap only narrows to roughly 3.3x by 2030 - closing it entirely would also require competing Western projects, none of which is licensed or operating yet. These are projections, not deliveries: the demand side depends on next-generation reactors actually being built, and the supply side on scale-ups that are funded but not yet constructed.
qualifier: official projections vs current capacity
vault_ref: HALEU research page / demand-supply gap

section_title: Government-rationed fuel
body_text: In April 2025 the US Department of Energy selected 5 reactor developers from 15 that requested HALEU fuel allocations. Developers receive scarce output through government allocation rather than contracting directly with the producer. A government agency deciding which customers of a fuel commodity get supplied reads as a strong signal of how binding the shortage is: at current production, an ordinary commercial market would not have enough to go around.
qualifier: from the DOE record
vault_ref: HALEU research page / DOE allocation

section_title: The Russia exit deadline
body_text: The Prohibiting Russian Uranium Imports Act became law in May 2024, with a full import ban taking effect January 1, 2028. Russia historically supplied about 24% of US enrichment services, so the ban forces Western enrichment capacity to expand against a hard multi-year deadline. Utilities are already restructuring procurement away from Russian suppliers ahead of the ban, and market trackers report spot enrichment prices roughly doubled between 2024 and 2025. A remaining uncertainty runs the other way: Russia could retaliate with its own export ban before 2028, which would tighten the squeeze further.
qualifier: from the statute and filings; price moves per market trackers
vault_ref: HALEU research page / Russia exit forcing function

section_title: The funded supply response
body_text: In January 2026 Washington awarded $2.7 billion to expand Western enrichment - three task orders of $900 million each, to the incumbent producer and two challengers. One challenger has leased a former government enrichment site in Kentucky for a project of roughly $1.5 billion and secured export-bank letters of interest worth up to $2.4 billion for Japan and $1.8 billion for South Korea over ten years. The honest counterweight: every new entrant remains pre-license and pre-operational, with production targeted for around 2030 or later - so the incumbent's 3-5 year head start is intact for now. What the new money really changes is the shelf life of the words 'sole Western producer,' which now look datable to roughly the end of the decade.
qualifier: verified at DOE and EXIM records
vault_ref: HALEU research page / Western competition status

section_title: Sold out, in the maker's own words
body_text: Micron, the one US-listed maker of high-bandwidth memory, said in mid-2026 that demand is 'far in excess of our ability to supply,' with output booked through 2026 and into 2027-2028. The company has signed 16 strategic customer agreements totaling $22 billion of committed purchases, roughly $18 billion of it already backed by cash - non-cancelable, take-or-pay contracts with price floors. Its quarterly filing says it expects those floor prices to keep gross margins 'well above our peak quarterly margins in any past cycle.' Management also expects market tightness to continue beyond 2027, and projections it cited put the AI-memory market crossing $100 billion in 2027, earlier than prior estimates. Those market-size figures are projections, not deliveries.
qualifier: from the quarterly filing and earnings call
vault_ref: HBM research page / Micron primary confirmation

section_title: The 3-to-1 squeeze
body_text: High-bandwidth memory is made on the same production lines as ordinary DRAM, and it is a hungry tenant: an industry rule of thumb holds that every AI chip consumes the wafer capacity of roughly three normal PC chips. By late 2025, AI memory had taken about 23% of all DRAM wafer capacity. Each generation tightens the squeeze - the newest stacks carry 16 memory dies where the prior generation carried 12, a third more silicon per AI accelerator. The effects show up downstream: press reports say Meta has extended server lifespans because of the memory shortage, and ordinary server-memory prices have tightened across the market. These are estimates and reported figures, not audited numbers.
qualifier: industry rule of thumb plus reported figures
vault_ref: HBM research page / capacity displacement

section_title: A buyer's income statement confirms it
body_text: The memory squeeze is now visible in a buyer's audited numbers, not just in supplier talk. Cisco - a networking-equipment maker that buys memory rather than making it - attributed part of a roughly 2.6-percentage-point gross-margin decline to higher memory costs, calling the pricing 'unprecedented' on its mid-2026 earnings call. Its response sits on the balance sheet: purchase commitments surged to $16.0 billion, from $7.6 billion nine months earlier, as it locked in supply. When a customer that large starts pre-committing at that scale to guarantee components, the shortage has moved from forecast to income statement.
qualifier: from Cisco's quarterly filing and earnings call
vault_ref: HBM research page / demand-side confirmation

section_title: Why demand may not roll over
body_text: A common worry is that AI-memory demand collapses once the training build-out matures. Independent research points the other way: running AI models is bound by memory bandwidth rather than raw compute, and 'reasoning' models that think in long chains multiply the memory each query needs. That would make high-bandwidth memory demand rise with AI usage itself, independent of the training-hardware cycle - one research house estimated demand grew more than 130% in 2025 and projects more than 70% growth in 2026. These are third-party estimates, and the honest counterweight is real: if the next generation ramps smoothly at all three makers, pricing could normalize and this shortage would read as a great cycle rather than a permanent structure.
qualifier: independent research; projections, not deliveries
vault_ref: HBM research page / inference demand floor

section_title: An 80% imported bottleneck
body_text: The United States imports roughly 80% of its power transformers, mostly from Mexico, China, and Thailand, and China alone is estimated to control about 60% of global transformer production capacity. One step upstream it gets narrower still: exactly one US company - Cleveland-Cliffs - produces grain-oriented electrical steel, the specialized steel that transformer cores require, so domestic factory expansion is gated by a single supplier's output. About $2 billion of announced US transformer-capacity expansions only come fully online in 2027-2028, and one industry forecaster projects the transformer shortfall narrowing from roughly 30% in 2025 to about 5% only by 2030. The bottleneck is real, imported, and slow to fix.
qualifier: independent research and trade data
vault_ref: Transformer research page / import dependency and GOES steel

section_title: The makers' order books
body_text: The equipment makers' own filings show the queue. Eaton ended fiscal 2025 with a $19.6 billion total backlog, up 29% year over year - growing much faster than its revenue - and reported a 228-gigawatt datacenter backlog that its CEO called '12 years of backlog at 2025 build rates.' It is spending roughly $1.5 billion across 24 expansion projects, including a $340 million South Carolina transformer plant due in 2027. GE Vernova, meanwhile, paid $5.3 billion in early 2026 for the half of transformer maker Prolec GE it didn't already own, whose backlog stands near $10 billion. When makers are acquiring factories and rivals at this pace, the order book is the message.
qualifier: from the makers' filings and earnings calls
vault_ref: Transformer research page / Eaton and GE Vernova backlogs

section_title: Half the datacenters are waiting on power
body_text: Press and industry reports say roughly half of planned 2026 US datacenter builds have been delayed or cancelled because of power infrastructure shortages; one climate-research tracker counted only about 5 gigawatts under construction out of 12 gigawatts announced for 2026. The subtle part is what those delays mean: they largely reflect an inability to get sites energized - the transformer and grid-connection shortage itself - rather than weak demand for computing. A stalled project does not free up a transformer; it is standing in line for one. That distinction matters for reading the shortage: developer delays are the constraint showing up in someone else's statistics, and these figures are reported estimates, not audited counts.
qualifier: reported figures; not audited counts
vault_ref: Transformer research page / 2026 build delays

section_title: The disruptor that isn't (yet) - and what would end the shortage
body_text: Solid-state transformers - the electronics-based challenger technology - look additive rather than disruptive on current evidence: they cost an estimated 5-10 times more per unit, lack an industry qualification standard, and use different core materials, so they don't even relieve the specialized-steel bottleneck. They win new sockets like high-density datacenter power rather than replacing the iron-core grid base, and the incumbents own both paths while funded startups contest the new one. The honest end-of-shortage test is elsewhere: watch the makers' order intake. If new orders fall below shipments for two-plus quarters as the 2027-2029 factory wave arrives, pricing power is eroding - and distribution-class lead times have already eased even as the largest units stay tight and rising. These are independent-research findings, not filed figures.
qualifier: independent research; projections, not deliveries
vault_ref: Transformer research page / SST shift and pricing falsifier

section_title: Why this chip gates the link
body_text: An optical module cannot simply run faster without smarter signal processing: higher lane speeds mean more loss, more noise, and heavier error correction, and the digital signal processor has to recover the signal while consuming as little power as possible. At 1.6-terabit speeds the DSP becomes both the performance enabler and the power problem - it decides whether 200-gigabit-per-lane links hit their targets inside strict power and thermal budgets. Independent research ranks this chip layer among the highest-conviction bottlenecks in the AI optical supply chain, partly because the semiconductor layer tends to capture more value than the module-assembly businesses around it. That value-capture claim is an analytical judgment, not a filed number.
qualifier: independent research framework
vault_ref: Optical DSP research page / chokepoint mechanics

section_title: Two giants and a challenger, in their own words
body_text: The merchant market for these chips concentrates in three US companies, each claiming an edge. Broadcom's CEO told investors 'we are the only one out there at 1.6T DSP,' pairing its DSP with its dominant switch chips. Marvell claims first-to-market at every generation of the technology and raised its fiscal-2027 interconnect growth outlook to more than 70% year over year. Credo, the challenger, grew revenue 206% to $1.34 billion in fiscal 2026 by building on older, cheaper chip-manufacturing nodes - claiming similar performance at lower cost - and guided roughly $600 million of optical growth for fiscal 2027. All three of those competitive claims are the companies' own words from earnings calls; they cannot all be the only leader.
qualifier: from earnings calls; competitive claims are the companies' own
vault_ref: Optical DSP research page / competitive positioning

section_title: The design-me-out question
body_text: The existential question for this chip is co-packaged optics - moving the optical parts into the processor package, which threatens the pluggable module the DSP lives in today. The three makers are betting three different ways. Broadcom publicly dismisses the shift as 'bright, shiny objects' while simultaneously claiming to lead it, and advocates copper links where possible. Marvell spent up to $5.5 billion (including earn-outs) buying Celestial AI to become a direct co-packaged-optics competitor, targeting $500 million of annualized revenue by fiscal 2028 and $1 billion by fiscal 2029 - management targets, not booked revenue. Credo claims its microLED-based approach will fix co-packaged optics' reliability pitfalls, then bought a silicon-photonics maker to participate in the architecture anyway. Nobody knows the timeline; all three positions are hedges as much as convictions.
qualifier: company statements plus independent analysis
vault_ref: Optical DSP research page / CPO displacement strategies

section_title: When the customer becomes an investor
body_text: In early 2026, NVIDIA invested $2.0 billion in Marvell through convertible preferred stock, disclosed in Marvell's quarterly filing, alongside an expanded silicon-photonics collaboration for scale-up networking. For years the two companies followed an industry convention of not naming each other in public materials; that pattern is now broken - Marvell names NVIDIA throughout its recent disclosures. A dominant chip designer taking an equity position in its interconnect supplier reads as a signal of how strategic the optical-signal-processing layer has become: the buyer is paying to secure the roadmap, not just the parts. The same partner remains, simultaneously, a potential competitor in some product lines - both things are true at once.
qualifier: from the quarterly filing
vault_ref: Optical DSP research page / NVIDIA investment

section_title: What it is, and why it's hard
body_text: Co-packaged optics moves the optical links of an AI datacenter from replaceable front-panel modules into the switch or compute package itself. The prize is power: a conventional 800G pluggable module runs roughly 17.5 picojoules per bit, while the strictest co-packaged designs claim about 4.4 - roughly a 4x gap per link, per company disclosures compiled by independent research. The price is six harder integration problems: heat (lasers sit next to hot processors and degrade), laser reliability (a failure can no longer be swapped from the front panel), sub-micron optical alignment, serviceability, package yield (one dead laser can destroy the whole package's value), and field repair. One architectural workaround - keeping the lasers outside the package as external light sources - relieves the heat and serviceability problems, and happens to preserve the laser makers' position in the value chain.
qualifier: independent research; power figures per company disclosures
vault_ref: CPO research page / integration mechanics

section_title: Announcements versus deployments
body_text: Independent research calls definition confusion the single most common analyst error on this topic: 'adoption is already happening' depends entirely on which architecture is being counted. Under the strictest definition - optical engines sharing the package substrate with the switch chip - the genuine production deployment to date is one hyperscaler's characterization cohort of 15 switches, not a hundred-thousand-port fleet. NVIDIA's much-publicized version is a hybrid design with detachable optical sub-assemblies and external lasers, closer to strict co-packaging than the alternatives but not the same thing, with availability cited for the second half of 2026. Meanwhile intermediate approaches - linear-drive pluggables that simply remove the signal-processing chip - offer part of the power savings without the integration risk. The transition is real; the tense of the verb is the argument.
qualifier: independent analysis; deployment counts as reported
vault_ref: CPO research page / adoption reality check

section_title: The $4 billion vote of confidence
body_text: On March 2, 2026, NVIDIA invested $2 billion each into Lumentum and Coherent - the two big makers of the high-power lasers that external-light-source optical architectures need - per the companies' SEC filings. Coherent's deal came with more: a multibillion-dollar, multi-year supply agreement for co-packaged-optics components extending through the end of the decade, which its CEO called an expansion of a more-than-20-year relationship. Lumentum's $2 billion cash injection is funding a fifth indium-phosphide laser fab in North Carolina. When the dominant AI-chip designer pays billions to secure the laser supply chain years ahead of volume deployment, it is a statement about how seriously it takes the optical transition - and about how scarce it expects those lasers to be.
qualifier: from SEC filings and earnings calls
vault_ref: CPO research page / NVIDIA laser investments

section_title: Three giants, three bets
body_text: The executives are betting against each other in public. Marvell's CEO splits the market in two: co-packaged optics for connecting racks together ('scale-out') stays 'relatively limited,' while inside-the-rack ('scale-up') is 'inflecting in a pretty big way' - and Marvell set itself targets of $500 million annualized co-packaged revenue by fiscal 2028 and $1 billion by fiscal 2029, management targets rather than booked revenue. NVIDIA's CEO says 'copper scale-up or optical scale-up? We're gonna do both.' Broadcom's CEO dismisses the shift as 'bright, shiny objects' and argues copper is best where possible - while simultaneously claiming 'we are the lead' in the technology he is dismissing. All quotes are from earnings calls and keynotes; at most one of these framings ages well.
qualifier: the executives' own words from calls and keynotes
vault_ref: CPO research page / platform positioning

section_title: The value is not in the board
body_text: AI accelerator trays and switch line cards need advanced circuit boards - 22-plus layers, some over 100 - and the demand is real. But independent research finds the durable value migrating upstream of the board, into materials with far fewer makers. Ajinomoto - yes, the seasonings company - holds over 95% of ABF build-up film, the insulating layer in every advanced chip substrate, made in just two Japanese plants; its planned capacity growth of roughly 7% a year trails AI-substrate demand estimates compounding at 16-33%. Nittobo holds about 90% of the specialty T-glass cloth that low-loss laminates need, and Resonac reportedly over 90% of a key high-grade copper-foil material. The board makers, by contrast, face five or six credible rivals each. These shares are industry estimates, not audited figures - but the pattern is consistent across sources: the fewer the makers, the deeper in the stack they sit.
qualifier: industry estimates from independent research
vault_ref: PCB research page / value migration verdict

section_title: The 45% that wasn't in the filing
body_text: An activist presentation claimed one Chinese board maker, WUS Kunshan, supplies roughly 45% of NVIDIA's switch boards. That number is not in the company's own Hong Kong listing filing. What the filing actually supports: #1 in datacenter boards at 10.3% share, #1 in 22-layer-and-above boards at 25.3%, and #1 in switch and router boards at 12.5% - a genuine leader, not a monopoly. The competitive tail is also closing fast: rival Victory Giant's AI and high-performance-computing board share jumped from 1.7% in 2024 to 13.8% in the first half of 2025 as a core supplier of Google's AI-chip boards. The board layer rewards operational excellence, but excellence with five capable rivals is a different asset from a materials monopoly - and the gap between a pitch-deck number and a filed one is worth reading twice.
qualifier: verified against the exchange listing filing
vault_ref: PCB research page / WUS claim verification

section_title: The buyer reaches past the board
body_text: The clearest signal of where scarcity actually binds is where the biggest buyer spends its defensive effort. Industry reports say NVIDIA and other major customers bypassed the laminate makers to contract directly with the glass-cloth and high-grade copper-foil suppliers, reserving capacity more than a year in advance - while laminate suppliers put the board makers on quota allocation, with lead times reaching about six months by early 2026. The direct-contracting mechanic is reported rather than filed, but the primary-source echoes are there: NVIDIA's annual filing confirms prepaid manufacturing and capacity agreements in general terms, and Broadcom's CEO said supply is 'fully secured' for 2026 through 2028, explicitly including 'substrates and T-glass.' Buyers defend the node they fear losing - and they are defending the materials, not the boards.
qualifier: reported mechanics; filings confirm the general pattern
vault_ref: PCB research page / buyer-defense signal

section_title: The substrate leader's own numbers
body_text: Ibiden, the lead maker of the package substrates directly under AI processor dies, put primary numbers behind the substrate tier's durability in its fiscal-year results (year ended March 2026). Its own disclosure places its leading-edge AI-server substrate share at roughly 70-80% - near-100% at each new product generation, then decaying 20-30% over three to six months as rivals qualify. Its electronics operating margin rose from 13.6% to 18.6% and is guided toward roughly 22.7%. Most telling is the funding model: customers bear part of the investment cost of new capacity, paid in advance - buyers pre-funding a supplier's factories is about as strong a scarcity signal as filings offer. One caveat the disclosure leaves open: the company describes only logic-chip substrates; a claimed second role in memory-stack substrates is not confirmed in its own materials.
qualifier: from the company's filed results
vault_ref: PCB research page / Ibiden primary confirmation

section_title: The honest counterweight: brutal cycles, split geography
body_text: Circuit boards are historically a brutal cyclical business - the global market fell about 15% in 2023 before recovering - and the 2017-18 copper-foil spike-and-bust is the cautionary template for today's tightness. What looks structurally different this cycle is a genuine per-generation material gate: each new speed grade of laminate cuts signal loss by an order of magnitude versus commodity material, and suppliers must re-qualify every generation, with share swinging violently between the few who pass. Geography splits the risk: board fabrication is China-heavy (roughly half of global board value), carrying tariff and export-control exposure, while the durable material nodes - the build-up film and glass cloth - are concentrated in Japan, which makes them hard to replicate but also a single-geography point of failure. If AI spending pauses, the board makers crack first; the materials crack last.
qualifier: independent research; cyclical history as context
vault_ref: PCB research page / cyclicality and geography

section_title: The job: keeping the chip alive
body_text: Multilayer ceramic capacitors are the passive stabilizers in an AI server's power-delivery network. A GPU's load swings cause near-instant current spikes, the voltage regulator is electrically far away, and chip-adjacent capacitors bridge the microsecond gap - without them the chip suffers voltage droop, downclocks, or crashes. Samsung Electro-Mechanics' own materials say an AI server uses more than 10 times the capacitors of a general-purpose server. The hard part is that AI-grade parts must be high-capacitance, small, low-inductance, voltage-tolerant, and highly reliable all at once - built from ultra-fine ceramic powder stacked in layers around two microns thick, up to a thousand layers, sintered without cracking. The manufacturing is the moat; the physics is unforgiving.
qualifier: from the makers' product materials
vault_ref: MLCC research page / decoupling function

section_title: The leader's own numbers
body_text: Murata, the #1 maker, put primary numbers under the AI-capacitor story in its April 2026 full-year results: server-capacitor growth guided at roughly 85-90% (revised up from about 30%), and - for the first time - a broken-out data-center revenue line, up 74% in the year just ended and guided up 84%. It also announced roughly 80 billion yen of emergency capacity spending for server capacitors. The #3 maker, Taiyo Yuden, independently confirmed the market structure in its results Q&A: customers multi-source, but from a limited set of qualified suppliers - an oligopoly held together by qualification, not contracts. One asymmetry worth noting: the leader discloses its data-center split; the #3 does not - the cleanest evidence comes from the top of the oligopoly.
qualifier: from the makers' earnings materials
vault_ref: MLCC research page / Murata and Taiyo primary confirmation

section_title: The famous price hike that wasn't
body_text: The loudest number in the MLCC boom story - a rumored 15-35% price increase on ceramic capacitors from the market leader - does not survive contact with the makers' own disclosures. Murata's confirmed April action covered non-capacitor products (inductors and ferrite parts, driven by silver costs); on capacitors, its president said plainly 'we will not adopt a policy of simply raising prices,' and its own profit bridge assumes capacitor prices keep falling, with the revenue gain coming from mix and volume. Taiyo Yuden independently said 'there are no signs of industry-wide price increases yet.' Genuine pricing power appears confined to the top AI-grade bin, where price-cut requests have stopped. A boom where the makers themselves talk down the price story is a very specific kind of boom - worth describing exactly.
qualifier: debunked against both makers' own disclosures
vault_ref: MLCC research page / price-hike verification

section_title: 'The new memory,' tested
body_text: A major investment bank dubbed MLCCs 'the new memory' - the third-largest AI-server component bill after the GPU and memory, with AI demand projected to grow about 4.3x by 2030 against industry capacity growing roughly 10% a year. The analogy holds in places: the AI-grade bin is reportedly a near-duopoly, the powder-and-yield process moat is real (ultra-high-capacitance parts reportedly run around 40% yields, which is why AI servers consume 2-3% of units but about 10% of capacity), and parts get designed in through costly qualification. But it breaks in the ways that matter for durability: MLCCs are under 0.1% of a rack's cost versus memory's roughly 25%, so buyers have little incentive to defend the oligopoly; new capacity is kilns and presses answering in 12-24 months, not 3-plus years; and a second tier including Chinese makers is already producing AI-grade parts for domestic servers. The honest verdict: a real bottleneck only at the top chip-adjacent bin - the broad market stays quality-but-cyclical. All segment figures are analyst estimates, not disclosures.
qualifier: analyst estimates; the 'new memory' label is a bank's framing
vault_ref: MLCC research page / HBM comparison

section_title: The 2018 rhyme
body_text: The cautionary template is recent and well-documented: standard MLCC spot prices rose 5-10x in the 2017-2018 super-cycle, then the unwind was severe - one major maker's revenue fell 34.5% in 2019 as hoarded channel inventory cleared. Several elements of that cycle are present now: industry trackers documented distributors double-booking consumer-grade parts in mid-2026, with actual equipment-maker orders declining even as channel orders surged - meaning part of today's 'shortage' is hoarding, not consumption. Meanwhile the supply response is funded and dated: the leader's new building came online in April 2026, a third Philippines plant arrives in 2027, and multiple makers are expanding - a wave that lands in 2027-28 just as consensus expects AI-server spending growth to decelerate sharply. If unit growth disappoints, 2028 could look like 2019. These are tracker reports and consensus estimates, not certainties - but the rhyme is worth hearing.
qualifier: industry tracker data and consensus estimates
vault_ref: MLCC research page / cycle bear case

section_title: The queue that created me
body_text: Behind-the-meter generation exists because the grid cannot keep up. Press and industry reports put roughly half of planned 2026 US datacenter builds delayed or cancelled over power infrastructure shortages; large transformers run multi-year lead times, grid-interconnection queues stretch years in the busiest markets, and independent research estimates a gap of roughly 50 gigawatts between the datacenter capacity hyperscalers have committed to and what the grid can actually connect through 2028. The workaround inverts the usual economics: operators accept a higher levelized cost of electricity in exchange for time-to-energization - paying more per kilowatt-hour to have power in months instead of years. These delay and gap figures are reported estimates, not audited counts, but the direction is consistent across sources.
qualifier: reported figures and independent research
vault_ref: BTM research page / grid constraint context

section_title: Four technologies, one race
body_text: Four technology bases compete to power datacenters on-site, and the differentiator is speed. Solid-oxide fuel cells deploy in roughly 90 days from manufacturing release per independent research - the fastest path - in modular megawatt-scale units that can run as an islanded microgrid. Reciprocating engines and smaller aero-derivative gas turbines take 12-24 months and carry rich long-term service revenue. Molten-carbonate fuel cells offer carbon-capture co-products in standardized 12.5-megawatt blocks, though at roughly one twenty-fifth the commercial scale of the fuel-cell leader. Heavy utility-scale gas turbines bring the biggest capacity per unit but lead times of 4-7 years - so long they largely miss the window this workaround exists to exploit. The trade-offs are real: the fastest option costs more per megawatt; the cheapest options take longer; none of them is the grid.
qualifier: lead-time estimates from independent research
vault_ref: BTM research page / technology-base comparison

section_title: The fastest horse, in its own words
body_text: Bloom Energy, the solid-oxide fuel-cell maker with the fastest reported deployment time, framed the market on its April 2026 earnings call in unusually absolute terms: 'Time to power has gone from a procurement consideration to an existential necessity. The companies driving the AI transformation are racing against each other on the one hand, and on the other, bumping into the bottlenecks common to building conventional infrastructure, such as permissions, permits, and community acceptance.' The numbers behind the rhetoric: first-quarter 2026 revenue of $751 million, full-year guidance raised to roughly $3.6 billion at the midpoint, and manufacturing capacity of about 1 gigawatt being doubled to 2 gigawatts by the end of 2026. It has also disclosed a deal with Oracle, made public through a joint announcement and a warrant filing. The honest caveat: fuel cells remain unproven at multi-gigawatt single-site scale - the largest commercial deployments run in the hundred-megawatt range.
qualifier: from the earnings call and filings
vault_ref: BTM research page / Bloom Energy positioning

section_title: The industrial heavyweight's order book
body_text: Caterpillar - treated by many as a construction-and-mining cyclical - is quietly one of the biggest winners of the on-site-power race, and its filings show it. In April 2026 it announced a 2.1-gigawatt prime power agreement, its sixth agreement at 1 gigawatt or larger, with deliveries running well into 2028. Its large reciprocating-engine backlog has grown more than 3.5x since January 2024; it is expanding capacity to roughly 3x 2024 levels, mostly through 2027-2029; and it raised its 2030 power-generation sales target to more than 3x the 2024 baseline. Its CEO framed the edge plainly: stringing together on-site power needs multiple products, 'us having turbines and recips is an advantage for us. We can configure it, one way or the other or a mix.' A superlative worth flagging: the claim that its Solar Turbines unit is the world's largest industrial gas-turbine maker circulates in research but is not stated in the company's own annual filing.
qualifier: from filings and the earnings call
vault_ref: BTM research page / Caterpillar positioning

section_title: The honest counterweights
body_text: Two disciplined caveats keep this story honest. First, GE Vernova's CEO - whose company sells the biggest turbines - says the machines are not the real constraint: 'the gas turbines are really not the gating item when you're talking about a three-year cycle from when a project starts, the EPC build-out, the permitting, the fuel availability.' The bottleneck is broader than equipment, and about 20% of its 100-gigawatt turbine backlog is datacenter-attributed without disclosing how much is truly on-site versus grid-feed. Second, the demand side is still mostly promises: datacenter host Core Scientific is pursuing behind-the-meter gas - a pipeline to its Texas campus and roughly 440 megawatts via an Oklahoma acquisition, citing 12-14-month lead times versus years for grid interconnection - but none of it is operational yet, and the company hasn't decided whether to own the generation or take the power under contract. The workaround is real; much of it is still on order.
qualifier: from earnings calls; forward plans flagged as not yet operational
vault_ref: BTM research page / honest-verdict counterweights

section_title: Two suppliers or three - either way, none in the West
body_text: Indium phosphide is the foundational wafer material for datacenter optics: the lasers that carry data between AI chips are grown on it. Global substrate production concentrates in a handful of companies, and even the count is disputed within the lead supplier's own materials: AXT's annual filing says 'there are three primary suppliers of InP substrates worldwide' - AXT, Sumitomo Electric, and JX Nippon - while its CEO told investors 'today there are two InP substrate suppliers in the world, and we're one of them.' The filing complies with disclosure obligations; the call tells the more dominant story. Whether two or three, the structural fact is the same: every commercially significant maker sits in China or Japan, and no InP substrate manufacturing exists in the United States or Europe.
qualifier: from the filing and earnings call; the two-vs-three gap noted
vault_ref: InP research page / supply concentration

section_title: A backlog that outruns the expansion
body_text: AXT's early-2026 results show the substrate constraint converting into numbers. Its InP backlog grew from $60 million-plus to over $100 million - a record - with InP now more than half of company revenue and the next quarter guided to be its largest-ever for the material. The capacity response is funded and escalating: from roughly $8 million of InP revenue per quarter, the company targets ~$35 million per quarter by end-2026 and ~$65-70 million by early 2028, backed by a $632.5 million capital raise in April 2026, with a further plant under planning - possibly the first outside China. The line worth remembering: management says demand 'is 10x' even the planned doubling. If that holds, the constraint survives its own expansion. It is a management claim about future demand, not a booked order number.
qualifier: from the earnings call and filings; the 10x line is a management claim
vault_ref: InP research page / AXT capacity trajectory

section_title: The permit gate
body_text: All of the lead supplier's production sits in China, which means the wafer under America's AI datacenters passes through a Chinese export-control gate. Since early 2025, InP substrate exports have required permits; per the company's 2026 disclosures, permits for most non-US destinations continue to flow readily, while US-destined permits remain pending - China's Ministry of Commerce has requested additional data on the US applications. Meanwhile, in-China shipments require no permit at all, and the company says its China laser-market revenue more than doubled quarter-over-quarter, with China estimated at roughly 30% of the InP demand it sees, rising toward 40% by year-end as China builds its own optical supply chain. The asymmetry is the story: the constrained channel is the American one.
qualifier: from the company's disclosures
vault_ref: InP research page / export-control gate

section_title: The wafer-size race
body_text: A substrate alone is not a laser: after the wafer exists, epitaxial layers must be grown on it with near-perfect quality, and that processing step is its own bottleneck - poor epitaxy destroys yield even when wafers are plentiful. The economics turn on wafer size. Coherent, which processes InP on six-inch wafers at sites in Texas, Sweden, and Switzerland while most of the industry runs the three-inch standard, told investors the six-inch process yields 'more than 4x chips at less than 50% cost' versus three-inch - roughly an 8x improvement in cost per chip. It quadrupled InP wafer starts in a single quarter and plans to double total capacity by the end of 2026. The moat is not owning wafers; it is the accumulated process knowledge to run big ones without killing the yield.
qualifier: from the earnings call and filings
vault_ref: InP research page / epitaxial tier and wafer size

section_title: Two rivals, one story
body_text: In the same reporting quarter (February 2026), the two leading datacenter-laser makers independently described the same shortage. Lumentum: its indium-phosphide wafer fab is 'fully allocated,' it is under-shipping customer demand by roughly 25-30%, and all of its EML laser capacity is spoken for through calendar 2027 under long-term agreements. Coherent: datacenter book-to-bill above 4x, capacity doubling by end of 2026 - yet its CEO said demand absorbs all new capacity 'and then some,' and 'I don't foresee the supply-demand getting back in balance this calendar year. I don't think it happens next calendar year... we could be in a very sustained, long period of supply-demand imbalance on indium phosphide.' An analyst cross-checked directly on the call - with a competitor adding 40% capacity, is the industry still short? The answer was yes. When competitors corroborate each other's scarcity story under questioning, it stops being marketing.
qualifier: cross-validated across both makers' earnings calls
vault_ref: Laser research page / supplier cross-validation

section_title: Four bottlenecks wearing one name
body_text: 'Laser supply' is really four progressively narrower constraints. First, the indium-phosphide substrate and epitaxial capacity every device starts from. Second - narrower - the ability to fabricate qualified ultra-high-power lasers at 400-milliwatt class output with strict noise, linewidth, and thermal requirements: Lumentum's CEO calls that 'not something that many people can do.' Third, packaging those devices into external light-source modules that feed multiple optical engines from a shared source - roughly 2-2.5x the content value of standalone lasers, per Lumentum, but harder to qualify. Fourth, integrating optics into the same package as a hot switch or compute chip, with sub-micron alignment tolerances. Today's confirmed shortage sits at the first two layers; the analytical warning is that as wafer capacity doubles, the effective constraint may simply migrate downstream to packaging and integration. A supply chain is only as loose as its tightest remaining stage.
qualifier: from earnings calls; layer decomposition per independent research
vault_ref: Laser research page / decomposed constraint layers

section_title: The customers confirm it
body_text: The shortage story would be weaker if only the sellers told it. The buyers tell it too. Applied Optoelectronics' CEO described a 'huge issue of laser shortage' - outside suppliers quoting waits of 'at least one year or even longer' - and responded by tripling the company's own in-house laser production by mid-2027, projecting that more than 95% of its laser output will be AI lasers by year-end. Fabrinet, the contract manufacturer that assembles leading-edge optical modules, said plainly: demand outstrips supply and 'we could ship more if we had more components,' identifying the EML laser as the main cause and noting its customer had just approved a second laser source. Supplier, customer, and assembler each describe the same constraint from their own side of it - about as close to triangulated as supply-chain evidence gets.
qualifier: from the customers' and assembler's earnings calls
vault_ref: Laser research page / integrator-side confirmation

section_title: Follow the $4 billion
body_text: On March 2, 2026, NVIDIA invested $2 billion in each of the two laser makers - $4 billion total - one month after both had disclosed customer investment negotiations on their earnings calls. Coherent's deal includes a multibillion-dollar, multi-year co-packaged-optics supply agreement extending through the end of the decade. The suppliers were already spending like the shortage is real: Lumentum's capital spending roughly two-and-a-half-times'd in three years to an annualized ~$320 million, consuming nearly all its operating cash flow; Coherent's networking capital spending tripled year-over-year, with inventory up 29% in six months as it builds ahead of demand. Then at its March 2026 conference, NVIDIA's CEO disclosed co-packaged-optics switches in production and said the company needs 'a lot more capacity for CPO.' Investment, capacity spending, and production status all point the same direction; none of it guarantees the demand persists.
qualifier: from SEC filings, earnings calls, and the GTC keynote
vault_ref: Laser research page / NVIDIA strategic investment

section_title: Physics made me mandatory
body_text: Industry surveys put the ceiling for air cooling at roughly 20-35 kilowatts per rack. AI racks blew through it: 2023's flagship GPU racks ran 30-40 kW on air; the current liquid-mandatory generation runs 120-155 kW; the generation shipping from late 2026 is specified at roughly 180-250 kW with no air-cooled version offered at all; and the 2027 roadmap rack is around 600 kW, with 'multi-megawatt per rack' promised beyond that. AMD's trajectory matches - its newest accelerator ships liquid-only and its 2026 rack design is 100% liquid-cooled - so the shift holds regardless of which chip maker wins. And the training-to-inference shift deepens it: research puts dense inference racks near 370 kW, roughly three times training density. These generation-by-generation figures compile vendor specifications and independent research, not one audited source.
qualifier: vendor specifications compiled by independent research
vault_ref: Liquid cooling research page / rack density trajectory

section_title: The order books, on the record
body_text: The cooling demand shock is now in the suppliers' own filings, across four companies at once. Vertiv's backlog more than doubled year-over-year to $15.0 billion. Modine's data-center product group grew 78% year-over-year, with its filing attributing the growth to 'significant data center growth fueled by artificial intelligence.' nVent's organic orders rose 40%, primarily AI-datacenter-driven, and its annual filing names artificial intelligence explicitly as a demand driver. Eaton reported data-center orders up 240% in its Americas electrical segment. Four independent filers, one quarter-adjacent window, the same attributed cause - about as clean as cross-company primary evidence gets. The counterweight to carry: order books this steep are also the raw material of double-ordering, and none of these companies breaks out how much of the surge is safety-stock.
qualifier: from the suppliers' filings and earnings calls
vault_ref: Liquid cooling research page / primary demand confirmation

section_title: The leak jokes are dated
body_text: The oldest objection to liquid cooling - water near servers - has a two-decade counterexample: Google has operated more than 2,000 liquid-cooled TPU pods at gigawatt scale with roughly 99.999% uptime since 2018, per operational disclosures cited in independent research. The efficiency case now has peer-reviewed backing too: a 2025 Microsoft life-cycle study in Nature found cold plates and immersion cooling cut greenhouse-gas emissions 15-21%, energy demand 15-20%, and water consumption 31-52% versus air-cooled baselines. NVIDIA's CEO went further at CES 2026, claiming the new generation needs 'no water chillers' at all - racks cooled with 45-degree-Celsius supply water, eliminating mechanical chillers in most climates. That last claim is a keynote promise about an unshipped product; the Google reliability record and the Nature study are the load-bearing evidence.
qualifier: operational disclosures and a peer-reviewed study; keynote claims flagged
vault_ref: Liquid cooling research page / reliability and efficiency evidence

section_title: Where my own supply chain pinches
body_text: Inside the cooling loop, the constraints are uneven. Cold plates - the precision copper micro-channel blocks bolted onto each chip - are the tightest node, at 6-12 month lead times, with industry research calling their manufacturing 'the new TSMC-like bottleneck.' Quick-disconnect couplings that let racks be serviced without draining the loop are effectively a two-supplier duopoly for spec-compliant parts. Coolant distribution units look crowded - roughly 40 vendors tracked - but the top five take over 70% of revenue, and a consolidation wave is running: Schneider bought Motivair, Vertiv bought CoolTera and two more, Eaton bought Boyd. Meanwhile immersion cooling fluids face a severe regulatory overhang after 3M exited its flagship fluid line over PFAS concerns. The pattern rhymes with the rest of AI hardware: the visible product is competitive; the precision sub-component underneath is where scarcity lives.
qualifier: industry research and reported M&A
vault_ref: Liquid cooling research page / sub-component bottleneck map

section_title: Certain demand, contested capture
body_text: The honest structural read: this bottleneck is not an oligopoly. Unlike AI memory (three makers) or datacenter lasers (two leaders), liquid cooling has at least five competing technology approaches - direct-to-chip cold plates, rear-door heat exchangers, immersion, hybrid air-liquid, and free-cooling designs - and ten-plus credible suppliers, with no single-source dominance and no scarce raw material gating production. Direct-to-chip cold plates remove only 70-75% of rack heat, so multiple approaches co-deploy by design rather than one winner taking all. Market researchers size direct liquid cooling at $8 billion-plus by 2030 inside an $80 billion-plus datacenter physical-infrastructure market - meaningful, but smaller than the headlines suggest. Demand is as certain as thermodynamics; which suppliers capture durable value is genuinely open. That distinction - mandatory technology versus defensible position - is the whole analysis.
qualifier: independent research; market sizing is a forecast
vault_ref: Liquid cooling research page / competitive structure verdict

section_title: One country, end to end
body_text: High-performance neodymium-iron-boron magnets - and the heavy rare earths, dysprosium and terbium, they need to hold magnetism at the temperatures inside drone, missile, and motor applications - run through a supply chain one country controls end to end. Agency estimates put China at roughly 85-90% of finished magnets, about 91% of separation and refining, roughly 69% of mining, and essentially 100% of the production equipment. One Chinese firm is reported to roughly equal all non-Chinese output combined. The subtlety worth keeping: the binding constraint is not ore - mining is the least concentrated step - but the midstream separation chemistry and finished-magnet manufacturing, which take years to replicate regardless of what any statute says. These concentration figures are agency estimates relayed through research, not audited disclosures.
qualifier: agency estimates via independent research
vault_ref: Magnet research page / China concentration

section_title: The Pentagon writes a price floor
body_text: In July 2025 the US Department of War took the most direct government action on any AI-or-defense supply bottleneck to date: a partnership with MP Materials, the only scaled US rare-earth producer, comprising $400 million of convertible preferred stock, a 10-year price floor on the key magnet input, a 10-year magnet purchase commitment, and a project loan - all under Defense Production Act authority, per the company's SEC filings. MP mines and refines in California, shipped its first commercial magnets from its Texas facility in December 2025, is building a larger plant targeted for 2028, and is commissioning heavy-rare-earth separation - the most China-controlled step of all. The scale of the gap it is building into: the Pentagon's stated need is estimated at 3,000-4,000 tons of specialized magnets a year, rising toward 10,000 by 2030, against minimal current US output. Those tonnage figures are research estimates; the deal terms are filed.
qualifier: deal terms from SEC filings; tonnage figures are estimates
vault_ref: Magnet research page / MP government partnership

section_title: The consumers say it in their filings
body_text: The dependence is not inferred - the buyers disclose it. AeroVironment's annual filing states its 'motors, batteries, and other advanced components rely on rare earth metals, of which a significant majority are sourced from China,' and that China placed the company on its export-control list in March 2025. Teledyne's filings disclose that China 'has also restricted the export of certain rare earth minerals and permanent magnets that are used in our products, which has in the past delayed and could in the future limit' its sales - and add germanium and gallium, two more China-controlled materials, to the list affecting its imaging and defense products. A discipline note: another defense maker whose drones structurally use the same magnets discloses no such dependence in its filings - so that link stays an inference, not a claim. Disclosed is disclosed; inferred is inferred; the two are not the same grade of fact.
qualifier: from the consumers' annual filings
vault_ref: Magnet research page / disclosed consumer dependence

section_title: Five demand stories, one bottleneck
body_text: The same concentration constrains five distinct demand stories at once: defense drones and missiles (motors, actuators, seekers), AI-datacenter cooling (the fan and coolant-pump motors), offshore wind turbines, EV traction motors, and - the furthest out - humanoid robots, where every joint runs on these magnets and a single robot carries an estimated 0.9-4 kilograms, roughly twice an EV's content. One forecaster projects robotics becomes the largest magnet demand source by around 2040, though today's humanoid volumes are in the low thousands industry-wide. The honest scoping cuts the other way too: drones are under 3% of global magnet demand, so their exposure is about criticality - drone motors skew toward the most China-controlled heavy rare earths - not tonnage. No single demand story justifies the bottleneck's importance; the stack of five does. All demand projections here are third-party estimates.
qualifier: third-party demand estimates
vault_ref: Magnet research page / multi-thesis demand map

section_title: The mirror: one side's vulnerability is the other's moat
body_text: The concentration reads differently depending on where a company sits. US and allied consumers disclose delays, export-list placements, and sourcing risk. China-domiciled motor makers - the firms building permanent-magnet actuators for humanoid robots and EV systems inside China - sit on the protected side of the same bottleneck, with privileged domestic access to the material their Western competitors must import under permit. Tellingly, those companies disclose no rare-earth dependence as a risk factor at all, consistent with having none worth disclosing. The same 91% separation concentration is simultaneously an American vulnerability and a Chinese industrial advantage - one fact, two balance sheets. Any framing of this bottleneck that only tells the vulnerability half is telling half the story.
qualifier: structural observation; the secure-side link is inferred, not disclosed
vault_ref: Magnet research page / geopolitical mirror

section_title: The bench every AI chip crosses
body_text: CoWoS - chip-on-wafer-on-substrate - is TSMC's advanced packaging platform, the step where leading-edge logic dies are integrated with stacked high-bandwidth memory into one package. It is the primary packaging technology for AI accelerators, and it is a bottleneck today, not a forecast: TSMC described advanced-packaging capacity as 'very tight' on its early-2026 call, expects constraints to persist through at least 2027 in parallel with wafer tightness, and says it is pulling construction and equipment schedules forward to speed expansion. The annual filing gives scale without a product breakout: packaging, testing, and related services were 14% of revenue - roughly NT$537 billion - across seven advanced backend fabs, with customers holding about NT$190 billion of advance capacity-reservation payments on TSMC's balance sheet. One quiet detail: even the largest packaging relationships are classified as ordinary-course contracts - no single customer agreement rises to formal materiality.
qualifier: from the annual filing and earnings call
vault_ref: CoWoS research page / capacity status

section_title: The customers' filings show the queue
body_text: The dependence shows up in the customers' own disclosures, from three angles. Broadcom's annual filing states approximately 95% of its contract-manufactured wafers come from TSMC, and its executives say supply - leading-edge wafers, memory, substrates - is 'fully secured' for 2026 through 2028, 'probably the first one to secure that up to 2028 or beyond.' NVIDIA disclosed a total supply commitment of $145 billion across inventory, purchase commitments, and prepayments, with commitments extended further out than usual; its CEO explains the architecture pressure - the company splits chips 'only when we absolutely have no choice,' so each generation uses maximum-size dies that demand ever-larger packages, with nearly 9 gigawatts of the current generation already deployed. And Marvell's Taiwan-shipped revenue grew from $162 million (3% of total) to $1.66 billion (20%) in two years - custom AI chips physically shipping to Taiwan for packaging - with its operations chief naming 'advanced packaging' among its binding constraints. Three customers, three kinds of evidence, one queue.
qualifier: from the customers' filings and earnings calls
vault_ref: CoWoS research page / customer-side dependence

section_title: Watch the toolmakers to see me coming
body_text: Packaging capacity is preceded by packaging equipment, so the toolmakers are the leading indicator. Aehr Test Systems - whose burn-in systems screen dies before they enter a multi-die package - saw bookings surge 6x quarter-over-quarter to $37.2 million and effective backlog hit a company record of $50.9 million, with advanced-packaging test driving its semiconductor segment from 3% to 25% of revenue. The economics explain the urgency: one defective die inside a completed multi-die package destroys the whole package's value, so known-good-die testing gets more valuable as packages get bigger. Onto Innovation, the metrology supplier whose largest customer is TSMC at 21% of revenue, guided advanced-packaging revenue growth to 50%. Tool orders precede wafer starts; both signals point to the packaging constraint being invested against hard - which is also the honest caveat, because capacity that is being built eventually arrives.
qualifier: from the equipment makers' filings and calls
vault_ref: CoWoS research page / equipment-tier leading indicators

section_title: Named on stage, unnamed in filings
body_text: A disclosure quirk worth savoring: NVIDIA's earnings calls do not name TSMC - sixteen pages of a recent call discuss a product generation whose production depends directly on TSMC packaging without saying the word. But at its March 2026 developer conference, NVIDIA's CEO named the partner freely: 'We invented the process technology with TSMC. We're the only one in production with it today,' describing the co-developed optical packaging process. The pattern is venue-specific - keynotes and product launches are where the partnership gets named; earnings calls preserve supply-chain silence. For readers of disclosures, the lesson is practical: what a company says depends on the room it says it in, and silence in one venue does not mean the relationship - or the dependence - isn't there.
qualifier: from the earnings call and keynote records
vault_ref: CoWoS research page / venue-specific naming pattern

section_title: Boring but decisive
body_text: Optical packaging is not ordinary electronics assembly. A 1.6-terabit module or co-packaged optical engine requires laser dies placed with sub-micron accuracy, lenses aligned to the laser output, optical fiber attached with minimal signal loss, wavelength-multiplexing components integrated, hermetic sealing, thermal management, and final calibration across a battery of signal-quality tests. Tiny misalignments create insertion loss, degraded signals, and field-reliability failures - so even when every component works individually, the finished assembly can fail. Independent research calls this the 'boring but decisive' layer: as bandwidth rises, alignment precision and yield requirements rise non-linearly, and at hyperscale volumes, yield improvements compound. If demand for leading-edge optical modules keeps exploding, the suppliers who can assemble and calibrate at scale capture more value than the market tends to expect from an assembly business.
qualifier: independent research framework
vault_ref: Optical packaging research page / yield chokepoint mechanics

section_title: The neutral assembler everyone hires
body_text: Fabrinet is the purest listed expression of the precision-optical-assembly layer: it designs no products and makes no materials, by design - neutrality is what lets competing customers all trust it with their manufacturing. Its annual filing shows who depends on that neutrality: NVIDIA at 27.6% of revenue and Cisco at 18.2%, nearly half the company between two customers, with an Amazon high-performance-computing ramp emerging. On co-packaged optics its CEO disclosed programs with 'three different customers - it's not just one customer... it's actually three,' calling the technology 'an evolution from silicon photonics and the precision photonics packaging capabilities we've developed over many years,' while honestly noting the revenue 'amounts are relatively small right now.' The structural point: an assembler positioned across three competing co-packaging programs benefits from the architectural transition regardless of which platform wins it.
qualifier: from the annual filing and earnings call
vault_ref: Optical packaging research page / Fabrinet positioning

section_title: Four ways to own the same craft
body_text: Four structurally different business models compete at the optical-assembly layer, each with different economics. The neutral contract assembler (Fabrinet) owns no products, so every customer is safe to hire it. The component maker with in-house packaging (Lumentum) keeps its laser-attach know-how captive to its own parts. The full-stack vertical integrator (Coherent) spans substrate to epitaxy to chip to module to system, capturing more value per unit at the cost of competing with potential customers. And the module assembler with in-house lasers (Applied Optoelectronics) integrates upward from assembly into components, trading customer neutrality for supply security. None of these models is obviously superior - the neutral assembler wins on trust, the integrators on capture - and independent research treats the coexistence of all four as evidence the layer itself is the scarce thing, not any one configuration of it.
qualifier: structural comparison from research and filings
vault_ref: Optical packaging research page / integration model comparison

section_title: Why three-inch isn't backward
body_text: A nuance the headlines flatten: Coherent's CEO says 'we are the world's only producer of six-inch indium phosphide,' with the six-inch platform yielding more than 4x the chips at under half the cost of the industry's three-inch standard - a real advantage, from three qualified sites in Texas, Sweden, and Switzerland. But the industry standard being three-inch is not a sign of laggard competitors. Indium phosphide is brittle and thermally sensitive; scaling wafer diameter raises stress, warp, and defect-management challenges all at once, and historical industry commentary confirms larger InP wafers don't pay off until yields are brought under control. So the honest framing runs both directions: six-inch mastery is the unusual achievement, and three-inch is the sensible baseline - not substandard. In precision manufacturing, the size of the wafer matters less than the discipline of the yield curve on it.
qualifier: from earnings calls; industry context per independent research
vault_ref: Optical packaging research page / wafer-size context

section_title: Known-good-die, or expensive regret
body_text: As optical chips migrate into costlier package architectures - co-packaged with switch silicon, or integrated into compute packages - the price of packaging a bad die rises sharply. Wafer-level reliability screening stresses photonic dies before packaging: a weak die caught at that stage costs one test step, while the same die caught after assembly costs the packaging, the optical-engine integration, and the rework or scrap of the finished module. The spread between those two detection costs widens with every step up in package value, which is the entire structural case for this layer. It is distinct from its neighbors: metrology measures a die's dimensions and films, and finished-module testers check assembled products after the expensive steps - neither substitutes for stress-screening the bare die. The framing comes from independent research; whether the industry adopts it at scale is the open bet.
qualifier: independent research framework
vault_ref: Wafer test research page / KGD economics

section_title: The one pure-play's numbers
body_text: Aehr Test Systems is currently the only public pure-play on wafer-level photonics reliability test. Its recent results show the layer inflecting: bookings surged 6x quarter-over-quarter to $37.2 million, effective backlog reached a company record of $50.9 million, and advanced-packaging test drove its semiconductor segment from 3% to 25% of company revenue. It names the leading advanced-packaging platform explicitly as an application for its systems, and its CEO says the company is 'in discussions with key memory suppliers' with orders expected in fiscal 2027. The counterweights are equally on the record: customer concentration is high, some of the most-cited opportunities are forward-looking rather than shipping, and its annual filing does not mention some markets its calls emphasize. One company's order book is evidence of a layer waking up - it is not yet evidence of an industry standard.
qualifier: from the filings and earnings calls
vault_ref: Wafer test research page / Aehr positioning

section_title: The narrowest maybe in the feed
body_text: Full candor about status: this is the narrowest and least-proven bottleneck in the AI optical supply chain, and independent research treats it accordingly - a single-company exposure flagged as provisional rather than confirmed. Burn-in attach rates in silicon photonics are currently low; the thesis depends on them rising as photonic devices enter expensive packages, and that adoption is not yet demonstrated at volume. What would strengthen the case: multi-customer adoption disclosures, chip-packaging houses confirming third-party wafer-test integration, or a second equipment player entering the segment. What would weaken it: packagers handling screening in-house, finished-module testing capturing the demand instead, or photonics volumes plateauing. A bottleneck that names its own kill conditions is worth more than one that only argues its case - this page keeps both lists in view.
qualifier: independent research; provisional status stated openly
vault_ref: Wafer test research page / provisional status and tests

section_title: Commoditization as policy
body_text: The most important fact about the drone-platform layer is that its commoditization is deliberate government design, not a market accident. Per independent research on the US drone programs, the Pentagon's stated goal is to drive one-way-attack and small drones toward a roughly $2,000 unit-cost ceiling while maintaining multi-vendor competition so no supplier captures pricing power. The Drone Dominance Program - about $1.1 billion across four phases - targets hundreds of thousands of weaponized drones by end-2027. Its first competitive gauntlet ran 25 vendors under unit-cost ceilings around $5,500 and $4,500, tightening toward the $2,000 final-phase target; the second gauntlet qualified roughly 48-49 companies under harder conditions including GPS denial and jamming. A layer the buyer engineers toward a price floor, with competition deliberately preserved, is the structural opposite of a bottleneck. These program details are research-sourced, to be verified against official program documents.
qualifier: program structure per independent research
vault_ref: Drone commoditization research page / market design

section_title: The margins are the evidence
body_text: The squeeze is showing up in the platform makers' filed numbers. Kratos's Unmanned Systems segment runs a gross margin of roughly 17% - well below the company's other franchises - and consolidated operating margin was about 1.3% in a recent quarter. AeroVironment's gross margin compressed from roughly 38% to 24% after its BlueHalo acquisition, then partially recovered to about 30% adjusted for fiscal 2026 - but that recovery is a blended-portfolio figure, lifted by higher-value counter-drone and loitering-munition lines, and the one-way-attack drone line's own economics are not separately disclosed. So the honest reading: the recovery is real at the portfolio level and unproven at the airframe level. Neither company is failing - both have strong demand - but demand growth and margin capture are different things, and the airframe line is where the thesis predicted the thin margins would be.
qualifier: from the companies' filings; blended-figure caveat noted
vault_ref: Drone commoditization research page / platform margin evidence

section_title: Two escapes from the same squeeze
body_text: The two public drone primes respond to the same price squeeze in opposite ways. Kratos moves upstream: it builds its own jet engines and, through a joint venture, its own solid rocket motors - its CEO calls it the 'only company in the world that builds the plane and the engine.' The strategy is to escape platform commoditization by owning a piece of the inputs; whether that genuinely defends margin or merely absorbs capital intensity is an open question its cash flows have not yet answered. AeroVironment goes the other way: production scale, breadth across drone categories, and a climb up the value layers - its closest thing to a defensible position is its counter-drone system, a higher-value layer, not the airframes. Both companies qualified for the same government gauntlet that is engineering the price floor - meaning both are competing inside the machine that compresses them. Neither approach has proven margin protection yet; that unresolved fork is the most interesting thing in the sector.
qualifier: strategies per the companies' calls and filings; outcomes unresolved
vault_ref: Drone commoditization research page / the strategic fork

section_title: Where the margin runs
body_text: If the airframe becomes a $2,000 commodity, the value doesn't vanish - it migrates to the layers every airframe needs. Two live examples, each with an honest caveat. Teledyne owns the infrared sensor and seeker layer and supplies detectors, in its executive's words, 'to everyone else across the world that's making drones' - so it captures value regardless of which airframe wins; the caveat is that drones are only a slice of its defense revenue, and even sensors face price discipline on high-volume cheap drones. Amprius supplies high-energy-density silicon-anode batteries to many drone makers and competes inside the same government gauntlet as the primes - but on endurance, the dimension the price ceiling pushes value toward; its caveat is starker: it is loss-making and cash-burning, and its edge rests partly on a policy mandate for compliant batteries rather than on physics alone. The pattern to watch: supply every side of a commodity war, and the commodity war works for you.
qualifier: from the suppliers' filings and calls; caveats carried
vault_ref: Drone commoditization research page / value-migration winners

section_title: The convergence is the evidence
body_text: The strongest form of the AI-demand case is not any single company's claim - it is that companies at every layer of the supply chain report the same thing at once. The platform tier: NVIDIA's quarterly datacenter revenue hit $62 billion, up 75%. The foundry: TSMC says supply stays constrained through at least 2027, with high-performance computing rising from 43% to 58% of revenue in two years - a three-year structural shift, not a spike. Components: one laser maker under-ships demand by 25-30%; its competitor's datacenter book-to-bill exceeds 4x. Equipment: a test-equipment maker's bookings jumped 6x in one quarter. Systems: a cooling-and-power supplier's backlog more than doubled to $15 billion. When suppliers, their suppliers, and their suppliers' toolmakers all report constraint simultaneously, the demand signal is validated across vantage points that cannot easily coordinate a story. The caveat: every one of these companies benefits from the narrative it reports.
qualifier: cross-validated across many companies' filings and calls
vault_ref: Demand durability research page / multi-layer convergence

section_title: The payers, on the record
body_text: The roughly $640-720 billion of 2026 hyperscaler capital spending underlying the AI build-out is now anchored to the payers' own filings rather than analyst estimates. Microsoft guided ~$190 billion for calendar 2026, with one quarter's capex alone at $30.9 billion, up ~85%. Alphabet raised its guide to $180-190 billion - its capex roughly doubled - and guided 2027 to 'significantly increase.' Meta raised to $125-145 billion, and said the quiet part: most of the raise is 'due to higher component costs, particularly memory pricing' - the supply squeeze feeding back into the spending number. One honest distinction: Amazon, at $151 billion of trailing-twelve-month capex, gave no precise annual dollar guide - only qualitative commitments - so its circulating 2026 figure is an analyst number, not a filed one. Oracle extends the cohort: capex $55.7 billion, up 162%, with free cash flow at negative $23.7 billion - the clearest case of a payer funding the build with financing rather than operations.
qualifier: from the hyperscalers' filings and calls; Amazon's annual figure flagged as analyst-sourced
vault_ref: Demand durability research page / hyperscaler capex primary sourcing

section_title: Half a trillion, contracted
body_text: Beyond the spending, the demand is increasingly contractual. Microsoft's total remaining performance obligations stand at $633 billion. Oracle's reached $638 billion, up 363% year-over-year - though long-dated, with only about 12% recognized within twelve months, and the widely-reported counterparty behind the surge is named in neither the filing nor the call. Google Cloud's backlog is $462 billion, having nearly doubled in a quarter; AWS's is $364 billion, excluding a newly-announced deal described as over $100 billion, plus more than $225 billion of chip-revenue commitments. On the supply side, memory maker Micron holds 16 non-cancelable take-or-pay agreements worth $22 billion, with management stating bit growth is now 'supply-determined, not demand-determined' and visibility running into 2028.
