body_text: The AI datacenter's optical plumbing splits into two product architectures - today's front-panel pluggable modules (800G moving to 1.6T) and the emerging co-packaged optics that move light into the chip package - sitting on ten shared infrastructure layers: lasers and external light sources, modulators, optical DSP, fiber attach and connectors, optical circuit switches, photonic foundries, assembly and packaging and test, thermal and liquid cooling, upstream materials and substrates, and specialized 224G electronics. The near-term battle is bill-of-materials competition underneath the module shell - the control points are the DSP, the modulator choice, the laser source, the connector, and the manufacturing-test flow, each a separate layer with its own competitive structure. The rule that organizes the whole map: architecture transitions re-weight value across these layers rather than eliminating them. Co-packaging doesn't delete the supply chain; it shifts the bottleneck toward optical engines, external lasers, detachable fiber attach, package integration, and wafer-level photonic test. Anyone arguing about the architecture label is arguing about the wrong altitude.
qualifier: structural map from curated research plus company filings
vault_ref: Photonics map research page / architecture and layer overview

section_title: The unglamorous reasons things persist
body_text: Two under-appreciated mechanics govern the pluggable-versus-co-packaged transition. First, serviceability: a failed pluggable module is replaced at the switch faceplate in minutes without disturbing anything else, while a co-packaged failure can mean package-level rework - an operational advantage that keeps pluggables the volume center of gravity even as the co-packaged roadmap advances. Second, fiber attach: in pluggables, fiber connectivity is mostly a density-and-cleanliness problem; in co-packaged designs it becomes precision packaging - repeatable low-loss coupling, detachability, blind-mate alignment - which the research calls 'one of the least appreciated but most real bottlenecks in the CPO transition,' simultaneously a precision-mechanics problem, a loss-budget problem, and a field-service problem. The lesson both mechanics teach: the deciding factors in architecture wars are rarely the headline speeds - they're the boring operational physics of what a technician can fix at 3 a.m.
qualifier: from the research primer's bottleneck-hierarchy analysis
vault_ref: Photonics map research page / serviceability and fiber attach

section_title: The fiber layer's demand anchor - and a corrected myth
body_text: The fiber-and-cable layer has the map's cleanest primary demand signal: Corning's Optical Communications segment grew 35% year-over-year to $6.3 billion (38% of segment revenue), anchored by a $6 billion multi-year agreement with Meta supporting its North Carolina capacity expansion for AI infrastructure - with 'several' similar-size agreements concluding with other major customers, their financial impact landing 2027-2028. A fact-check from the primary filings is worth carrying: circulating research described Corning's AI innovation as 'multicore fiber' - multiple light-carrying cores in one strand, with 4x path density. The company's own filings document a different architecture: Contour fiber, a single-core design with a 40% smaller outer diameter, and cabling that doubles density in the same footprint. Single-core densification and multicore fiber are structurally different technologies; whether multicore is even on the roadmap is an open question. Small correction, useful habit: even the boring layers deserve their claims checked against the filing.
qualifier: from the annual filing and earnings call; the multicore correction noted
vault_ref: Photonics map research page / fiber layer anchor

section_title: Where the money hides in a robot
body_text: A humanoid is five functional systems on a materials substrate with an AI brain across the top - and value distributes brutally unevenly. Actuators run an estimated 33-60% of the bill of materials depending on whose model you use. Goldman Sachs scored every component on six criteria (content value, certainty of technology choice, technical barrier, manufacturing barrier, capacity flexibility, incremental opportunity): harmonic reduction gears score highest (16/18) - high value, high barriers, its explicit top pick; dexterous hands score 15 but carry the lowest certainty-of-architecture (nobody agrees what a robot hand should be); actuator assembly and planetary roller screws score 14. Cameras, LiDAR, and batteries sit at the bottom - 'mostly ready,' meaning commoditized. The deeper overlay ranks by what makes each position durable: magnet physics beats precision-grinding know-how beats integration skill beats commodity parts. Score suppliers on the durability of their position, not on today's order book - the order book is mostly hope at this stage anyway.
qualifier: bank component rankings, attributed; BOM splits vary by source
vault_ref: Humanoid research page / value-capture gradient

section_title: The best parts are the hardest to own
body_text: The defining feature of the humanoid supply map: value ranking and market access run in opposite directions. The four highest-value layers have no clean US-listed pure-play - the harmonic-gear gold standard is Japanese (Tokyo-listed, no clean ADR), the roller-screw incumbents are German and Taiwanese, and the actuator complex is overwhelmingly Chinese A-share or private. US-listed names cluster in the lower-ranked layers: sensors, cameras, LiDAR - plus the magnet substrate and the AI brain investors already own through the datacenter story. Two cracks in the wall: the actuator headline pick Sanhua completed a Hong Kong listing in July 2025 (the first top-value-layer name internationally reachable), and Tuopu announced a planned Hong Kong listing of its own. The important nuance: this 'terrible access' framing is a US-brokerage lens - for an investor who can hold China A-shares directly, the tension inverts entirely, and the strongest component makers become the most ownable layer. Access is a route, not a verdict; the durable question is always the quality of the position.
qualifier: listing statuses verified; the access framing is lens-dependent
vault_ref: Humanoid research page / access inversion

section_title: 150 robots and a million-unit supply chain
body_text: The scale reality check that belongs before every humanoid projection: industry estimates put the leading makers - Tesla, Figure, Agility - at roughly 150 units shipped EACH in 2025, with realistic 2026 output in the low thousands. Against that, Goldman's base case reaches a $38 billion market by 2035 (~1.4 million units), and the more excitable projections run to trillions by 2050. What drove the banks' upgrades was real: a ~40% component-cost decline and - their words - 'AI progress surprised us the most,' as end-to-end vision-language-action models replaced hand-coded behaviors. But the demand side carries a pre-registered gamble the research keeps in view: Goldman's own supplier survey found Chinese component makers building 100,000-to-1-million-unit annual capacity without confirmed binding orders - capacity first, customers later. The first sizable confirmed OEM order validates the gamble; its continued absence into late 2026 signals overcapacity. An industry can be simultaneously the future and 150 units a year - holding both facts at once is the discipline.
qualifier: industry estimates and bank surveys, attributed
vault_ref: Humanoid research page / early-innings scale check

section_title: What the filings say when you actually read them
body_text: Twelve supply-chain names have now been read at primary source, and the filings deflate the headlines with precision. The two actuator 'headline picks': both are healthy industrial blue-chips whose humanoid actuator businesses are research-and-sample stage - pre-revenue - so the banks' 'dominance' framing is a forward bet, not either company's disclosed reality. The harmonic-gear gold standard, Harmonic Drive of Japan: the strongest, most-validated position in the whole chain, on a fortress balance sheet - and a cyclically-depressed incumbent whose own management is the cohort's most candid that the AI-robot order ramp has slowed. The only company anywhere in the map that quantifies humanoid revenue is a US sensor maker - at roughly $600,000 a quarter, about 1.5% of its revenue. And the most credible roller-screw entrant mentions humanoids in its annual report exactly zero times. The pattern: the positions are real, the businesses are real, and the humanoid revenue is almost everywhere still a rounding error. That's not a reason to ignore the chain - it's the reason to read it with the lights on.
qualifier: from the companies' filings via the primary-ingested pages
vault_ref: Humanoid research page / primary-ingest honest verdicts

section_title: Own the corpus, or rent out the labor
body_text: The AI-data category spans a steep moat gradient that its collective label obscures. At the top: toll booths - proprietary data fused to sticky professional workflows (legal research, credit ratings, financial data), which no AI company can relabel or synthesize around; a court affirmed exactly this when a legal-data giant won against an AI startup trained on its headnotes. In the middle: scarce corpora (a uniquely scaled social-conversation dataset, licensable but non-exclusive) and challenged owners (stock-photo libraries licensing to the technology displacing them). At the bottom: annotation and labeling services - labor arbitrage with low switching costs, the layer most exposed to being in-housed. The organizing distinction: a unique corpus is a stock you own; annotation capacity is a flow you rent. Scarcity raises the value of owned stocks far more durably than rented flows - the same own-the-bottleneck-versus-rent-out-the-service gradient that governs every supply chain in this feed, applied to information itself.
qualifier: commissioned research framework; placements are structural, not advice
vault_ref: AI data research page / value-capture gradient

section_title: A sector that is mostly one company
body_text: Two findings deflate the category's narrative. First: the publicly-investable pure-play supplier surface is razor thin - once you remove the incumbents who keep their data for themselves, the only US-listed sells-into-the-AI-boom data company of meaningful size is Innodata; the social-media corpus owner's actual licensing line is small (~5-6% of its revenue, the rest is advertising), and the interesting expert-evaluation middle (Scale, Surge, Mercor) is entirely private. Second, and more telling: the highest-quality 'AI-data' names win by refusing to supply. The professional-data giants restrict training use of their content, monetize AI through live, permissioned access - the emerging connector model lets an AI agent query the data source in real time without the owner ever surrendering training rights - and build their own AI products on top. By their own disclosures they are AI deployers, not data suppliers. The uncomfortable corollary: the companies whose actual business is supplying the AI boom are thin and fragile; the great businesses keep their gold.
qualifier: commissioned research with primary verification on the one listed pure-play
vault_ref: AI data research page / investable-surface findings

section_title: Three ways the feedstock trade dies
body_text: Three demonstrated risks bound the category. In-housing: Google terminated its major annotation contractor and internalized rater capacity; Meta's $14.3 billion stake in Scale AI prompted Google, OpenAI, and xAI to pull their work - a hard ceiling on commodity-labeling margins, though it never touches the toll-booth owners. Synthetic data: a bifurcation, not a substitution - machine-generated data already displaces human annotation in verifiable domains like code and math, but recursive training on synthetic data causes documented 'model collapse' (published in Nature), so expert judgment and safety evaluation stay human-dependent; commodity labeling loses, expert evaluation wins. Copyright: the 2025 rulings tilted pro-developer, making training-as-fair-use the trajectory - data owners now get paid only with both unique content and enforcement leverage, and the one clean large transfer (a $1.5 billion settlement with an AI lab) was for pirated acquisition of books, not for training itself being infringement. The pattern across all three: value keeps retreating up the gradient toward what can't be substituted, in-housed, or fair-used.
qualifier: documented cases and rulings via commissioned research
vault_ref: AI data research page / risk pillars

section_title: The inversion law
body_text: The drone supply chain runs from upstream inputs (magnets, secure chips, seekers, batteries, edge-AI compute) through components and platforms to autonomy software and counter-drone systems - and value capture runs inverted to visibility. The platform layer everyone photographs scores lowest on structural attractiveness: small and one-way-attack drones are being deliberately commoditized toward a ~$2,000 price floor by their own government buyer. The upstream inputs score highest: scarce, concentrated, capacity-constrained. Autonomy software scores 'very high' with an asterisk - the best capability is private. Counter-drone systems are persistent, under-served, and fragmented. This is the defense analogue of the AI build-out's central insight that money is not the bottleneck: airframes are not the bottleneck either - the magnet, the secure chip, the seeker, and the autonomy stack are. Anyone evaluating a defense-drone company should first ask which layer it actually lives on, because the layer predicts the economics better than the headlines do.
qualifier: research framework; layer placements cross-referenced to filings
vault_ref: Defense map research page / value-chain inversion

section_title: Dependence, in the primes' own words
body_text: The owner-consumer links on this map are documented in filings, not inferred from logic. AeroVironment's annual filing states its motors and batteries rely on rare-earth metals 'of which a significant majority are sourced from China,' notes China placed it on an export-control list in March 2025, and separately names its secure-microelectronics dependence - so the visible platform prime explicitly consumes the magnet bottleneck and the secure-chip bottleneck it does not own. Kratos runs the opposite play: its filings disclose no rare-earth dependence at all (its China references are to China as an adversary driving demand, not a supplier), because it vertically integrates - building its own jet engines and, through a joint venture, its own solid rocket motors, trying to move upstream rather than depend on it. A discipline note that matters as much as the links: where a company's drones structurally must use these inputs but its filings don't disclose the dependence, the map marks it inferred and doesn't assert it. Disclosed and inferred are different grades of fact, and a map is only as honest as its weakest link-label.
qualifier: from the primes' annual filings; non-disclosures noted as such
vault_ref: Defense map research page / owner-consumer links

section_title: The squeezed middles and the Taiwan seam
body_text: Two structural patterns complete the map. First, the squeezed middle: several strong positions own a bottleneck downstream while depending on one upstream. Teledyne dominates Western infrared sensors - supplying detectors 'to everyone else across the world that's making drones' - yet its filings disclose rare-earth-magnet and germanium dependence that has 'in the past delayed' production. Amprius is positioned as the NDAA-compliant domestic drone-battery answer while sourcing materials 'primarily from China.' Owning one layer doesn't exempt you from the ones above. Second, the cross-thesis seam: the secure-chip node bottlenecks on the same foundry the AI build-out lives on - the leading secure-FPGA designer is fabless on TSMC in Taiwan with heavy China revenue, and the 'trusted supply chain' rules govern design and assembly but never solved the fabrication layer. The defense supply chain and the AI supply chain converge on the same Taiwanese fabs and the same Chinese materials - two theses, one set of physical dependencies.
qualifier: disclosed dependencies from filings; the seam framing is analysis
vault_ref: Defense map research page / squeezed middles and cross-thesis seam

section_title: Reading the warrants
body_text: The custom-silicon relationships between hyperscalers and chip designers are visible through an unusual instrument: revenue-milestone-vesting warrants. Astera Labs granted Amazon warrants vesting on $6.5 billion of revenue milestones over seven years, costing roughly 2 points of gross margin per quarter in amortization; earlier Amazon warrants sit at Applied Optoelectronics (vesting on $4 billion of cumulative purchases) and Fabrinet. Marvell carries two warrants for unnamed customers tied to its custom-chip programs. The mechanics are universal: vesting on purchases, not time - so the hyperscaler cannot walk away without forfeiting unvested shares, and the supplier accepts margin compression as the price of multi-year volume lock-in. Reported margins therefore understate the product's underlying economics while overstating what's left for the supplier after the customer's equity participation. Warrants are what supply-relationship depth looks like when it has to be disclosed - revealed preference, printed in a footnote.
qualifier: from the suppliers' filings and calls
vault_ref: Custom ASIC research page / warrant comparison

section_title: The one who doesn't pay in equity
body_text: The naming pattern in the warrant record is itself a signal. Amazon's investment entity is the only named warrant holder anywhere - across three different suppliers, in procurement and contract-manufacturing contexts where naming is unavoidable - while the custom-chip warrant holders stay anonymous, consistent with the industry convention that custom-silicon customers are never named. And then there is the outlier: Broadcom discloses six custom-chip customers (four named on calls: Google, Anthropic, Meta, OpenAI) approaching roughly 10 gigawatts of aggregate 2027 deployment - the largest custom-silicon franchise disclosed anywhere - with no warrant structures in its filings at all. The absence is analytically loud: it may mean Broadcom's position (its interconnect leadership, its secured supply through 2028) gives it enough leverage to keep its equity, or that its relationships run through different structures entirely. In a market where everyone else pays for lock-in with shares, the one who doesn't is telling you something about who needs whom.
qualifier: from the filings; the interpretation of absence is analysis
vault_ref: Custom ASIC research page / structural observations

section_title: The risk in the filing, dismissed on the call
body_text: The custom-chip designers' deepest structural risk appears in their own annual filings: customer-owned tooling - as hyperscalers accumulate chip-design expertise across generations, they may need their design partners less and less. Both major custom-ASIC designers flag exactly this risk in their formal filings; both CEOs dismiss it on earnings calls - a documented gap between what the lawyers write and what the executives perform, and a recurring pattern worth reading as its own signal. The market-level boundary is equally honest: hyperscaler chips genuinely ship (Microsoft's inference chip is confirmed in production; Google's, Amazon's, and Meta's programs scale), and roughly half of top-hyperscaler CPU compute already runs on Arm-based custom designs - but the widely-claimed aggregate revenue displacement of NVIDIA failed adversarial verification. What the evidence supports today: custom silicon and merchant GPUs growing concurrently, with the zero-sum endgame asserted often and demonstrated never. The first disclosed per-vendor custom-chip revenue breakout would change that; until then, both stories remain open.
qualifier: the filing-vs-call gap is documented; displacement claims flagged unverified
vault_ref: Custom ASIC research page / COT risk and displacement boundary

section_title: Look at the meter, not the moat
body_text: The research's sharpest contribution: the billed unit predicts AI-moat durability better than the moat's type, the company's margin, or its retention rate. Seat-billed software - per-user licenses - is structurally exposed: where the customer pays by human seat and AI removes or reduces the humans, revenue compresses even if software usage rises. Consumption-billed software - compute, tokens, records, workflow executions - enjoys a tailwind: agents consume more of the metered resource than humans ever did. Outcome-billed - per resolution, per completed task - is the AI-native model incumbents are racing toward. The reframe cuts through familiar categories: the same 'system of record' is amplified when consumption-metered and contested when seat-metered. Which is why the seat incumbents are all quietly building consumption overlays - 'assists,' 'flex credits' - the explicit defensive conversion. The tension stopped being hypothetical in early 2026, when analysts began downgrading seat-priced software names explicitly on per-seat AI risk. The meter is the moat's stress test.
qualifier: commissioned research framework; company metrics as reported
vault_ref: Software moats research page / billed-unit discriminator

section_title: Half the demand story is proven; the other half isn't
body_text: This research exists because the AI infrastructure build-out is only justified if AI value gets captured at the software layer - and the verdict bifurcates. The validated leg: AI monetization is real and metered where the billed unit is consumption - cloud infrastructure, data platforms, observability, security. Snowflake's product revenue grew 34% with 126% net retention; these revenues scale with agent activity and genuinely corroborate infrastructure demand. The unproven leg: the seat-priced productivity narrative. Microsoft cites an AI run-rate of ~$37 billion growing 123% - a management construct spanning many products - but its 20-million-plus Copilot seats represent roughly 3.3% penetration of a 450-million-seat base, and one survey found only 8% of users preferring Copilot against 70% for ChatGPT when they hold both. If the seat-to-consumption transition stalls or agent ROI disappoints at renewal time, the productivity leg of the capex thesis weakens even while the infrastructure legs hold. Two legs, one verdict each - blending them is how the demand story gets oversold.
qualifier: figures as reported in the anchor; management constructs labeled
vault_ref: Software moats research page / bifurcated demand read

section_title: Five moat archetypes, five different fates
body_text: Run five classic software-moat archetypes through the AI lens and they diverge sharply. Distribution-and-bundling: contested - shipping AI into an owned installed base works only if users actually prefer the bundled AI, and the early preference data is unflattering. System-of-record with proprietary data: amplified - agents need trusted, governed context, and the data layer is consumption-metered, with the caveat that agents may detach the workflow from the interface the vendor monetizes. Workflow lock-in: contested - process embedding defends the base while seat deflation erodes the expansion. Data flywheels: amplified where the flywheel is proprietary usage data - Palantir's US commercial revenue grew 133% with 150% net retention. And the category the research names 'AI-vulnerable deep moats': eroded - businesses whose core value is a task AI automates, where high retention and high margins mask the exposure right up until they don't. Electronic signatures, tier-one support tooling, stock media: the moat was real; the task it defended is what's dissolving.
qualifier: archetype verdicts per the research; company figures as reported
vault_ref: Software moats research page / archetype matrix

section_title: The bull steelman, from the vendor who needs it true
body_text: The strongest argument that software moats survive AI came from Microsoft's CEO in a June 2026 essay: the durable layer is not the frontier model (a commoditizing input, he argues) but the firm-owned 'learning loop' - private evaluations against the company's own outcomes, models trained on its real internal traces, and a queryable institutional memory. His proposed moat test is elegant: a firm should be able to swap out the underlying AI model without losing its accumulated 'company veteran' expertise. Read honestly, this is the argument a platform company needs to be true - it describes Microsoft's exact competitive position and de-emphasizes the frontier-model race where it depends on a partner it doesn't control. So the claim carries a pre-registered verifier rather than acceptance: the learning loop graduates from narrative to moat only when it shows up as a measurable switching cost - retention holding through a model swap, a separately-disclosed durable revenue line, or usage depth that survives a migration. Vendor essays don't count. Filings will.
qualifier: a CEO's own-book essay, framed as claim with a pre-registered test
vault_ref: Software moats research page / learning-loop cross-read

section_title: 'No shortcuts' - the moat in four pillars
body_text: Asked repeatedly about challengers on its early-2026 call, TSMC's CEO C.C. Wei gave the same four-pillar answer: technology leadership, manufacturing excellence, customer trust - and 'no shortcuts.' On Intel specifically: 'they are our competitors and we view Intel as a formidable competitors and do not underestimate them. But having said that, there are no shortcuts, the fundamental rule of the foundry game never change.' The structural version of the argument is the timeline: a new fab takes two to three years to build and one to two more to ramp, so even technical parity at a node leaves the incumbent's installed base intact for years - competitive threats develop slowly and in plain sight. A venue note worth keeping: TSMC's formal annual filing names no competitor at all, listing threats generically, while the earnings call discussed Intel's initiatives and Samsung's win specifically - for competitive dynamics, the call tells you more than the filing, exactly opposite to the usual reliability ranking.
qualifier: from the earnings call and annual filing
vault_ref: Foundry research page / TSMC competitive defense

section_title: The challenger grades himself
body_text: The most credible assessment of Intel's foundry challenge came from Intel's own CEO, on a mid-2026 podcast: he calls Intel 'very distant from TSMC' on foundry performance, frames the catch-up as a multi-year 'trust business' that only 'surfaces up' around 2030-2032, and says plainly 'we have a lot of respect for TSMC... we both need more capacity.' He also recounts the decision to double down on foundry against internal voices calling it too expensive - backed by an industrial-policy capital stack he describes as a US-government equity stake (he analogizes it to Taiwan's early stake in TSMC), a reported ~$5 billion NVIDIA investment, and SoftBank. The honest reading: a CEO talking his book still chose to size the gap in years, not quarters - his own account is consistent with the incumbent's 'no shortcuts' framing rather than a challenge to it. A challenger who says 'trust me by 2032' has told you the shape of the race. These are spoken claims, flagged for verification against Intel's filings.
qualifier: the CEO's spoken account; capital-stack figures flagged for verification
vault_ref: Foundry research page / Intel own-side view

section_title: The inroad that shrank under inspection
body_text: Samsung's celebrated 'first true inroads' into AI foundry - an analyst's framing on TSMC's call, citing Samsung manufacturing 'NVIDIA's LPU' - is a case study in chasing a claim across venues. NVIDIA's own earnings call never uses the term 'LPU'; TSMC's 244-page annual filing doesn't either. The likely resolution surfaced at NVIDIA's March 2026 conference: the third-generation Groq-designed inference chip, manufactured by Samsung, absorbed into NVIDIA's serving stack as a specialized low-latency accelerator. If that identification holds - and it remains a strong link, not a confirmed identity - Samsung's AI-foundry win is an inference-accelerator co-opted into the NVIDIA ecosystem, not a GPU or leading-edge-logic win: real, symbolically significant, and much narrower than the 'first true inroads' headline implied. TSMC's CEO, notably, confirmed awareness and said the quiet competitive part aloud: 'we are working with our customers for their next-generation LPU anyway.' Watch for broadening beyond the single chip; that would be the material development.
qualifier: cross-venue reconstruction; the LPU identification is a strong link, not confirmed
vault_ref: Foundry research page / Samsung LPU resolution

section_title: Insurance you can read in the margin line
body_text: The most honest indicator that semiconductor geopolitics is real isn't commentary - it's a cost accepted on an income statement. TSMC's overseas fabs dilute its gross margin by an estimated 2-4%, a structural headwind the company and its customers knowingly accept in exchange for geography. That acceptance is the signal: when the world's most disciplined manufacturer and its most demanding customers pay a recurring premium to reduce Taiwan concentration, they are pricing a risk they take seriously into business decisions. The expansion cuts two ways for anyone tracking the foundry's position: it slightly weakens the margin story while slightly strengthening the resilience story - and it quietly confirms that everyone involved considers the concentration risk worth paying real money to reduce, which is itself information about the risk.
qualifier: from the company's disclosures; the dilution range is management-cited
vault_ref: Overseas fab research page / margin-dilution signal

section_title: Arizona: from hedge to strategy
body_text: TSMC's Arizona site began as a geopolitical concession and is becoming something more. Per the annual filing: the first fab entered high-volume manufacturing at the end of 2024, a second is under construction with 3-nanometer volume production scheduled for the second half of 2027, a third commenced in 2025, and an additional land parcel was acquired in 2026 - all 100% TSMC-owned, supported by $6.6 billion of direct CHIPS Act funding, up to $5 billion in loans, and a 25% investment tax credit. North American headcount has roughly doubled in two years to over 5,000. On the call, the CEO expressed 'much more confidence than last year' in improving the Arizona cost structure as operational experience accumulates, and an analyst noted the site 'is becoming more and more strategic' with its leading-edge commitment. The trajectory matters: a hedge doesn't need a third fab and more land - a second manufacturing center does.
qualifier: from the annual filing and earnings call
vault_ref: Overseas fab research page / Arizona trajectory

section_title: The co-owner model, and the honest denominator
body_text: Japan shows the alternative structure: the JASM fab is 72.6% TSMC-owned with Sony, DENSO, and Toyota as industrial co-owners - customers embedded as shareholders, each bringing demand and domain expertise, with the first fab running image-sensor production since December 2024 and a second planned for 3-nanometer around 2028. But the honest denominator check keeps the whole diversification story in proportion: US noncurrent assets stand at roughly 14% of TSMC's total (and Japan's at ~3%), nearly flat year-over-year despite active construction, and the true leading edge - the newest nodes, the advanced packaging that gates every AI accelerator - remains concentrated in Taiwan. The overseas build-out is real, funded, and accelerating; it is also, so far, a modest fraction of the machine. Anyone claiming Taiwan concentration is 'solved' hasn't read the asset footnote.
qualifier: from the annual filing's segment and asset disclosures
vault_ref: Overseas fab research page / JV model and scale check

section_title: Integrate first, narrate later
body_text: The playbook was established with Mellanox: acquire a best-in-class technology in an adjacent domain (~$6.9 billion, closed April 2020), integrate it into the platform, and turn it into a growth vector - full-year networking revenue reached $31 billion, ten times the acquisition-era level. The under-appreciated detail is the narration lag: Mellanox contributed 14% of revenue in its first quarter inside NVIDIA, confirming immediate operational integration - but networking wasn't disclosed as its own revenue line until four years later, once it was large enough to support an independent growth story. That gap between operational absorption and public narration is itself the pattern signal: the platform integrates quietly and surfaces acquired capabilities only at scale. The taxonomy since spans three modalities - full acquisition (Mellanox), licensing-plus-team (Groq), and equity-plus-purchase-commitments (the laser makers) - each calibrated to how much of the target the platform actually needs to own. Watch the integrations, not the announcements; the announcements come years late by design.
qualifier: from filings, calls, and the acquisition record
vault_ref: NVDA playbook research page / Mellanox template

section_title: Absorbing the threat that beat you
body_text: The Groq deal deserves double-entry bookkeeping. What happened: NVIDIA structured a non-exclusive license for Groq's low-latency inference technology, hired the engineering team, and folded the chip into its serving platform as a specialized accelerator - Jensen's framing: 'we'll extend our architecture with Groq as an accelerator in very much the ways that we extended NVIDIA's architecture with Mellanox.' The scale surfaced later in a quarterly-filing footnote: $3,957 million of accrued purchase consideration - roughly $4 billion, the first hard quantification, comparable to both $2 billion laser stakes combined. Two interpretations, both true: it is platform strength - a competitive threat co-opted rather than fought; and it is an admission - Groq's design was genuinely superior at low-latency decoding, evidence the GPU architecture is not universally optimal for all inference work, specialized enough that the platform chose to license rather than replicate. Asked directly whether NVIDIA should build workload-specific silicon, Jensen emphasized versatility - and notably did not say no.
qualifier: from the 10-Q footnote, calls, and the GTC record
vault_ref: NVDA playbook research page / Groq absorption

section_title: One deal, two stories, zero mentions
body_text: The March 2026 laser investments - $2 billion each into Lumentum and Coherent - offer a rare perspectival experiment, because both suppliers discussed the then-unannounced negotiations on consecutive days. Lumentum's framing: 'active negotiations with leading customers to offset our capital requirements in exchange for long-term supply assurances' - the investment fills a capital gap (its free cash flow was ~$25 million against ~$320 million of annualized capex). Coherent's framing: customers making 'financial commitment... like investment for CapEx,' later 'an expansion of a more than 20-year relationship' - relationship formalization from a company that could self-fund. Same capital flow, two truthful stories, sorted by which side needed the money. Then the coda: on NVIDIA's next earnings call, neither supplier was mentioned - not by name, not by description - despite $4 billion deployed. The asymmetry is structural: customers get named (they define the platform's reach); suppliers stay anonymous (they're treated as ecosystem infrastructure). Reading this platform means reading its silences.
qualifier: from both suppliers' calls and NVIDIA's disclosure record
vault_ref: NVDA playbook research page / laser-stake perspectives

section_title: A portfolio, not a parade
body_text: The cooling transition maps onto rack-density bands, and the bands coexist rather than replace each other. Legacy air handles under ~30-50 kW per rack - the vast installed base. Hybrid air-liquid (rear-door heat exchangers plus coolant-distribution overlays) covers the 50-100 kW mainstream, favored because it retrofits into existing buildings. Direct-to-chip liquid owns the 100-240 kW band where the current AI accelerator generations live. Immersion - submerging hardware in dielectric fluid - remains hyperscaler experimentation at 240 kW-plus, greenfield-only since the fluid doesn't retrofit. The forward path points to 300 kW-plus and eventually megawatt racks. The operators' own survey data explains the shape: retrofit ease is the top viability criterion at 46%, which is why the brownfield world moves through hybrid stages while only new AI campuses jump straight to direct-to-chip. Transitions in physical infrastructure are gated by what the existing building can accept - the keynote architecture and the deployed architecture are different data series.
qualifier: operator surveys and vendor disclosures via research
vault_ref: Cooling transition research page / architecture bands

section_title: Mandatory for whom, and when
body_text: The honest mandatoriness verdict is scope-bifurcated, and blending the scopes is how the story gets oversold. For frontier AI facilities: new training halls and premium AI suites are liquid-ready by default around 2028; liquid becomes the default design assumption for new high-density facilities above 50-100 kW per rack by roughly 2030; and it turns effectively mandatory at hundreds of kilowatts and megawatt rack envelopes by ~2035, when an air-only AI factory becomes a niche exception. For general enterprise computing, storage, and networking: air cooling remains dominant and viable for years - the installed base is 75% perimeter air-cooled today, with direct liquid at 22%. The merchant-chip roadmaps lock in the frontier half regardless of vendor share: AMD's newest accelerator ships liquid-only and its 2026 rack is fully liquid-cooled, matching NVIDIA's no-air-option generation. So the claim 'liquid cooling is mandatory' is true for the AI frontier on a dated schedule, and false as a universal - precision about scope is the whole analysis.
qualifier: scenario framework from research; survey figures as cited
vault_ref: Cooling transition research page / mandatoriness timeline

section_title: Seven suppliers, eighteen months, one conviction
body_text: The strongest behavioral evidence for the transition comes from the suppliers' own strategic actions: all seven tracked thermal-management companies executed transformation events within a single 18-month window - Eaton acquired Boyd Thermal, Vertiv acquired multiple coolant-distribution specialists, Flex acquired the microconvective-cooling maker JetCool (whose technology handles over 3,000 watts per device), Modine restructured around its datacenter cooling business, and the others built capacity or launched dedicated product lines. Companies spending acquisition dollars simultaneously is demand conviction expressed in capital, not commentary. The barriers operators actually report - standardization (39%), expense (38%), reliability concerns (35%), maintenance (26%) - describe an industrial maturation problem, not a physics problem. That framing also sets the open question honestly: NVIDIA's CEO claims the new generation runs chillerless on 45-degree supply water; whether warm-water design eliminates a whole equipment layer or merely relocates the heat problem is a keynote promise awaiting deployed evidence.
qualifier: transformation events from filings; barrier data from operator surveys
vault_ref: Cooling transition research page / supplier cadence and barriers

section_title: Why the rack is going to 800 volts
body_text: As AI racks climb toward 600 kilowatts and beyond, conventional lower-voltage power distribution hits its physical limits - the copper gets too thick, the losses too large - so NVIDIA is driving a transition to 800-volt DC architecture for its 2027 rack generation, with the power conversion moving closer to the chips. Every step of that chain needs power semiconductors: gallium-nitride and silicon-carbide devices for conversion stages, power-management chips beside the processors, vertical power delivery underneath them. The per-rack power-chip content expands accordingly - which is the entire investment narrative of the sub-sector. The discipline the research insists on: the transition is real and dated, but 'the 800V theme' is not a business result. Five companies claim the tailwind with five different structural positions, and the value capture is demonstrably asymmetric - which makes variant-sorting, not theme-riding, the actual analysis.
qualifier: architecture per NVIDIA disclosures; content-expansion per industry research
vault_ref: Power semis research page / 800V transition substrate

section_title: Five claimants, five different animals
body_text: The five listed claimants on the 800-volt story span a 131-to-1 scale range and utterly different economics. Navitas: the theme-central pure-play - $45.9 million of revenue (down 45%), a widening $117 million loss, ~14 quarters of cash runway, and an NVIDIA 'partnership' substantiated only as a demo board at a trade show. Power Integrations: a profitable 30-year incumbent where AI is a minor embedded end-market. Vicor: the commercial inflection - backlog up 70% in a quarter to $300.6 million, book-to-bill above 2.0, guidance up ~40% - carrying real execution risks (a securities class action, patent-court dependencies, acute single-customer concentration on an inferred wafer-scale AI customer). ON Semiconductor: the scale IDM at a deep cyclical trough, guiding AI revenue from $250 million toward $500 million - a figure that appears on calls but not in its SEC filings. Monolithic Power: the quality compounder, record $804 million quarterly revenue with an actually-named Enterprise Data segment. One theme; a venture bet, three incumbents, and a compounder. Precision about which one you're discussing is the entire game.
qualifier: figures from the five companies' filings and calls
vault_ref: Power semis research page / five-variant matrix

section_title: A socket-loss rumor, dissected at primary
body_text: The sub-sector produced a textbook case of checking research chatter against primary sources. Industry research circulated a claim that Monolithic Power had lost 60-70% of its flagship AI-platform power socket to European and Japanese rivals - a thesis-weakening story that traveled widely. At primary: the company posted record quarterly revenue of $804 million (up 26%), gross margins held at ~55% for the fourth consecutive quarter, its named Enterprise Data segment stayed essentially flat in dollars (the falling percentage was a composition effect from five other segments growing 26-46%), and its CEO answered the socket question directly: 'I don't see we lose any socket, okay? The major socket at least, okay? We keep winning' - while raising the segment's growth floor to 85%. A CEO's denial is an incentivized claim, not proof - but a rumor that contradicts the revenue, the margins, and the guidance simultaneously carries the burden of proof. The general lesson: in a hot sub-sector, socket-rumor churn is constant; the filings are the referee.
qualifier: the rumor was third-party research; the contradiction is primary
vault_ref: Power semis research page / MPWR socket-rumor contradiction

section_title: Thirteen bottlenecks, ranked
body_text: The framework decomposes the AI optical supply chain into thirteen bottlenecks across six categories, each with a rank and a conviction grade. The top of the table: lasers and EML devices at #1 (very high conviction - 'the most direct optical bottleneck' for the 800G/1.6T and co-packaged ramps, because a weak light-source supply chain constrains the entire module ramp); optical signal processors at #2 (the power-performance gate); indium-phosphide substrate supply and epitaxial capacity at #3-4 (the material every laser starts from); precision optical packaging at #5 (the manufacturing-yield gate). The middle ranks cover test infrastructure and the switch-platform control point; the lower ranks cover foundry photonics capacity, co-packaging integration, fiber attach, thermal, and board power. The ordering is itself the analytical claim: it says where scarcity binds hardest and where an investor's attention earns the most per hour. Like all rankings, it is a dated hypothesis - drawn in April 2026 - and each rank carries a to-be-validated flag until primary evidence promotes it.
qualifier: curated research framework; ranks are graded hypotheses
vault_ref: Rankings research page / 13-chokepoint league table

section_title: Scarcity is one axis; authority is the other
body_text: The framework's most useful structural distinction: there are two different ways to matter in a supply chain. Bottleneck participation is supply-side scarcity - owning capacity that demand exceeds, like laser fabs or substrate crystal growth. Control-point participation is architectural decision authority - shaping the design choices that determine where ecosystem value accrues, whether or not you own any scarce capacity. The switch-platform layer is the framework's most explicit control point: the companies that define rack architectures decide which optical components get designed in, which is power over other people's bottlenecks. The two axes are complementary, not competing - the deepest positions in the whole map combine both (scarce capacity AND architectural influence), while the most fragile combine neither (commodity capacity taking architecture as given). When evaluating any supply-chain company, ask both questions separately: is its capacity scarce, and does it shape the decisions? One yes is a position. Two is a franchise.
qualifier: framework distinction, paraphrased
vault_ref: Rankings research page / bottleneck-vs-control-point axes

section_title: How a rank earns its keep
body_text: A ranking framework is only as good as its validation record, and rank #1 provides the template. When drafted, 'lasers are the top bottleneck' was a research assertion. Then the evidence arrived in layers: both leading laser makers independently confirmed the shortage in the same reporting quarter - fully-allocated fabs, one under-shipping demand by 25-30%, all EML capacity spoken for through 2027, an analyst cross-checking on the call whether the industry was still short with 40% capacity additions coming (answer: yes). Then the demand side voted with capital: NVIDIA invested $2 billion in each laser maker. The rank graduated from framework-asserted to operationally validated - assertion, supplier confirmation, cross-validation, revealed-preference capital, in that order. That ladder is the standard every other rank on the table should be held to, and most haven't fully climbed it yet. A ranking that names its own validation ladder can be trusted about what it hasn't proven; that's the point of publishing the ladder.
qualifier: the validation evidence is primary; the ladder framing is the method
vault_ref: Rankings research page / rank-1 validation case

section_title: The quarter, and the re-drawn map
body_text: NVIDIA's quarter ending April 2026: total revenue of $82 billion, up 85% year-over-year and 20% sequentially - the fourteenth straight quarter of sequential growth, with the $13.5 billion sequential increase itself a record. Data Center revenue was $75 billion (up 92%), of which networking was $15 billion, nearly tripled year-over-year. Free cash flow was a record $49 billion. Guidance for the following quarter: $91 billion, plus or minus 2%. The company also re-drew its own reporting map into two market platforms: Data Center - split into Hyperscale ($38 billion, up 12% sequentially) and 'ACIE' for AI clouds, industrial and enterprise ($37 billion, up 31% sequentially) - plus Edge Computing ($6.4 billion). The detail worth noticing: the faster-growing half is ACIE, the diverse non-hyperscaler world of AI clouds, enterprises, and sovereign programs. The formal SEC filing still reports under the older segment structure; the new framework lives in the earnings presentation and investor materials.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2027 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: NVDA company page / Q1 FY2027 financial snapshot + segmentation

section_title: Three customers, 54% - the number stated plainly
body_text: The concentration disclosure in NVIDIA's quarterly filing moved sharply in one year. Three direct customers represented 21%, 17%, and 16% of total revenue in the April 2026 quarter - 54% combined - versus two customers at 16% and 14% (30% combined) a year earlier. The filing separately estimates that one unnamed 'AI research and deployment company' contributed a meaningful amount of revenue indirectly, by purchasing cloud services from NVIDIA's direct customers - widely assumed to be a major AI lab, but not disclosed. Geography concentrated too: only 22% of revenue came from customers headquartered outside the United States, down from 42% a year earlier - a halving, driven by China export controls and the weight of US-domiciled hyperscalers. The revenue is enormous and the customer list is narrowing at the same time; both facts sit in the same filing.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2027 quarterly filing's concentration disclosures
vault_ref: NVDA company page / customer + geographic concentration

section_title: Vera: the CPU market entry, in four use modes
body_text: NVIDIA entered the standalone CPU market with Vera, described by management as 'the world's first CPU purpose built for agentic AI' - built on custom Arm cores, with claimed advantages over x86 alternatives of 1.5x performance per core, 2x performance per watt, and 4x density per rack. Management's own framing: a brand-new $200 billion addressable market and visibility to nearly $20 billion of total CPU revenue this year - a management framing, not yet a reported result. The CEO's economic argument, quoted from the call: 'CPUs of the past were designed to have many cores, so that it could be easily rentable. People rented cores. Well, agents don't rent cores. They just want the work to be done fast.' The economics, in his telling, shift from dollars per core to tokens per dollar. Vera ships four ways: paired with Rubin GPUs (one Vera per two Rubins), standalone, with a storage stack, and with a confidential-computing stack - and the $20 billion figure covers the standalone mode only. The CEO's supply read, hedged in his own words: 'my sense is that we'll be supply constrained throughout the entire life of Vera Rubin.'
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2027 call; forward figures are management's framing
vault_ref: NVDA company page / Vera CPU competitive entry

section_title: Who gets named, and who never does
body_text: A consistent pattern runs through NVIDIA's own disclosure venues: customers get named, suppliers do not. The most recent earnings call named Microsoft's Fairwater data center (hundreds of thousands of GPUs), AWS (adding over 1 million Blackwell and Rubin GPUs starting this year), Google's A5X instances (supporting up to 960,000 Rubin GPUs), Anthropic as a strategic partner, and eleven frontier labs in one enumeration. Meanwhile the foundry that manufactures everything was not named - it has been named exactly once, at a conference keynote, never on an earnings call - and the two laser makers that received $2 billion equity investments each in March 2026 went unmentioned on the very next call. CPU competitors appear only as 'x86-based alternatives.' The same asymmetry applies to topics: co-packaged optics received extensive treatment at the conference keynote and zero mentions across consecutive earnings calls. Reading this company means noting which venue a fact is allowed to appear in - and which facts never appear at all.
qualifier: disclosure-pattern reading across the company's own primary venues
vault_ref: NVDA company page / reciprocal non-naming + cross-venue pattern

section_title: China: licensed, and still zero
body_text: NVIDIA's China position, in its own words: the US government has approved licenses for H200 shipments to China-based customers, but 'we have yet to generate any revenue, and we are uncertain whether any imports will be allowed into the country.' The consequence is stark - zero China data center compute revenue is assumed in guidance, for the second consecutive quarter. The related write-down picture is improving: the H20 excess-inventory charge shrank to $1.1 billion in the April quarter from $5.3 billion a year earlier. Management also noted, on an earlier call, that Chinese competitors 'bolstered by recent IPOs, are making progress and have the potential to disrupt the structure of the global AI industry over the long-term.' A company of this scale plans around a zero from what was once a major market, and says so plainly - whether those imports are ever allowed is an open question it does not control.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2027 call and quarterly filing; the outlook exclusion is explicit
vault_ref: NVDA company page / China exposure

section_title: The quarter, and the full-year picture
body_text: TSMC's first quarter of 2026: revenue of $35.9 billion, up 6.4% sequentially and slightly above its own guidance; gross margin of 66.2%, up 3.9 percentage points sequentially and 120 basis points above the high end of the range it had set three months earlier; operating margin of 58.1%; and a 40.5% return on equity. For the second quarter it guided revenue of $39.0 to $40.2 billion - about a 10% sequential increase, or 32% year-over-year at the midpoint - with gross margin of 65.5% to 67.5%. It raised its full-year 2026 outlook to revenue growth above 30% in US dollars. For the prior full year, 2025, revenue was $121.4 billion, up 31.6%, at a 59.9% gross margin - management is explicit that the 66.2% quarterly figure is a peak-quarter rate, not a sustained annual level, and its long-term target is 56% and higher through the cycle, with a high-20s return on equity.
qualifier: from the earnings call and the annual filing
vault_ref: TSM company page / Q1 2026 results + FY2025 financial snapshot

section_title: Customer A, B, and C - the names it never says
body_text: TSMC's annual filing discloses its largest customers only as Customer A, Customer B, and Customer C - it names none of them. In 2025, Customer A rose to 19% of net revenue from 12% a year earlier, its revenue more than doubling in a single year, and it passed Customer B to become the single largest customer. Customer B's share slipped to 17% from 22%, even though its revenue still grew in absolute terms. The top ten customers together were 78% of net revenue, up from 76% in 2024 and 70% in 2023 - a customer base that is concentrating, not broadening. The filing also states that TSMC is not party to any material contract beyond the ordinary course of business, meaning no single customer supply agreement - even one representing 19% of revenue - rises to the threshold that would require disclosure. The revenue is large and the customer list is narrowing at the same time; both facts sit in the same filing.
qualifier: from the annual filing
vault_ref: TSM company page / customer concentration disclosure

section_title: Supply is tight, and there are no shortcuts
body_text: Demand continues to outstrip supply across both front-end wafers and advanced packaging, and management expects it to stay tight through at least 2027 - on its own framing, it takes two to three years to build a new fab and another one to two years to ramp it, so 'there are no shortcuts.' Advanced packaging - the step that assembles large AI chips, including the largest-reticle CoWoS in the market - is described as 'very tight'; a next-generation panel-based approach, CoPoS, is at pilot-line stage now with production expected a couple of years later. Non-wafer work (packaging, testing, and mask-making together) was 14% of net revenue in 2025. To meet demand, TSMC is raising 2026 capital spending toward the high end of a $52 to $56 billion range; for context, its total across the prior three years combined was $101 billion, so 2026 alone is over half of that, and it expects the next three years to run 'significantly higher' still. In allocating scarce capacity, management says it does not 'pick-and-choose or play favorites' among customers.
qualifier: from the earnings call and the annual filing
vault_ref: TSM company page / capacity, advanced packaging, and capex

section_title: The single point of failure upstream of the foundry
body_text: There is a single point of failure upstream of TSMC's own position: every leading-edge chip it makes depends on extreme-ultraviolet (EUV) lithography tools that are available from only one equipment supplier in the world. TSMC is by far the largest user of those tools - an outside reconstruction, not a company disclosure, put it at roughly 56% of the global installed base as of 2023. This makes TSMC's manufacturing a compound bottleneck rather than a single one: a frontier AI chip can be held up either because the logic die cannot be produced without those EUV tools, or because advanced-packaging capacity cannot assemble the finished part fast enough. The two constraints run in parallel, not in sequence - both have to scale together for total output to grow. On its calls, management characterizes the equipment relationship simply as a partnership: 'we're always working with our supplier because we view them as partners.'
qualifier: independent analysis; the installed-base figure is an outside reconstruction, not a company disclosure
vault_ref: TSM company page / EUV upstream dependency + compound bottleneck

section_title: Pricing power framed as restraint
body_text: TSMC earns a gross margin above 66% and holds an effectively singular position in leading-edge manufacturing, yet management describes its pricing not as leverage but as partnership: 'we always view our customers as our partners... we don't change our pricing dramatically... our customers got to be successful... we grow together.' The open question is what that restraint means. It could be strategic - not fully pressing a dominant position, to keep customers from funding alternatives - or it could reflect genuine competitive caution. This stays unresolved, with implications for how far margins could run over time. When an analyst pressed on whether its current profitability fully reflects the value it creates, management deflected to the partnership framing rather than answering the pricing-power question directly.
qualifier: an open question - not resolved by the filings or the call
vault_ref: TSM company page / pricing power open question

section_title: The quarter, and the AI ramp
body_text: Broadcom's quarter ended May 3, 2026: total revenue of $22.2 billion, up 48% year over year, with the semiconductor segment at $15.0 billion (68% of revenue, up 79%) and infrastructure software at $7.2 billion (up 9%). AI semiconductor revenue was a record $10.8 billion, up 143%, and 49% of total revenue; AI networking was almost 40% of that AI revenue - a level management called a 'stars align' peak it expects to settle back toward 30%. Bookings for AI semiconductors were more than $30 billion in the quarter against $10.8 billion shipped, extending visibility to 2028, and free cash flow was a record $10.3 billion (46% of revenue). Guidance for the next quarter: total revenue of $29.4 billion (up 84%), semiconductor revenue of $20.5 billion, and AI semiconductor revenue of $16 billion (up over 200%). The finance chief flagged consolidated gross margin stepping down to about 74% as AI grows as a share of the mix, adding that this 'does not represent a structural change' but reflects product mix - and recommended modeling the semiconductor and software segments separately.
qualifier: from the Q2 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: AVGO company page / Q2 FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: Six customers, named on the call and not in the filings
body_text: Broadcom has six custom-accelerator customers, corrected from five during the call. Named on the call with gigawatt figures: Google (an April long-term agreement for multiple generations of TPUs and networking, 'very substantial dollars,' with Broadcom accepting there will be 'some diversity of sources'); Anthropic (access to more than 1 gigawatt of TPU-based compute in 2026, plus an April agreement for another 5 gigawatts of next-generation TPU-based compute from 2027); OpenAI (silicon delivered, production on track for late 2026, a contractual 1.3 gigawatts in 2027 within a larger 10-gigawatt-by-2029 deal); and Meta (multiple generations of MTIA accelerators, 3 gigawatts through end-2028, an initial 1-gigawatt order received for delivery in the second half of 2027). Two further customers are not named; shipments begin late 2026 and $6 billion of purchase orders have been received. Neither the annual nor the quarterly filing names any customer - both describe them only as 'hyperscalers, companies with AI frontier models and system integrators.'
qualifier: from the Q2 FY2026 earnings call; the filings keep customers anonymous
vault_ref: AVGO company page / custom AI accelerator franchise

section_title: The $100 billion claim, and the gigawatt math
body_text: For 2027 Broadcom reiterates AI semiconductor revenue 'in excess of $100 billion,' and is careful to scope it to silicon content only - XPUs, switch chips, DSPs - explicitly excluding racks. It expects to ship about 10 gigawatts of compute in 2027, back-half loaded, and 'a lot more gigawatts' in 2028. Content per gigawatt 'varies, sometimes quite dramatically' across customers; management describes dollars per gigawatt as 'relatively stable' even as each chip's price rises and the number of chips per gigawatt falls. It declines to re-guide the 2027 figure every quarter: 'We're not trying to guide you every quarter what 2027 would be like... We basically say it continues to be in excess of $100 billion.' Supply for these components is described as secured for 2026 and 2027, with 2028 and 2029 'being worked on right now.'
qualifier: from the earnings calls; a management forward framing
vault_ref: AVGO company page / 2027 AI revenue outlook

section_title: Ninety-five percent, one foundry, never named
body_text: About 95% of the wafers Broadcom's contract manufacturers produce come from a single foundry - the most explicit foundry-dependency disclosure the company makes - and its wafer requirements 'represent a meaningful portion' of that foundry's total capacity. There are no long-term capacity commitments; substantially all manufacturing is on a purchase-order basis with no minimum quantities. The filing adds that the foundry 'has raised, and may in the future raise' its prices. Broadcom does not name the foundry on its earnings calls, referring only to 'leading-edge wafers' and 'T-glass.' Separately, its Breinigsville, Pennsylvania site is described as the sole source for the indium-phosphide wafers used in its fiber-optic products, which it fabricates internally.
qualifier: from the 10-K and 10-Q
vault_ref: AVGO company page / supply chain dependencies

section_title: The $29 billion backstop, and 'it's all chips'
body_text: Broadcom's quarterly filing discloses that it 'arranged for an investor partner to take on certain agreements to purchase AI racks based on custom AI accelerators designed by us and the related lease agreements with a customer,' and entered a backstop for that customer's lease obligations over five-year terms - a maximum exposure of $29 billion that grows as racks deploy and shrinks as the customer pays; on default, remedies include assuming the lease or selling the racks. On the call, management described creating an AI XPU platform with Apollo and Blackstone and other investors to deploy more than 20 gigawatts of compute through 2028, with a first tranche valued at $35 billion being launched by Apollo, for frontier labs 'including Anthropic and OpenAI.' The structure keeps rack economics off Broadcom's own income statement - 'No rack. It's all chips. We're in the chip business only' - while holding the customer's credit as a contingent exposure. The filing does not name the backstopped customer.
qualifier: from the 10-Q and the Q2 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: AVGO company page / AI-rack lease backstop

section_title: The quarter, and the earnings gap
body_text: Marvell's quarter ended May 2, 2026 set a record: revenue of $2,417.8 million, up 28% year over year and 9% sequentially, with data center 76% of the total. GAAP gross margin was 52.1% and non-GAAP gross margin 58.9%; operating cash flow was a record $639.0 million. The headline gap was between GAAP and non-GAAP results, driven by purchase accounting from the Celestial AI and Xconn acquisitions: a $331.8 million fair-value remeasurement of the Celestial earn-out plus about $225.2 million of acquired-intangible amortization, both non-cash. It was the sixth consecutive quarter of positive GAAP net income, and management expects GAAP earnings to normalize the following quarter. For the full prior fiscal year, revenue was $8,194.6 million, up 42%, with data center at 74%. To hold its supply, the company plans about $1 billion of supplier prepayments this fiscal year, beginning in the second quarter.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2027 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: MRVL company page / Q1 FY2027 financial snapshot

section_title: Custom silicon and the $10 billion target
body_text: Marvell's custom-silicon business scaled from zero to about $1.5 billion of revenue in fiscal 2026, doubling year over year, with more than 20 design wins in or entering production. Management guides custom revenue to grow more than 20% in fiscal 2027 and to more than double in fiscal 2028, and holds a target of over $10 billion in fiscal 2029 - framed as roughly a 20% share of an approximately $55 billion custom market, which is a target and share assumption rather than booked revenue. The growth rests on three legs: a lead accelerator program moving to its next generation, more than ten attach programs (custom network cards and memory-expansion chips) ramping, and a new tier-1 accelerator program entering volume production. Management is candid on margin mix - the custom accelerator itself is lower-margin, while the attach products around it are more margin-rich. The work depends on leading-edge manufacturing and advanced packaging; ship-to revenue into Taiwan rose roughly tenfold in two years, to about 20% of revenue, which the company cautions reflects shipment destination, not end-customer location.
qualifier: from the Q4 FY2026 and Q1 FY2027 earnings calls and the annual filing
vault_ref: MRVL company page / custom silicon business + geographic proxy

section_title: Celestial and the scale-up optics bet
body_text: Marvell closed its acquisition of Celestial AI on February 2, 2026 for total purchase consideration of $3.5 billion, with earn-outs that could raise the total to about $5.5 billion through fiscal 2029. The technology - Photonic Fabric, a chiplet co-packaged into custom accelerators and their switches - has been selected by one hyperscaler for its next-generation accelerator scale-up networks, with the first-generation chiplet moving into high-volume manufacturing with TSMC on its CoWoS packaging. On the prior quarter's call, management set run-rate targets of $500 million annualized by late fiscal 2028 and $1 billion by late fiscal 2029; this quarter it reframed scale-up optics next fiscal year to more than double the earlier roughly $150 million Celestial-only outlook. Reaching $500 million annualized from roughly zero in about two years is an extremely steep assumption, and the earn-out's quarterly remeasurement - $331.8 million this quarter - swings reported GAAP earnings. Management splits the opportunity three ways: co-packaged optics for scale-out is relatively limited, scale-up is where it sees the technology inflecting, and scale-across spans multiple data centers.
qualifier: from the Q4 FY2026 and Q1 FY2027 earnings calls and the quarterly filing
vault_ref: MRVL company page / Celestial AI + CPO positioning

section_title: One channel at 45%
body_text: Marvell's customer concentration is the highest it has carried and still rising. In the quarter ended May 2, 2026, one distributor channel reached 45% of revenue - the company's single largest channel - up from 36% a year earlier, while distributors in aggregate were 51% of revenue. One direct customer held at 16%, flat year over year. At the prior fiscal year end, four customers accounted for 73% of gross accounts receivable. The company does not name the customer or the distributor. Separately, two customer warrants that vest against revenue milestones - covering 4.2 million and 1.0 million shares - are both recognized as reductions to revenue, which points to at least two major custom programs carrying equity-participation terms.
qualifier: from the annual and quarterly filings
vault_ref: MRVL company page / customer concentration

section_title: NVIDIA: partner and competitor
body_text: In March 2026, NVIDIA bought $2.0 billion of newly issued convertible preferred stock in Marvell - 2.0 million shares - convertible at the holder's option into common stock, a maximum of about 21.8 million shares, subject to regulatory clearance. Alongside the investment, Marvell announced a three-part partnership: extending its long-standing supply of optical DSPs, transimpedance amplifiers and drivers into a silicon-photonics collaboration; NVLink Fusion integration, which lets hyperscalers mix and match custom and merchant silicon with Marvell positioned as the bridge between the two; and AI-RAN, pairing Marvell's base-station processors with NVIDIA GPUs. The relationship is genuinely two-sided: Marvell's own annual filing names NVIDIA as a direct custom-silicon competitor, and the two overlap at the lead accelerator customer. The broader partnership scope is disclosed on Marvell's side; full confirmation against NVIDIA's own reporting is still pending. This reverses Marvell's earlier practice of not naming NVIDIA on its calls.
qualifier: the $2.0 billion preferred is from the quarterly filing; the partnership scope is disclosed on Marvell's side
vault_ref: MRVL company page / NVIDIA partnership

section_title: The quarter, and the inflection
body_text: AMD's first quarter of 2026 (period ended March 28, 2026): total revenue of $10.3 billion, up 38% year-over-year, with growth across every segment. Data center revenue was a record $5.8 billion, up 57%, and is now the primary driver of the company's revenue and earnings. Total operating income was $1.48 billion, up 83%; GAAP net income was $1.38 billion, up 95%. Gross margin was 55%, and free cash flow set a record at $2.6 billion, more than tripling. For context, full-year 2025 revenue was $34.6 billion, up 34%, with the data center segment at $16.6 billion, or 48% of the total. Management framed the quarter as a clear inflection and a structural shift in the business.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: AMD company page / Q1 2026 + FY2025 financial snapshot

section_title: OpenAI and Meta: 12 gigawatts, and the equity behind it
body_text: AMD has two multi-year strategic partnerships that anchor its AI accelerator roadmap. Under the OpenAI agreement (October 2025), OpenAI is to deploy 6 gigawatts of AMD GPUs, with the first gigawatt powered by Instinct MI450 series products. Under the Meta agreement (February 2026), AMD is supplying a custom GPU accelerator based on its MI450 architecture, co-designed for Meta's next-generation workloads, also up to 6 gigawatts, with the first gigawatt paired with 6th Gen EPYC Venice CPUs. Each partner received a warrant for up to 160 million AMD shares at a $0.01 exercise price, vesting in tranches tied to Instinct GPU purchase milestones. Combined, that is up to 320 million shares of potential dilution - roughly 20% of the current share count - a material capital-structure feature by which AMD commits substantial future equity to secure the commitments.
qualifier: from the 10-K and Q1 2026 quarterly filing
vault_ref: AMD company page / OpenAI + Meta deployment warrants

section_title: EPYC records, and why agentic AI needs more CPU
body_text: AMD's server CPU business (EPYC) posted a fourth consecutive quarter of record revenue in the first quarter of 2026, up more than 50% year-over-year, with sales to both cloud and enterprise customers each growing more than 50%. The growth was led by 5th generation EPYC processors. AMD's framing is that as AI adoption scales, demand rises not only for accelerators but also for the high-performance CPUs that power and orchestrate those workloads - the orchestration, data movement, and head-node compute around inferencing and agentic AI. The next generation, 6th Gen EPYC Venice, is designed to extend that position across cloud, enterprise, and AI workloads and pairs with Instinct MI450 in the Helios rack-scale platform.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: AMD company page / EPYC server CPU + agentic demand

section_title: ZT Systems: acquire, divest, keep the design
body_text: In 2025 AMD ran an unusual two-stage transaction. In March 2025 it acquired ZT Systems - a data center systems manufacturing and design business - for $3.2 billion in cash plus 8.3 million AMD shares. In October 2025 it divested the manufacturing arm (the ZT Manufacturing Business) to Sanmina for $2.4 billion in cash, plus 1.2 million Sanmina shares (about $154 million at close) and up to a $450 million contingent earnout, receiving $1.4 billion in cash net. AMD retained the ZT design business - the IP and design employees - to help deliver end-to-end AI systems, while Sanmina became its preferred partner for manufacturing complex AI solutions. The structure keeps AMD a fabless designer while adding rack- and system-level design capability.
qualifier: from the 10-K
vault_ref: AMD company page / ZT Systems acquire-and-divest

section_title: The MI308 China charge
body_text: AMD took about $440 million in net charges during 2025 tied to U.S. government export controls on its Instinct MI308 data center GPU: roughly $800 million of inventory and related charges in the second quarter of 2025, with about $360 million reversed in the fourth quarter after some export licenses were granted. Whether and how much China data center revenue continues from here depends on the evolving license framework - an open item the company is still working through.
qualifier: from the 10-K
vault_ref: AMD company page / MI308 China export controls

section_title: The quarter, both sides of it
body_text: Intel's quarter ending March 2026: revenue of $13.6 billion, up 7% year-over-year. On a non-GAAP basis, gross margin was 41% (roughly 650 basis points ahead of guidance) - the sixth consecutive quarter of results above the company's own guidance - with the AI-driven businesses now 60% of revenue, up 40% year-over-year. On a GAAP basis, though, the same quarter was an operating loss of $(3.1) billion and a net loss of $(4.28) billion, driven by $4.07 billion of restructuring and other charges (about 26 times the $156 million a year earlier). Operating cash flow was $1.1 billion and gross capital spending was $5.0 billion. Guidance for the following quarter: revenue of $13.8-$14.8 billion, non-GAAP gross margin of 39% - the margin stepping down on a larger contribution from the still-early Intel 18A ramp. Both pictures, the beat and the loss, sit in the same quarter.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: INTC company page / Q1 FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: The foundry gap, owned
body_text: Intel Foundry is the manufacturing side of the business, and it loses money. For full-year 2025 it had revenue of $17.8 billion but an operating loss of $(10.3) billion (an operating margin of about -58%); in the March 2026 quarter the operating loss was $(2.4) billion, an improvement of $72 million from the prior quarter. Almost all of that revenue is internal - external foundry revenue was just $174 million in the quarter, up more than fivefold year-over-year but small in absolute terms. Intel 18A, which the company says introduces gate-all-around transistors (RibbonFET) and backside power delivery (PowerVia), is in high-volume production ramp, and the foundry carries the bulk of the early-ramp cost. Management expects the operating loss to improve through the year as 18A ramps into volume and yields improve further, and separately describes the foundry build-out as a long journey. The improvement is real and the loss is still large - both at once.
qualifier: from the 10-K and Q1 FY2026 filing
vault_ref: INTC company page / Intel Foundry segment

section_title: The condition under which we might stop chasing the leading edge
body_text: Intel 14A is the first Intel process node designed from the start as an offering to outside foundry customers, and Intel's own annual report attaches an unusual condition to it. In its risk factors, the company states that if it is unable to secure a significant external customer and meet important customer milestones for Intel 14A, it faces the prospect that developing and manufacturing 14A and successor leading-edge nodes will not be economical on a go-forward basis. In that event, it says, it may pause or discontinue its pursuit of 14A and successor nodes and various manufacturing-expansion projects, and would expect over time to shift manufacturing to third-party foundries, particularly TSMC. Against that, management says 14A yield and performance are outpacing 18A at a similar point in time, and it expects earlier design commitments to emerge beginning in the second half of 2026 and expanding into the first half of 2027. The company is spelling out, in its own filing, the condition under which it might stop chasing the leading edge.
qualifier: from the 10-K risk factors and earnings call
vault_ref: INTC company page / Intel 14A exit-risk

section_title: Partner here, competitor elsewhere
body_text: Intel disclosed a strategic partnership with NVIDIA to co-develop custom client and data center products that combine Intel's x86 CPU technologies with NVIDIA's AI and accelerated-computing capabilities - a collaboration Intel says is intended to span multiple generations of products for hyperscale, enterprise, and consumer markets. Separately, Intel states that its Xeon 6 was selected as the host CPU for NVIDIA's DGX Rubin NVL8 systems, and that Xeon remains the most deployed host CPU, which it attributes to its memory, security, and networking orchestration. These are Intel's own statements about its own products and its own partnership. It is worth holding both sides in view: Intel is a partner to that company here and, through its own AI accelerators, a competitor elsewhere.
qualifier: from the earnings call and 10-K
vault_ref: INTC company page / NVIDIA partnership
