section_title: The U.S. government is a shareholder now
body_text: Under an amendment to its CHIPS Act agreement with the U.S. Department of Commerce, Intel received the full accelerated disbursement of the remaining $5.7 billion of that funding - and in exchange it issued the government equity in the company. Intel issued the Department of Commerce 275 million shares of common stock, plus a warrant to purchase up to 241 million additional shares; that warrant becomes exercisable only if Intel were to cease to directly or indirectly own at least 51% of its Intel Foundry business. It also issued 159 million shares into escrow tied to the Secure Enclave program for the Department of Defense, of which 3 million had been released as of the end of December 2025. The structure gives the U.S. government a direct stake in Intel and ties part of it to Intel keeping control of its own foundry.
qualifier: from the 10-K
vault_ref: INTC company page / CHIPS Act and U.S. government stake

section_title: The toll on every chip
body_text: Arm licenses CPU architecture and reference designs rather than shipping the chips itself, and it earns a per-chip royalty recognized when a customer ships a chip containing Arm IP - a toll on nearly every chip built on its platform. In fiscal 2026 that model produced $4.92 billion of revenue, up 23%: royalty revenue of $2.61 billion (up 21%) and licensing of $2.31 billion (up 25%), the third consecutive year above 20% growth since the 2023 public listing. Royalty growth comes from higher per-chip rates as customers adopt the newer Armv9 architecture and Arm's Compute Subsystem reference designs. By Arm's own account, about 50% of CPU compute among the top hyperscalers now runs on Arm, and data center royalty more than doubled year over year - a figure management characterizes rather than breaks out line by line.
qualifier: from the earnings call and shareholder letter
vault_ref: ARM company page / royalty + licensing model

section_title: Arm's first chip of its own
body_text: For most of its history Arm licensed IP rather than making silicon. That is changing: the Arm AGI CPU, announced in March 2026, is Arm's first datacenter chip of its own design. Committed customer demand more than doubled within about six weeks of launch, from $1 billion to over $2 billion across fiscal 2027 and 2028. Even so, management deliberately held its first-revenue outlook flat at roughly $90 million for the fourth quarter of fiscal 2027 while it secures supply - in the CFO's words, it is 'maintaining our outlook of $1 billion while we pursue supply chain capacity.' The constraint spans memory, wafers, packaging, and test equipment. Management frames the chip and the IP business as two growth vectors that run alongside each other rather than cannibalize, and points to a longer-term trajectory of $15 billion of AGI CPU revenue plus $10 billion of IP revenue by fiscal 2031 - a management framing, not a booked result.
qualifier: from the earnings call; forward figures are management's framing
vault_ref: ARM company page / Arm AGI CPU trajectory

section_title: The customer it doesn't control, and the owner that controls it
body_text: Two uncomfortable facts sit in Arm's own filings. First, its single largest customer, Arm China, was about 16% of total revenue in fiscal 2026 - and it is a related party whose operations neither Arm nor SoftBank controls; Arm China sublicenses Arm's IP to customers in China. That share has been declining as data center royalty grows, from 24% in fiscal 2023 to 21%, 17%, and now 16%. Arm's three largest customers together were 42% of revenue (16%, 14%, and 12%). Second, SoftBank beneficially owned about 86.4% of Arm as of May 21, 2026, which makes Arm a 'controlled company' exempt from several board-independence requirements. Aggregate related-party revenue - Arm China plus SoftBank - rose to 30% of total revenue in fiscal 2026.
qualifier: from the annual report
vault_ref: ARM company page / customer concentration + controlled-company status

section_title: Mobile pays the bills; the data center is the growth
body_text: Arm's royalty base is still anchored in mobile even as the data center becomes the growth story. Mobile applications processors were about 43% of royalty revenue in fiscal 2026, down from about 46% a year earlier as data center royalty grows - Arm says it has held greater than 99% share of mobile applications processors for many years. In the near term, smartphone unit growth is expected to be flat to slightly negative, concentrated at the low end, which management expects to be more than offset by cloud AI demand. In data center networking chips - DPUs and SmartNICs - management describes Arm's share as close to 100%, though that is a management characterization the annual filing does not break out separately.
qualifier: from the earnings call and annual report
vault_ref: ARM company page / royalty end-market mix

section_title: Licensor, litigant, and now rival
body_text: Arm and Qualcomm are entangled as licensor, litigant, and rival. Arm sued Qualcomm and the chip-design startup Nuvia in 2022 after Qualcomm acquired Nuvia, arguing the Nuvia license could not be transferred without Arm's consent; Arm is the plaintiff and did not acquire Nuvia. A jury found for Qualcomm in December 2024, and on September 30, 2025 the district court entered final judgment in Qualcomm's favor - Qualcomm's Nuvia-derived CPU cores are covered under its existing Arm license, and Arm's remaining claims were dismissed. Arm filed a notice of appeal on October 1, 2025, so the matter is now pending on appeal and its outcome is unresolved. Separately, Arm's own first-party datacenter chip now competes with Qualcomm's re-entry into datacenter CPUs.
qualifier: still pending on appeal
vault_ref: ARM company page / Qualcomm-Nuvia litigation

section_title: The quarter that beat its own record guide
body_text: Micron's fiscal third quarter of 2026 (ended May 28, 2026) came in far above its own record guidance: revenue of $41,456M, up 74% sequentially and 346% year-over-year, roughly 24% above the guided $33.5 billion plus or minus $750 million. GAAP gross margin rose to 84.6% from 74.4% the prior quarter, on higher DRAM and NAND selling prices and favorable mix. GAAP operating income was $33,318M, an 80.4% operating margin; net income was $28,243M. By business unit, the Cloud Memory unit was $13,769M (33% of revenue), Core Data Center $11,524M (28%), Mobile and Client $11,521M (28%), and Automotive and Embedded $4,634M (11%). The four-segment structure was unchanged from the prior quarter, so the growth is like-for-like rather than a reclassification.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: MU company page / Q3 FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: The take-or-pay agreements, both halves
body_text: Micron has signed 16 Strategic Customer Agreements that cannot be canceled, structured as take-or-pay: the customer owes price times volume whether or not it draws the bits. Outside automotive they run five years, with annual volume commitments. The largest carry a price band - a ceiling near the market price in the second calendar quarter of 2026 and a floor that holds through the term, re-negotiated each quarter within the band; a minority carry no fixed price. Across the 16, total committed cash and financial commitments exceed $22 billion, of which the cash alone is almost $18 billion, provided upfront as deposits, returned on a back-end-weighted schedule and drawn down if a customer does not take its volume. Micron expects these agreements, even at floor pricing, to yield gross margins above its peak quarterly margins in any past cycle, and intends to grow coverage from 20% of DRAM bits and a third of NAND bits toward roughly half of revenue. The honest counterweight: only about $422 million is recognized as contract liabilities so far, against roughly $5 billion of remaining performance obligations - real but early in revenue terms, and the counterparties are not named.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: MU company page / Strategic Customer Agreements

section_title: Supply, not demand, now sets the pace
body_text: Micron's framing is that for the foreseeable future bit-shipment growth is determined by supply, not demand - in its words, 'the demand is so much above the industry's ability to supply' that supply growth sets how much ships. HBM demand is booked well above supply through 2026 and into 2027 and 2028, and the company now expects the HBM market to cross $100 billion in 2027, pulled forward from a prior view of 2028. It states plainly that it does not have a high-confidence view of when supply will catch up with demand and will not project one. The constraint is structural: cleanroom limits, long construction lead times, a higher HBM trade ratio (more silicon per bit), and declining bits-per-wafer gains from node transitions all bound how fast bits can grow. New greenfield fabs such as Idaho One and the acquired Tongluo site take time to ramp and do not contribute meaningful bits until around calendar 2028.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: MU company page / supply-demand framing

section_title: Record cash, a de-levered balance sheet, disciplined spend
body_text: Micron's cash generation stepped up sharply. Total debt fell from $14,577M at the end of August 2025 to $5,722M at the end of May 2026 - about $8.9 billion paid down in nine months - while cash and short-term investments stood at roughly $26 billion. Nine-month operating cash flow was $45,702M and nine-month capital expenditure was $19,602M, leaving about $26.1 billion of nine-month free cash flow; the company says the last two quarters generated as much cash as its entire history. It raised FY2026 capex to about $27 billion, still dominated by DRAM and HBM, and flagged a larger FY2027 step-up with more than half of the increase going to construction that will not produce bits until later. The dividend was raised 30%, and management intends to increase capital return - principally through share repurchase - starting after December 9, 2026, the second anniversary of its CHIPS agreement, while staying, in its words, 'extremely disciplined as we always are.'
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: MU company page / cash flow and capital return

section_title: Has memory stopped being cyclical?
body_text: Micron's own record is the argument on both sides. This is a business that lost $(5,833)M in fiscal 2023, earned $778M in fiscal 2024, and then earned $8,539M in fiscal 2025 - a swing that is the textbook shape of a cyclical memory maker. The open question the recent quarter sharpens is whether that has changed. The case that it has now rests on filing-grade support for the first time: the multi-year, take-or-pay agreements whose floor-pricing gross margins are expected to run above any past cycle's peak, and management's framing of memory as a strategic asset in the AI era. The case that it has not: the agreement model is still early in revenue terms, and management continues to refuse to project prices or long-term bit-growth beyond near-term commentary - the caution of a company that has lived through the down-cycles. The question is live and unsettled.
qualifier: a tracked open question
vault_ref: MU company page / cyclical-vs-structural open question

section_title: The record quarter, and the leverage marched down
body_text: Coherent's quarter ended March 2026 was a record $1.8 billion of revenue, up 7% sequentially and 21% year-over-year, or 27% year-over-year on a pro forma basis that excludes divested businesses. The datacenter and communications segment was $1.35 billion, 75% of the company, up more than 40% year-over-year. Non-GAAP gross margin was 39.6% (up 57 basis points sequentially, 105 basis points year-over-year), non-GAAP operating margin 20.3%. Capital spending rose to $290 million from $154 million the prior quarter, and $162 million of debt was repaid, cutting the leverage ratio to 0.5 times from 1.7 times the prior quarter and 2.1 times a year earlier. The industrial segment declined modestly. Backlog reached a record on 'another step function increase' in the order book, with orders now reaching into calendar 2028 and customer long-term agreements extending to the end of the decade. Guidance for the June quarter: revenue of $1.91 billion to $2.05 billion, non-GAAP gross margin of 39% to 41%.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: COHR company page / Q3 FY2026 financial snapshot + backlog

section_title: The NVIDIA tie: relationship, not lifeline
body_text: In March 2026, NVIDIA made a $2 billion equity investment in Coherent alongside a multi-year supply agreement running through the end of the decade - a multibillion-dollar commercial commitment that is structurally separate from the equity, covering multiple co-packaged-optics products including Coherent's high-power CW laser. Management framed the deal as an expansion of a more than 20-year relationship rather than a capital rescue: the company's cash balance rose from $1.5 billion to $3.0 billion in the same quarter, primarily from the investment, even as it paid down debt to about half a turn of leverage. What Coherent says it supplies into a co-packaged-optics solution goes well beyond the laser - the external laser source module, the fiber array unit with its micro-lens arrays and polarization-maintaining fiber, plus isolators and thermoelectric coolers - and management argues that owning all those ingredients is a strength because 'we're not dependent on others for those technologies.' NVIDIA is described as the lead co-packaged-optics customer, with other customers expected to follow. The specific volume and pricing terms of the supply agreement were not disclosed.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: COHR company page / NVDA investment relationship

section_title: Six-inch indium phosphide: the wafer economics
body_text: Coherent's core manufacturing argument is the shift to six-inch indium-phosphide wafers: management says a six-inch wafer yields more than four times as many devices at less than half the cost of the standard three-inch wafer, and that six-inch yields exceed three-inch across all three device types - EMLs, CW lasers, and photodiodes. It shipped its first transceivers with six-inch parts in the March quarter, expects to hit its goal of doubling internal capacity a quarter early, and to double again by the end of 2027 - a quadrupling over two years. Production runs at Sherman, Texas and Jarfalla, Sweden, with a third site in Zurich starting around early 2027. The CEO's 'world's only producer of six-inch indium phosphide' claim should be read carefully: multiple firms supply six-inch substrate, so the defensible claim is a narrower but real multi-site device-fabrication lead, not a substrate monopoly - and the cost and yield figures are management's own, not independently verified. Even so, management does not expect the industry supply-demand imbalance to resolve this year or next: 'it seems like every quarter we think we're gonna catch up, and then the demand keeps increasing.'
qualifier: from the Q2 and Q3 FY2026 earnings calls; cost and yield figures are management's own
vault_ref: COHR company page / InP manufacturing and the six-inch advantage

section_title: Co-packaged optics: the big, still-early opportunity
body_text: Coherent calls co-packaged optics one of its most important long-term opportunities, sizing it at more than $15 billion of incremental addressable market - which management calls 'probably a conservative estimate.' It disclosed 'an exceptionally large purchase order from a market-leading AI data center customer' for a solution built around its new high-power CW laser, and says a key factor in winning it was that the laser is produced on its six-inch line in Sherman, Texas. The deployment phasing matters: initial scale-out co-packaged-optics revenue is expected to begin in the second half of calendar 2026, with scale-up revenue - optics inside the rack, where the network is electrical today - beginning in the second half of calendar 2027. Management's view that the scale-up opportunity will 'dwarf' scale-out and be 'orders of magnitude larger' is its own forward framing, not a booked result. Coherent is pursuing co-packaged optics with both indium-phosphide and VCSEL-based approaches, and says it is engaged with multiple customers beyond its lead one.
qualifier: from the Q2 and Q3 FY2026 earnings calls; forward sizing is management's framing
vault_ref: COHR company page / CPO positioning and trajectory

section_title: Portfolio cleanup and a simplified balance sheet
body_text: Coherent has been rationalizing hard and deleveraging alongside its datacenter ramp. It sold or exited 33 sites in roughly six quarters, including its aerospace-and-defense business (for about $400 million, a $115 million gain) and its Munich materials-processing division, which had contributed about $25 million of quarterly revenue at a gross margin well below the corporate level. During the December quarter it fully converted the Bain Capital Series B convertible preferred stock into common shares - generating 30.1 million new shares but eliminating roughly $2.5 billion of mezzanine equity and about $130 million of annual preferred dividends, and simplifying the capital structure ahead of the March 2026 NVIDIA investment. Debt leverage fell to 0.5 times by the March quarter. One honest wrinkle in the annual numbers: in fiscal 2025 the company reported $49 million of net earnings, but after $130 million of preferred dividends that became an $81 million net loss available to common shareholders.
qualifier: from the FY2025 annual filing and the Q2-Q3 FY2026 filings and calls
vault_ref: COHR company page / capital structure + portfolio optimization

section_title: The record quarter, and the guide
body_text: Lumentum's quarter ending March 2026 set a company revenue record of $808.4 million, up 90% year over year and 22% sequentially. Components revenue was $533.3 million (up 77% year over year) and systems revenue was $275.1 million (up 121%), driven by the transceiver business and laser chips. Non-GAAP gross margin was 47.9%, up 540 basis points sequentially, and non-GAAP operating margin was 32.2%, up 700 basis points sequentially. Guidance for the following quarter is another record: revenue of $960 million to $1.01 billion, a midpoint of $985 million, with non-GAAP operating margin of 35% to 36%. Management's longer-term goal, set at an industry conference, is a $2 billion quarterly revenue run-rate - the record quarter was roughly 40% of that target. The CFO framed the results as 'demonstrating the leverage of our business model.'
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: LITE company page / Q3 FY2026 financial snapshot + Q4 guidance

section_title: Why the $2 billion mattered
body_text: Lumentum's free cash flow in the first half of fiscal 2026 was approximately $24.8 million - operating cash flow of about $184.6 million against roughly $159.8 million of capex - so capex was consuming nearly all of the company's operating cash flow, with capex annualizing near $320 million. Against that backdrop, NVIDIA made a direct equity investment: the CFO stated that cash and short-term investments increased by $2.02 billion to $3.17 billion, 'with the increase primarily driven by NVIDIA's direct investment in Lumentum.' Management's own framing, from the prior quarter, was that it was in 'active negotiations with leading customers to offset our capital requirements in exchange for long-term supply assurances.' It is pursuing similar long-term agreements with other customers, which management says can entail prepayment, take-or-pay commitments, or price increases - customers that commit are allocated supply first. The perspective is a capital constraint being solved, not a relationship being formalized.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing; investment framing is management's own
vault_ref: LITE company page / NVDA investment relationship + capex trajectory

section_title: Sold out, and under-shipping
body_text: Lumentum describes its indium phosphide wafer fabs as fully allocated, and says it is under-shipping customer demand by more than 30% - an imbalance management characterizes as up from a 25-30% gap the prior quarter, adding that it is 'having to make choices as to who we support.' All EML laser-chip capacity is spoken for through calendar 2027 via long-term agreements, and management says it is in active discussions to lock in pumped-laser supply the same way. The company front-loaded a roughly 40% capacity expansion and is adding a fifth fab, but its own framing is that it 'simply can't service' all the demand placed on it and could ship more than it has. Management judges the risk of oversupply to be low, reasoning that customers would be more reluctant to sign long-term agreements if they expected a glut.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: LITE company page / capacity constraints

section_title: A short customer list, mostly unnamed
body_text: Lumentum relies on a small number of customers for a large share of sales, and expects that concentration to continue. It moved to a single reportable segment in the first quarter of fiscal 2026 - eliminating its prior Cloud & Networking and Industrial Tech split - which reduced public visibility into datacenter-versus-industrial revenue trends, occurring as its cloud and networking business exceeded 85% of revenue. Its largest customer grew from 16% of annual revenue in fiscal 2025 to 24% of quarterly revenue by the December 2025 quarter, and the top two customers were more than 40% of revenue combined; the filings name no customer. On the March 2026 call, management named Google as one of its largest customers, saying Google 'is driving a lot of demand on our business,' and NVIDIA is now both an investor and a customer. Separately, Huawei - historically its largest networking customer in China - was lost entirely in early 2024 under export-control restrictions.
qualifier: from the FY2025 10-K, Q2 FY2026 quarterly filing, and Q3 FY2026 call
vault_ref: LITE company page / customer concentration

section_title: CPO, the ELS module, and a fifth fab
body_text: Co-packaged optics is Lumentum's forward story, and it is hedged. Management describes its 400 milliwatt ultra-high-power lasers as 'not something that many people can do,' holds a multi-hundred-million-dollar UHP order with material shipments inflecting in the second half of 2026, and expects its first scale-up CPO shipments in late calendar 2027. Rather than selling only bare laser chips, it aims to supply an external-light-source (ELS) module carrying roughly 2 to 2.5 times the content, which management calls 'a more convincing and shorter path to market than just supplying lasers.' In mid-March 2026 it announced a fifth indium phosphide fab in Greensboro, North Carolina, converting the site from gallium arsenide; management says the fab is 'not in our numbers,' will not come online until about 2028 - roughly six quarters from meaningful contribution - and, 'if we execute properly,' could add 'greater than $5 billion of incremental revenue' from the scale-up CPO opportunity. These are forward projections, not reported results.
qualifier: from the Q2 and Q3 FY2026 earnings calls; forward figures are management's framing
vault_ref: LITE company page / CPO positioning + Greensboro fifth InP fab

section_title: The record quarter, and how it earns
body_text: Fabrinet's March 2026 quarter set records: total revenue of $1.214 billion, up 39% from a year earlier and 7% from the prior quarter, with net income of $135 million, the highest in the company's history. Optical Communications revenue was $889 million (up 35%), led by a record $628 million in telecom and a 90% year-over-year jump in data center interconnect modules to $197 million. Non-Optical Communications reached $326 million (up 52%), driven by high-performance compute at $107 million. Gross margin was 12.1% - up 10 basis points from a year earlier but down 30 from the prior quarter on foreign-exchange headwinds - while operating margin was 10.7% as operating expenses fell to 1.4% of revenue. Guidance for the following quarter is revenue of $1.25 billion to $1.29 billion, about 40% growth at the midpoint, with gross-margin dynamics expected to look similar.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: FN company page / Q3 FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: Datacom: supply, not demand
body_text: Datacom revenue was $260 million in the quarter - up 4% from a year earlier but down 6% sequentially - and management is careful to frame the decline as supply, not demand: shipments and revenue were well below demand levels because of broadening component and material supply shortages, described as 'not demand risk; it is supply constraints.' The shortages span lasers, a shortage of memory, and certain ASICs - not any single part. A second source for the datacom laser (the EML that had been the main bottleneck) was approved by the customer during the prior quarter. Even so, management expects the supply-demand imbalance to persist into the following quarter, while remaining optimistic that supply conditions improve over time. Underlying datacom demand, in management's telling, remains exceptionally strong.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: FN company page / datacom supply constraint + EML second source

section_title: A pure contract manufacturer, by design
body_text: Fabrinet is a pure optical contract manufacturer: it makes other companies' designs and has no products of its own. Management treats that as the point, not a limitation - 'we don't have any of our own products; that's actually a positive for many of our customers; they don't want us to have our own products' - because it never competes with the companies it builds for. Its own annual filing names its competitors - Benchmark Electronics, Celestica, InnoLight (Suzhou), Jabil, Sanmina, and Venture - and notes that some customers also have in-house manufacturing capability. There is no monopoly position here; the company differentiates on customer relationships, capacity, and execution rather than owning a scarce technology. It describes itself as an advanced optical packaging and precision manufacturing-services provider.
qualifier: from the annual filing and earnings call
vault_ref: FN company page / pure contract manufacturer positioning + competitors

section_title: Co-packaged optics: the enabler, not the contestant
body_text: Fabrinet positions itself as an enabler in co-packaged optics (CPO) rather than a platform contestant - it manufactures CPO products for whichever design wins. It says it is working on CPO programs with three different customers (names not disclosed) and is 'far ahead of most of our competitors' in making the technology real, though the revenue so far is small and 'largely in front of us.' In April 2026 it took a $32 million minority stake (about 14%) in Raytec Semiconductor, a Taiwan-based wafer-level packaging provider, to extend its packaging and integration capabilities for CPO. Separately, it has begun shipping two datacom transceiver programs directly to a hyperscale customer - a shift from its historical build-for-OEMs model - with volumes expected to ramp through the following fiscal year, and it is also engaged on optical circuit switch (OCS) projects it calls incremental. Management frames all of it as manufacturing someone else's design, not building a product of its own.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: FN company page / CPO + OCS enabler positioning + Raytec investment

section_title: Narrow customer base, narrow footprint
body_text: Fabrinet's customers and its factories are both concentrated, and it is deliberately uneven about naming the first. In the first nine months of fiscal 2026, three customers were each more than 10% of revenue, together about 48.9% - up from two customers (about 50.5%) a year earlier - but the company names them only once a year in its annual filing, keeping its largest customers unnamed on quarterly calls and referring instead to 'our main customer.' Geographically, about 89% of long-lived assets sit in Thailand, which the filings flag as a single-jurisdiction risk. It builds capacity ahead of demand rather than waiting for firm commitments: current footprint supports roughly $4.8 billion of annual revenue, rising toward about $8.5 billion as a new 2-million-square-foot building (Building 10) and a smaller acquired campus (Navanakorn, an eight-acre site bought for about $11 million) come online through 2026 and into early 2027. Both the customer base and the footprint are narrow, and the company says so plainly.
qualifier: from the annual and quarterly filings and the earnings call
vault_ref: FN company page / customer concentration + Thailand geography + capacity expansion

section_title: The fourth record quarter, and the raised guide
body_text: AOI's first quarter of 2026 was its fourth consecutive quarter of record revenue: $151.1 million, up 51% year-over-year and 13% sequentially. Data center revenue was $81.4 million, up 154% year-over-year - the clearest sign of AI-datacenter demand in the business - and made up about 54% of revenue, with cable-TV equipment most of the rest. On the same call management raised full-year 2026 guidance from over $1.0 billion to over $1.1 billion in revenue, and lifted its non-GAAP operating income target from over $120 million to over $140 million. Management is explicit that this level is capped by its own production capacity and supply chain, not by customer demand, which it believes is much larger. The company was still loss-making in the quarter, on its stated path toward non-GAAP profitability.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: AAOI company page / Q1 FY2026 financial snapshot + guidance

section_title: Concentration: the most extreme number it discloses
body_text: AOI's customer concentration is the most extreme it reports. In 2025 just two customers were 81.9% of revenue: a cable-TV distributor at 53.1% of revenue - and 86.5% of accounts receivable - and Microsoft at 28.8%, under a five-year supply agreement. Amazon was about 7% and holds a warrant for up to roughly 7.9 million shares that vests over ten years, conditioned on $4 billion of cumulative purchases; about 2.5% of revenue from warrant customers is recognized as contra revenue. Oracle, above 10% the prior year, fell below that line in 2025. The top ten customers together were 96.6% of revenue. At this concentration, losing any single one of these relationships would be a near-existential event.
qualifier: from the 2025 annual report
vault_ref: AAOI company page / customer concentration

section_title: What its own 10-K admits about its products
body_text: AOI's annual report states something companies rarely put in writing: that its products are 'only minimally differentiated from those of our competitors,' and that 'many of our competitors are larger than we are and have significantly greater financial, marketing and other resources.' It names ten competitors, several of them larger, better-capitalized, and more diversified. The one structural edge it points to is that it fabricates its own indium-phosphide and gallium-arsenide laser chips in-house in Sugar Land, Texas - a capability it calls 'unique in our industry' - and it plans to roughly triple its in-house laser production by the middle of 2027. That laser capability is today built into its own modules rather than offered as a standalone product.
qualifier: from the 2025 annual report
vault_ref: AAOI company page / competitive position + laser vertical integration

section_title: How the growth gets funded: dilution
body_text: AOI cannot fund its growth from its own operations, and it says so in its numbers. In 2025 its operating cash outflow was about $174 million and capital spending about $210 million - a total cash need it covered mainly by issuing roughly $519 million of stock, which raised its share count about 52% in a single year. It also carries $125 million of 2.75% convertible notes due 2030. The stock issuance continued into 2026, with the share count up about 5% in the first quarter alone. The company remained loss-making even as revenue grew 83%, so the dilution is, for now, the price of building capacity fast. Whether the issuance slows depends on it reaching the profitability it has guided to.
qualifier: from the 2025 annual report and Q1 2026 filing
vault_ref: AAOI company page / capital structure and dilution

section_title: Co-packaged optics: the risk it doesn't name
body_text: Almost all of AOI's revenue comes from pluggable optical transceivers - the exact form factor that co-packaged optics is designed to replace. That makes co-packaged optics its most material structural threat, yet its filings do not mention co-packaged optics, silicon photonics, or linear-drive optics at all. On its earnings calls management discusses co-packaged optics only in the other direction - as a future source of demand for its in-house high-power lasers - and not as a threat to the transceiver business that generates nearly all its revenue today. When it last reported, none of the analysts on the call asked about displacement risk either. Whether, and how quickly, co-packaged optics erodes the pluggable-transceiver market is unresolved.
qualifier: the displacement question is unresolved
vault_ref: AAOI company page / CPO displacement analysis

section_title: The quarter, and the twice-raised outlook
body_text: Arista Networks' first quarter of 2026, ending March 31, 2026: revenue of $2.71 billion, up 35.1% year over year and above the company's own $2.6 billion guidance. Operating cash flow was about $1.69 billion, described by the company as the strongest in its history. Non-GAAP gross margin was 62.4%, within the guided 62-63% range and down from 63.4% the prior quarter. Management raised the full-year 2026 revenue outlook to roughly $11.5 billion, about 27.7% growth - the second consecutive quarterly raise, up from an original $10.5 billion. It also raised the AI networking target to $3.5 billion for the year, more than doubling AI sales from roughly $1.5 billion in 2025, while holding the campus goal at $1.25 billion. The company cautioned that the timing of customer projects with acceptance terms can create quarterly swings that do not follow prior-year patterns.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: ANET company page / Q1 2026 results and raised guidance

section_title: Two customers, named at last: Microsoft and Meta
body_text: Arista has had two customers each greater than 10% of revenue in each of the last three years. For 2025 those two were 16% and 26% of revenue, about 42% combined - and the mix has been shifting, with one customer easing from 21% to 16% over three years while the other rose from 18% to 26%. On its first-quarter 2026 call the company named the two directly for the first time: Microsoft and Meta, each a greater-than-10% customer for over a decade. Which of the two is the 16% versus the 26% was not specified. Management says it still expects to add one, maybe two more 10% customers in 2026, but caveats that the demand is there while shipments - not demand - are the gating question. By segment in 2025, cloud and AI titans were 48% of revenue, enterprise and financials 32%, and AI and specialty providers 20%.
qualifier: from the earnings call and the 10-K
vault_ref: ANET company page / customer concentration

section_title: Demand the best in a career, supply the opposite tale
body_text: Arista describes its demand as the best management has seen in its tenure, and supply as 'the opposite tale.' It reports industry-wide shortages across wafers, silicon chips, CPUs, optics and memory, with elevated costs to procure them, and now frames the squeeze as a one-to-two-year phenomenon rather than a one-to-two-quarter one. Lead times of about 52 weeks are described as reliable, with reservations needed beyond that, which is pushing the company into multi-year forecasts and purchase commitments - $8.9 billion at the end of the first quarter of 2026, up from $6.8 billion a quarter earlier and $4.8 billion the quarter before that. Management says it will do 'everything, including hurt our gross margins' to supply the demand this year and next, rather than leave customers' GPUs idle. It says it has raised prices only modestly so far, unlike some competitors, and is still absorbing much of the cost. The company also paused share repurchases in the quarter, buying back none after repurchasing stock the prior quarter.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: ANET company page / multi-year supply constraint

section_title: The one supplier it doesn't name
body_text: In its 2025 annual report, Arista lists as a risk that it is 'primarily reliant upon a predominant merchant silicon vendor' - a single supplier for the core switching chips its systems are built around - and it does not name that vendor in the filing. This sits alongside related risks the company flags: sole or limited sources for key components, extended lead times, and supply shortages. Arista is fabless, so it designs the switch and the EOS software but depends on outside silicon. The concentration on one predominant merchant vendor is a structural dependency the company discloses plainly, even as it does not identify who it is.
qualifier: from the 10-K risk factors
vault_ref: ANET company page / merchant silicon dependency

section_title: XPO, co-packaged optics, and the 2027 scale-up entry
body_text: Arista frames its three AI fabric use cases as scale-up (within a rack), scale-out (across racks) and scale-across (across data centers). It says scale-up will be a new entry for Arista in 2027 and beyond, conceding that today's scale-up is mostly NVLink from NVIDIA plus some PCIe switching, and that most Ethernet scale-up will only really happen in 2027 and 2028. At the Optical Fiber Communication Conference the company unveiled a pluggable-optics form factor it calls XPO - 12.8 terabits per module, 204.8 terabits per OCP rack unit, an integrated cold plate cooling up to 400 watts per module, and endorsement by more than 100 vendors. On co-packaged optics, management is dismissive for now: 'these are still science experiments and they're very proprietary,' saying Arista will 'embrace open CPO few years from now' but expects XPO to have a 10-year run at the higher speeds where liquid cooling is required. It positions XPO as a partner to the existing OSFP connector rather than a replacement at 400 and 800 gigabit.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: ANET company page / XPO and scale-up positioning

section_title: The AI slice, in proportion
body_text: Corning is a diversified materials-science company, and its AI exposure is concentrated in one segment, Optical Communications. In the first quarter of 2026, Optical sales were $1.8 billion, up 36% year over year, with segment net income of $387 million, up 93%. For the full year 2025 the segment was about $6.3 billion, up 35% year over year, with net income near $1 billion and a net income margin of about 16.7% - roughly 38% of total segment revenue. The rest of the company remains glass and glass-ceramics, automotive, life sciences and solar. Read plainly, AI is a real and fast-growing driver of one segment, not the whole business.
qualifier: from the earnings call and annual filing
vault_ref: GLW company page / Optical Communications financial snapshot

section_title: The Meta agreement, and two more like it
body_text: Corning announced a multi-year agreement of up to $6 billion with Meta to support Meta's apps, technologies and AI ambitions using its optical fiber, cable and connectivity products, with Meta as the anchor customer for a North Carolina expansion. On the following call the company said it had concluded two more large, long-term agreements with hyperscale customers, each similar in size and duration to the Meta agreement, and was working to conclude others. It declines to name those two customers, describing its philosophy as letting customers decide when and where to announce their own supply-chain decisions. The commercial structure copies the approach proven in display glass and Apple's roughly $2.5 billion Kentucky cover-glass commitment: customer prepayments and stringent long-term commitments so that Corning shares the cost and risk of the capacity expansions. The financial impact is expected to build mainly in 2027 and 2028 as dedicated capacity comes online.
qualifier: from the earnings call
vault_ref: GLW company page / hyperscaler long-term agreements

section_title: Generative-AI demand, named in the filing
body_text: Corning's annual filing attributes optical growth directly to AI: 'the rapid acceleration of artificial intelligence is driving strong demand for fiber and connectivity products inside and between data centers.' It names the generative-AI products behind that demand - SMF-28e Contour fiber, about 40% smaller in diameter for high-density environments, and Contour Flow Cable, which fits roughly double the fiber into the same cable diameter as comparable products. The filing describes these as being adopted by hyperscale data centers as well as by carriers building data-center-interconnect networks, focused on density, latency and sustainability. This is demand attribution stated in the company's own filing, distinct from generic forward-looking language.
qualifier: from the 10-K
vault_ref: GLW company page / Generative AI demand attribution

section_title: Photons inside the box - conviction, and the timing caveat
body_text: On whether the links inside the switch and near the GPU eventually move from electrons to photons - so-called scale-up and co-packaged optics - Corning's stated position is deep conviction on direction paired with open uncertainty on timing. As of the January 2026 call, no significant scale-up revenue was included in its growth plan. Management laid out a range of timing scenarios, from the period before 2028 to primarily late 2028 and beyond, and said that without compelling evidence of timing it would view the timeline conservatively rather than over-speculate. Corning frames itself here as an enabler that supplies fiber and connectivity to whichever platform approach prevails, not as a contestant in the packaging fight.
qualifier: an open question - timing not yet resolved
vault_ref: GLW company page / scale-up CPO positioning

section_title: The names it won't give you
body_text: Corning's most recent annual filing discloses that two combined end customers represented about 28% of Optical Communications segment sales, with no customers named in the filing itself. It names Meta only because Meta chose to make its own announcement; it declines to name technology partners, and the two additional hyperscale customers under long-term agreements remain unnamed pending their own disclosures. Concentration is set to rise as the Meta agreement and the pending agreements mature through 2027 and 2028. What is disclosed is the 28% figure; the specific identities beyond Meta, and whether that 28% already includes Meta's revenue, are not disclosed.
qualifier: from the 10-K
vault_ref: GLW company page / customer concentration

section_title: The quarter: a beat above every range
body_text: Viavi's quarter ending March 2026 came in above the high end of every guidance range it had set. Total revenue was $406.8 million, up 42.8% year-over-year and 10.2% sequentially, versus a guided $386-400 million. Operating margin was 21.0%, above the high end of the 19.2-20.2% range. The network test and monitoring segment was $321.5 million, up 54.4% year-over-year, driven mostly by the data center ecosystem and the acquired Spirent product lines; the optical security segment was $85.3 million, up 11.4%. Guidance for the following quarter is $427-437 million, again sequential growth. Management's own framing is that the company sits early in the ramp rather than at a peak.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: VIAV company page / Q3 FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: The data center became the biggest customer
body_text: The composition of Viavi's network-test revenue shifted hard toward the data center. A year ago the data center ecosystem - high-performance semiconductors, optical modules, network equipment makers, and hyperscalers - was roughly 40% of that segment; management now puts it around 45% and trending higher, with exit velocity 'inching to the high 40%s' and possibly reaching about half of the segment. Fiber field instruments show the same shift: data center customers were single digits of that product line a year ago, about a third by late 2025, and 40-50% by early 2026, trending toward 50%. Management's explanation is that AI data centers monitor every incoming wavelength to hold performance to a tight specification, buying more fiber monitoring than traditional service providers ever did. All of these percentages are management's own, disclosed on the calls rather than broken out in the filings.
qualifier: from the Q2 and Q3 FY2026 earnings calls
vault_ref: VIAV company page / customer segment mix + field-instrument data center mix

section_title: How far ahead it will look: about three quarters, no more
body_text: Viavi's forward visibility was historically short - management says it traditionally had 'maybe one, one and a half quarter' of view. That extended as hyperscalers began vertically integrating into their supply chains through partnerships and multi-quarter commitments; management now describes a good view of base demand up to three quarters ahead and expects data center momentum 'through calendar 2026.' It draws a hard line past that: as a practice it will not forecast beyond the end of the calendar year, and defines 'foreseeable future' as the next three quarters. Whether that extended visibility holds, or compresses back toward one to one-and-a-half quarters in a cyclical reversal, is an open question the company itself flags through its refusal to guide further.
qualifier: from the Q2 and Q3 FY2026 earnings calls
vault_ref: VIAV company page / multi-quarter visibility extension

section_title: Two acquisitions doing the heavy lifting
body_text: Two deals reshaped the company. It acquired Spirent's high-speed Ethernet, network-security, and channel-emulation test lines from Keysight for $425 million, closing October 16, 2025; the first full 13-week quarter contributed $54.2 million, in line with expectations, with about $16 million of synergies expected in fiscal 2026. Separately, it bought Inertial Labs in January 2025 for $121.7 million; that positioning, navigation, and timing business performed strongly enough in calendar 2025 to trigger a $75 million earn-out paid in early 2026. The PNT products go into drones and other autonomous air, land, sea, and undersea systems, and the company says it has begun winning U.S. tier-1 defense customers after bringing export-control and secure-access discipline to the acquired business.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: VIAV company page / Spirent + Inertial Labs acquisitions

section_title: The instrument on the bench, not the part being tested
body_text: Viavi's separator from the optical-component makers is that it measures their work rather than competing with it - it builds the test, monitoring, and assurance gear for optical networks, not the transceivers, lasers, or switch chips. Independent positioning places it as a specialized equipment supplier downstream of the platform decisions, with no architectural authority over how AI compute platforms are designed; it tests whatever architecture the chip and system makers settle on. Management describes co-packaged optics as a strong tailwind that 'plays extremely well' to its traditional strengths, and says packaging and test are moving from a backend afterthought to the system itself, pulling its instruments into everything from optical-component test to wafer-level and heterogeneous-integration packaging. The vantage is real, but it is also the ceiling: the value captured is instrumentation, not platform control. It also runs a quieter optical-security arm - anti-counterfeiting pigments used in banknotes and documents, plus 3D sensing - with different end markets and cyclicality from the network-test business.
qualifier: independent analysis of the company's positioning, with product facts from its filings
vault_ref: VIAV company page / thesis role + co-packaged optics positioning

section_title: The quarter, and the raise
body_text: Full-year 2025 revenue was $852.5 million, up 115%. The first quarter of 2026 came in at $308.4 million, up 14% sequentially and 93% year-over-year - above the prior outlook. Non-GAAP gross margin was 76.4%, up 70 basis points sequentially, driven by a lower mix of hardware sales across the signal conditioning portfolio. Non-GAAP operating expenses were $123.9 million, reflecting a full quarter of the Xscale acquisition and a partial quarter of a newly formed Israel design center. Guidance for the second quarter is revenue of $355 to $365 million, up 15 to 18 percent sequentially, with non-GAAP gross margin of approximately 73% that includes an estimated 200 basis point non-cash impact tied to a recently executed customer warrant. The company ended the quarter with about $1.18 billion of cash and marketable securities and no debt.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: ALAB company page / Q1 FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: One customer, more than 70%
body_text: The annual filing discloses that in 2025 one end customer represented more than 70% of revenue, and the top three end customers were about 86% combined. A separate billing-address view fragments differently: five customers each exceeded 10% of revenue, and the lead billing-address customer rose from 20% for the full year to 29% in the first quarter of 2026 - the largest such shift since the IPO. The filing cautions that billing address reflects distributors and manufacturing partners, not end-customer location, so the two views are not the same. Notably, across the two most recent earnings calls, no analyst asked about the more-than-70% single-customer concentration disclosed in the same-day filing. Management does not volunteer the concentration; it frames the answer as diversifying its business profile through new design wins across multiple customers, which remains a work in progress.
qualifier: from the 10-K risk factors and quarterly filing
vault_ref: ALAB company page / customer concentration

section_title: The Amazon warrant
body_text: In February 2026 the company entered a follow-on warrant agreement with Amazon, filed as an 8-K: 3.3 million warrant shares that vest against the achievement of up to $6.5 billion of revenue milestones, with a seven-year life to 2033, covering smart fabric switches, signal conditioning products, and optical engine solutions. Two prior warrants from October 2022 and October 2023 covered up to roughly 2.3 million shares, of which about 1.17 million had vested by the end of 2025 and contributed $5.5 million of contra-revenue in 2025. As the new warrants vest, the company takes a non-cash charge against revenue that flows directly into gross margin, modeled at about two points a quarter starting in the second quarter. The $6.5 billion vesting threshold implies multi-year, multi-billion-dollar procurement from this customer. The annual filing names the warrant holder as an Amazon investment entity but does not explicitly name Amazon as the more-than-70% end customer, though the structural inference is strong.
qualifier: from the 8-K warrant agreement and earnings call
vault_ref: ALAB company page / Amazon warrant

section_title: From retimers to AI fabric
body_text: Silicon dollar content is climbing past $1,000 per accelerator inside AI racks, driven by the transition to PCIe Gen 6, 800-gig and 1.6T Ethernet, and the shift from retimers to a full AI fabric. Scorpio P-Series is described as the only PCIe 6 fabric shipping in volume in the market, with a portfolio now spanning 32 to 320 lanes. The new flagship Scorpio X-Series 320-lane scale-up switch adds hardware-accelerated Hypercast and in-network compute that management says can boost collective operations by up to 2x by offloading accelerator-to-accelerator communication onto the switch; it began shipping initial volumes and ramps to full production in the second half of 2026. The company reports having shipped millions of PCIe Gen 6 ports, and PCIe Gen 6 across AI fabric and signal conditioning was more than one-third of first-quarter revenue. Management expects Scorpio to become the largest product line by the end of the year, up from about 15% of revenue last year, with X-Series revenue eventually exceeding P-Series.
qualifier: from the earnings calls
vault_ref: ALAB company page / Scorpio and product portfolio

section_title: Copper into optics, in three phases
body_text: The company plans to extend from copper into optics for scale-up in three phases: high-density detachable reusable fiber connectors shipping in volume starting 2027; near-package-optics chipsets to enable multi-rack AI clusters, also in 2027; and eventually fully optically-enabled Scorpio X fabric switches with co-packaged optics, described as the longer-term goal in roughly the 2028 timeframe. The aiXscale Photonics acquisition in November 2025 was $31.1 million in cash for fiber-chip coupling technology - a small tuck-in later renamed Xscale, with effects deemed not material and no photonics revenue disclosed. Management characterizes optical for scale-up as a very large opportunity, probably larger than the signal-conditioning business, while noting it has not been sized precisely. Copper and optical are expected to coexist for a long time, with the path running from pluggable to near-package to co-packaged optics. The filings contain no mention of co-packaged optics or silicon photonics outside the aiXscale acquisition paragraph, so this trajectory is forward-looking.
qualifier: from the earnings calls and 10-K; the timelines are forward-looking
vault_ref: ALAB company page / aiXscale and optical roadmap

section_title: The quarter, in proportion
body_text: Eaton's quarter ended March 2026 was a record: revenue of $7.5 billion, up 17% year-over-year - about 10% organic, the rest from acquisitions and currency. It posted a Q1 record segment operating profit of $1.7 billion and segment margins of 22.7%. Free cash flow rose 245% over the prior year. Total electrical backlog grew 48% year-over-year, and data center orders in Electrical Americas were up about 240%. Combined book-to-bill reached 1.2 on a rolling 12-month basis. On the strength of the quarter, the company raised its full-year organic growth outlook by 200 basis points to a 10% midpoint, even as it absorbs dilution from the Boyd Thermal acquisition.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: ETN company page / Q1 2026 financial snapshot

section_title: A diversified book, not a data center pure-play
body_text: Eaton is a diversified electrical and industrial company, not a data center pure-play. Data center is its dominant near-term growth driver - it made up 54% of the megaproject announcements Eaton tracks - but it is one vertical inside a portfolio that also serves utility, aerospace and defense, commercial and institutional, machine OEM, residential, and vehicle markets. Customer concentration reflects that breadth: 22% of the Electrical segments' sales came from six large customers, and no single customer was disclosed above 10%. Eaton does not name any hyperscaler as a customer; it refers to them generically. On the mix of demand in its own words: in 2025 its data center orders were split roughly 50/50 between cloud and AI, while revenue was still about 70% cloud and 30% AI, with the AI share growing.
qualifier: from the 10-K and earnings call
vault_ref: ETN company page / customer concentration + data center exposure

section_title: Grid-to-chip is a strategy, not a reported line
body_text: Eaton describes itself as having the broadest portfolio in power management solutions for data centers and markets an end-to-end 'grid-to-chip' approach - from utility gear in front of the meter, through gray-space power distribution, into white-space cooling and power near the chip. That is management positioning, backed by acquisitions - Fibrebond modular enclosures, Resilient Power solid-state transformers, Boyd Thermal liquid cooling - not a separately reported result. Eaton's segment financials do not break out how much revenue comes from integrated systems versus individual components. So the size of the grid-to-chip business, as opposed to the strategy behind it, is not something the filings let you measure yet. The portfolio narrative is best read as a direction of travel, not a disclosed revenue line.
qualifier: an open question - integrated-solutions revenue is not separately disclosed
vault_ref: ETN company page / integrated-solutions revenue mix open question

section_title: The backlog runway, on the company's own figures
body_text: Eaton frames a long runway of data center demand, on figures it tracks itself. Total data center backlog has grown to 228 gigawatts - which the company describes as roughly 12 years of backlog at 2025 build rates, up from 11 years a quarter earlier. It estimates 32 gigawatts of US data center capacity under construction, of which about 70% is AI-related. The broader megaproject backlog it tracks is around $3.3 trillion, up 31% year-over-year across more than 866 projects, and megaproject starts - when customers begin spending and buying equipment - reached $54 billion in Q1 2026, more than double the same period a year earlier and, by the company's count, the third-best quarter on record since it began tracking in 2021. These are Eaton's own market-tracking figures, and the 'years of backlog' measure depends on build rates staying at 2025 levels.
qualifier: from the earnings call; market-tracking figures, hedged to 2025 build rates
vault_ref: ETN company page / data center backlog + megaproject tracking

section_title: The capacity ramp and the margin it costs
body_text: To meet the order surge, Eaton is executing a roughly $1.5 billion, ~24-project US capacity expansion - the largest it has undertaken - with 12 facilities completed and ramping by early 2026, more due by year-end, and the rest beyond 2027, targeting full production in 2027. That ramp costs margin now. In Q1 2026, Electrical Americas operating margin was 25.6%, below the company's own expectations, which management attributed to a negative price-cost lag from commodity inflation and to accelerated ramp-up costs incurred ahead of volume. Management calls these headwinds temporary and expects an April 1 price increase and higher utilization to more than offset them, guiding to sequential margin improvement of about 150 basis points from Q1 to Q2 and an Electrical Americas exit margin above 30%. For the full year it lowered its combined Electrical segment margin guide by 50 basis points to 24.1%-24.5% while raising the organic growth outlook - a trade it made deliberately. Whether the ramp executes on that schedule and the margin recovers as planned is still ahead of it.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call; recovery is management's expectation
vault_ref: ETN company page / capacity expansion + Electrical Americas margin

section_title: The quarter, and the raised guide
body_text: GE Vernova's first quarter of 2026: orders of $18.3 billion, up 71% organically, with a book-to-bill of roughly 2; revenue up 7%; adjusted EBITDA of $896 million, up 87%, with margin expanding 390 basis points; and $4.8 billion of free cash flow - more than the full-year 2025 free cash flow of $3.7 billion, helped by down payments on higher orders. Total backlog reached $163 billion, up $13 billion in the quarter and up from $116 billion at the April 2024 spin; the $200 billion backlog target was pulled forward to 2027 from a prior 2028. On the strength of the quarter, full-year 2026 guidance was raised: revenue to $44.5-45.5 billion, adjusted EBITDA margin to 12-14%, and free cash flow to $6.5-7.5 billion, up from $5.0-5.5 billion. The balance sheet ended the quarter at roughly $10.2 billion of cash and below 1x gross debt to adjusted EBITDA. The company frames all of this as a strong start, not a finish.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: GEV company page / Q1 2026 results + raised FY2026 guidance

section_title: The data center orders, disclosed and bounded
body_text: Two data center figures the company disclosed for the first quarter of 2026: roughly 20% of its 100 GW of gas turbines under contract explicitly supports data centers, with the remaining 80% traditional utility and industrial customers; and its Electrification segment booked about $2.4 billion of data center orders in the quarter alone - more than the full year of 2025, a point the CEO repeated for emphasis. What is not disclosed matters as much: no hyperscaler is named as a customer anywhere in the results. Management describes the gas base as 90 distinct customers across 24 countries - 'a need for incremental electrons for many different applications in many different countries' - a diversified, utility-anchored book rather than a hyperscaler-concentrated one. So the data center demand is real and growing, and it arrives through a customer list the company chooses not to name.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: GEV company page / data center order disclosure

section_title: How much of the whole is really data center
body_text: Putting the pieces together, an estimated 9 to 13% of 2026 revenue is data-center-attributable: gas power at roughly 20% of a $7-8 billion segment is about $1.4-1.6 billion, and Electrification's data center orders imply roughly $2.8-4.3 billion of revenue, against total company revenue of $44.5-45.5 billion. On a backlog basis the share is smaller still, an estimated 6 to 9% of the $163 billion. These are estimates built from disclosed segment figures, not numbers the company reports directly. The takeaway is deliberately modest: the data center exposure is real, but it is a minority slice of a company whose center of gravity is still traditional power and grid.
qualifier: estimate built from disclosed segment figures
vault_ref: GEV company page / data-center-attributable revenue estimate

section_title: Where the constraint actually sits
body_text: Asked about capacity, the CEO was direct that his own equipment is not the bottleneck: on a build cycle of roughly three years, 'the gas turbines are really not the gating item' - the EPC construction, the permitting and the fuel availability are. Lead times run directionally around three years, with capacity still open in 2029 and 2030; the company sold a lot of 2030 slots in the quarter because customers, working around EPC schedules, needed the later slot. Capacity is being added anyway - 280 new machines installed in the gas factories over about 15 months, roughly 1,800 U.S. production workers added across 2025-2026, and a target of 20 GW of annualized output by mid-2026 stepping to 24 GW in 2028. The honest picture: strong demand for the turbines, but the thing that paces a project is everything around them, not the machine itself.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: GEV company page / generation-side constraint framing

section_title: The 'string of pearls' - integration, one bead at a time
body_text: The company's data center strategy is to add integrated products from power generation through to inside the data center - what the CEO calls a 'string of pearls,' with customers able to 'attach more LEGO blocks' over time. The sequence is deliberately staged and hedged: an energy management system was booked as first orders in the quarter with a second order in April; a stability block paired with a medium-voltage UPS could see incremental orders in the second half of 2026 'if things go our way'; and a solid-state transformer is due for a first delivery to an unnamed hyperscaler in the fall of 2026, followed by six months of customer testing before any order, likely in the first half of 2027. The company is candid that 'it doesn't come at once.' The pitch is that few companies can span power generation, electrical equipment, and automation software in one integrated offering - but most of that offering is still ahead of it, not yet in the revenue base.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call; the roadmap is forward-looking
vault_ref: GEV company page / Electrification 'string of pearls' roadmap

section_title: Power and thermal, sold as one system
body_text: Vertiv's positioning is that a data center should be looked at as one system, and it supplies both halves of it: power management - busways, low- and medium-voltage switchgear, UPS, rack power distribution - and thermal management - air and liquid cooling, CDUs, and heat rejection, from the chip out. FY2025 net sales were $10.2 billion, split roughly 80% products and 20% services and spares. The company reports revenue only by geography (Americas, Asia Pacific, EMEA); it does not disaggregate by product line, so the split between liquid cooling, air cooling, UPS, and switchgear is not visible from the outside. No single customer crossed the 10% disclosure threshold in FY2025 - the customer base spans hyperscale, colocation, neocloud, enterprise, and communication and industrial buyers. This is a broader footprint than a cooling-only or power-only supplier, and it is spread across manufacturing in more than 40 countries.
qualifier: from the 10-K and the earnings call
vault_ref: VRT company page / integrated systems positioning + reporting

section_title: The backlog that more than doubled
body_text: The single most striking financial signal at Vertiv is the order backlog: $15.0 billion at December 31, 2025, up from $7.2 billion a year earlier - more than doubled, against full-year revenue growth of about 28%. The majority is firm and is expected to ship within a 12-18 month window. Management described the backlog shape as 'a little bit more elongated' than before, not dramatically different, which it says gives good visibility into 2027. A related signal moved the same way: deferred revenue rose to about $2.46 billion at the end of Q1 2026, up roughly 36% in one quarter, reflecting customer prepayments accelerating with strong orders. Management noted it does not disclose order figures, but said it was very pleased with bookings across regions.
qualifier: from the 10-K, the quarterly filing, and the earnings call
vault_ref: VRT company page / backlog and orders

section_title: The NVIDIA partnership, with a named deployment
body_text: Vertiv's annual report describes its partnership with NVIDIA as supporting the development of advanced power and thermal infrastructure aligned with next-generation AI and high-performance computing architectures. On the Q1 2026 call, management put a specific deployment behind that language: EcoDataCenter in Sweden, designed to support demanding AI workloads with NVIDIA's latest-generation Vera Rubin GPUs, where Vertiv OneCore was selected to deliver the full data center solution - power, thermal, IT white space, and services. Notably, Vertiv names its technology partners (NVIDIA, and separately Caterpillar and Oklo on the energy side) and this one deployment customer, but it does not name specific hyperscaler customers, which appear only as illustrative end-market examples. The partnership is characterized as technology development, not an equity or exclusivity arrangement.
qualifier: from the 10-K and the earnings call
vault_ref: VRT company page / NVIDIA partnership + deployment

section_title: 800-volt DC, and why liquid cooling only correlates
body_text: Asked about the shift to 800-volt DC power architecture, management was careful not to overstate it: this is not seen as a wholesale transition, but 800 volt will be 'an important portion of the total market' as the industry moves into 2027 and beyond. The programs are described as on time - portfolio launches in the second half of 2026, with shipping framed as a 2027 event, and customer prototype and validation activities ongoing. On the link to cooling, management drew a deliberate distinction: there will be 'a correlation, not a causation necessarily,' because 800-volt DC is applied for very high-density compute, and that density drives liquid cooling not just for the chip but across a much bigger array of electronics in the whole IT stack, which in turn influences the entire power train and thermal chain. The framing is opportunity, hedged - it is early, and revenue was not yet flowing in the reported period.
qualifier: from the earnings call
vault_ref: VRT company page / 800V DC architecture + liquid cooling

section_title: 'Bring your own power' as more content for one system
body_text: Management framed behind-the-meter, or 'bring your own power,' as here to stay, especially in the U.S., and tied it back to the one-system view of the data center. Vertiv says it plays a role across microgrids, battery energy storage, and making sure the whole power train - grid or on-site - is consistent and designed for a bring-your-own-power solution, anchored by partnerships with Caterpillar and Oklo, and a collaboration with CPower Energy to turn on-site energy assets into grid resources. Management also extended the idea to the thermal side, noting that options like absorption cooling naturally come into play, with more detail promised at the May Investor Day. The honest hedge is that this is characterized as an opportunity precisely because it makes the system more complex - and 'possibly with more content for us' - rather than as a quantified revenue line today.
qualifier: from the earnings call
vault_ref: VRT company page / bring your own power

section_title: The record quarter, and the raised outlook
body_text: Bloom Energy's quarter ended March 2026: revenue of $751.1 million, up 130.4% year-over-year - the first quarter above 100% growth in its history as a public company. Product revenue reached an all-time high of $653.3 million (up about 208%); service revenue was $61.9 million (up 15.6%). Gross margin was 31.5% (up about 280 basis points); operating income was $129.7 million versus $13.2 million a year earlier, an operating margin of 17.3%; adjusted EBITDA was $143 million. Cash flow from operations was a positive $73.6 million - positive for the first time in a first quarter, which is seasonally weaker - and the company ended with $2.52 billion in total cash. Management then raised full-year 2026 guidance to $3.4-$3.8 billion in revenue (about 80% growth at the midpoint, up from about 60%), with non-GAAP gross margin now expected around 34% versus 30% in 2025 - a raise the CEO qualified 'barring any global shock or exogenous factors.'
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: BE company page / Q1 2026 financial snapshot + raised guidance

section_title: The 43% that sits next to the growth
body_text: Behind the record growth is a concentration worth reading plainly. In 2025, three customers accounted for roughly 43%, 13%, and 12% of Bloom's revenue, and the largest - the 43% - is a related party, most likely SK ecoplant. Oracle, despite the headline Project Jupiter deal, was under 12% and did not appear in the top three; it is an emerging hyperscaler customer rather than the main concentration. Management frames the widening customer base as the answer, noting that more than half of current data center backlog already comes from other hyperscalers, neoclouds, and colocation providers. Compared with other power-infrastructure names, a single customer at 43% is unusually high - Bloom stands out for a concentrated top customer, and whether that related-party share falls as hyperscalers ramp is an open question the company itself has not yet answered.
qualifier: from the 10-K
vault_ref: BE company page / customer concentration honest-verdict

section_title: Project Jupiter: 100% Bloom, no grid
body_text: Oracle's Project Jupiter is an up-to-2.45-gigawatt power block for an AI factory in New Mexico, described by Bloom as '100% Bloom' - an islanded microgrid that replaces the previously planned gas turbines and diesel backup generators. In the CEO's framing, these installations use 'no grid, no dirty diesel generators for backup, no battery banks for load following, no engines, no turbines, just Bloom and Bloom alone,' and when complete it would be one of the largest islanded microgrid power facilities in the world. The deal runs under a master services agreement structured so a hyperscaler can move a Bloom deployment from one site to another when one build speeds up or another slips. Bloom also agreed to issue Oracle a warrant to purchase Bloom common stock. The delivery cadence and revenue-recognition timeline were not disclosed.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and the 10-K
vault_ref: BE company page / Oracle Project Jupiter MSA

section_title: Never the bottleneck: the capacity model
body_text: Bloom's stated strategy is to invest ahead of demand so it is never the limiting factor for a customer's build. Its current manufacturing footprint is targeted to deliver 5 gigawatts of product a year, and it has shifted to adding capacity continuously - hundreds of megawatts a quarter, rather than one lumpy addition a year - using what it calls a 'copy-exact' model. Management's claim is that it is now neither order-constrained nor capacity-constrained: the pace of revenue growth is set by how fast customers can build their greenfield sites, not by how fast Bloom can supply them. The CEO contrasts this with the traditional power industry's multi-year backlog, which he casts as a symptom of constrained supply rather than strength. Going beyond 5 gigawatts would require building new factories, which management says it will do as the market needs - the step-function timing depends on where demand goes.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: BE company page / manufacturing scale + capacity expansion

section_title: The financing and utility partners - and what stays unpriced
body_text: Two partnerships sit behind Bloom's growth story, both genuine and both partly undisclosed. In August 2025 Bloom entered a strategic partnership with Brookfield's AI-infrastructure fund to help finance its fuel-cell business; under the framework Bloom generally holds only a passive equity interest in financed projects, Brookfield holds exclusivity over certain project types, and the total capital amount, expected project count, and revenue timing are not disclosed. Separately, an American Electric Power supply agreement from November 2024 covers up to 1 gigawatt of Bloom's solid-oxide fuel cells for high-demand applications including AI data centers, beginning with an initial 100-megawatt order and additional expansion phases in future years; the dollar value and phase timing were not disclosed. Bloom also does not disclose an aggregate backlog figure - its CEO de-emphasizes backlog as a measure - so the size of committed future demand is not quantified in the filings.
qualifier: partnerships disclosed in the 10-K; amounts and timing undisclosed
vault_ref: BE company page / Brookfield framework + AEP supply agreement

section_title: The quarter, owned plainly
body_text: In the quarter ended April 30, 2026, FuelCell Energy reported total revenue of $35.6 million, down about 5% year-over-year, as lower service and generation revenue outweighed higher product sales from module deliveries in Korea. Loss from operations was $77.9 million, up sharply from the prior year - but $42.6 million of that was a single non-cash impairment charge tied to upgrading the company's 7.4-megawatt Groton Navy submarine-base microgrid to current-generation power blocks. On a non-GAAP basis, adjusted EBITDA was negative $17.1 million, a 12% improvement from a year earlier, which management points to as the cleaner read on operating direction once the impairment is set aside. The company ended the quarter with $440.9 million of total cash (about $373.2 million of it unrestricted) and describes itself as essentially debt-free apart from project- and service-level financings, with no near-term debt maturities. Backlog stood at $1.14 billion, down roughly 9%, and is weighted toward company-owned generation projects under long-term power-purchase agreements that average about 15 years remaining.
qualifier: from the Q2 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: FCEL company page / Q2 FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: A pipeline of proposals, not orders
body_text: FuelCell Energy's proposal pipeline reached 4 gigawatts of submitted proposals, with about 89% tied to potential data-center customers and the average proposal size doubling to roughly 130 megawatts in a single quarter - management characterized the growth as a more than 250% increase, though measured against the prior quarter's 1.5-gigawatt figure the increase computes closer to 167%. The important qualifier is what these are: submitted proposals, not firm orders. Nothing enters the company's backlog until contracts are finalized, and as of this quarter there was no firm hyperscaler order disclosed - the Korea data-center relationship with Inuverse remains a memorandum of understanding, and a separate framework with an infrastructure-capital partner is described as 'up to 450 megawatts,' an upper-bound scope rather than committed capacity. Management's stated goal is to convert submitted proposals into contracted backlog within the fiscal year, while cautioning that large infrastructure decisions do not follow a predictable schedule and that bigger deals bring proportionally longer diligence.
qualifier: from the Q2 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: FCEL company page / pipeline vs firm orders

section_title: Molten carbonate - and the carbon-capture twist
body_text: FuelCell Energy builds proprietary molten-carbonate fuel cell systems - a high-temperature, carbonate-electrolyte design distinct from the more common solid-oxide approach. The company has run these at utility scale for years: proven 10-megawatt, 20-megawatt, and 58.8-megawatt installations, each with more than seven years of continuous run time, including what it describes as the largest fuel cell plant in the world at nearly 60 megawatts in South Korea. The chemistry carries an unusual second capability - it can capture carbon directly from an external emission source while simultaneously generating power, hydrogen, and usable heat, which makes it eligible for the federal 45Q carbon-capture credit of $85 per ton alongside a 30% investment tax credit. To prove that out, the company shipped two carbon-capture modules to an ExxonMobil refinery in Rotterdam, with delivery expected in June 2026.
