The systems are fuel-flexible across biofuels, renewable natural gas, and hydrogen-hydrocarbon blends.
qualifier: from the 10-K and the earnings call
vault_ref: FCEL company page / MCFC technology and carbon capture

section_title: A turnaround that funds itself by selling stock
body_text: FuelCell Energy is running a genuine turnaround, and it does not hide the mechanics. Over about seven months it cut its workforce roughly a third - a 13% reduction in November 2024 followed by a 22% reduction in June 2025 - described in its own filing as cost cuts amid slower-than-expected market investment. It funds the ongoing gap largely by selling shares: about 10.9 million shares issued through its at-the-market program during the quarter for roughly $100 million in net proceeds, plus another 4.1 million shares after quarter-end for about $53 million. The company frames its balance sheet as a strength - ample liquidity, essentially debt-free apart from project financings - but the path to positive adjusted EBITDA is explicit and conditional: it does not reach that point until its Torrington, Connecticut factory runs at an annualized production rate of 100 megawatts per year, and production sits below that level today. Separately, management raised its planned Torrington capacity expansion from 350 to 500 megawatts per year, at an estimated cost of $200 million to $275 million, but gated it on contracted backlog - stating it will not build ahead of the market.
qualifier: from the 10-K, the quarterly filing, and the earnings call
vault_ref: FCEL company page / restructuring, dilution, and EBITDA path

section_title: The test it set for itself
body_text: The open question hanging over FuelCell Energy is conversion: whether a large proposal pipeline turns into signed, contracted backlog. Management set its own dated test - converting submitted proposals into contracted backlog within fiscal 2026, which ends October 31, 2026 - making the year-end report the natural pass-or-fail venue. The demand narrative has strengthened quarter over quarter while the proof has lagged: the pipeline grew and average deal size doubled, yet reported revenue fell, the loss widened on the Groton impairment, and no firm hyperscaler order has been disclosed. The capacity-expansion plan is deliberately conditioned on that conversion happening - the company says it will scale Torrington in strict alignment with contracted backlog, not ahead of it. Until proposals become contracts, the gap between the size of the pipeline and the firmness of the orders remains the thing to watch.
qualifier: an open question on backlog conversion
vault_ref: FCEL company page / open questions

section_title: The year: records before the credit, a lower number after it
body_text: EnerSys reported fiscal 2026 (its year ended March 31, 2026) as a record for adjusted results despite a demand recession in its electric-forklift and transportation markets. Full-year sales were an all-time high of about $3.8 billion, up 4% year-on-year. Adjusted operating earnings were $540 million including a $159 million benefit from the Section 45X manufacturing tax credit; excluding that credit, adjusted operating profit was a record $382 million at a record 10.2% adjusted operating margin. Reported (GAAP) net earnings, by contrast, fell about 19% to $293.6 million - driven by a lower Section 45X credit ($158.6 million, down from $184.6 million) and higher restructuring charges ($51.0 million versus $14.4 million). Full-year free cash flow was $468 million. Management bridged the gap between the record adjusted figures and the lower reported number openly on the call.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 annual filing and earnings call
vault_ref: ENS company page / FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: Two data-center products, shipped but not yet revenue
body_text: EnerSys's two roadmap priorities - a lithium data-center UPS battery and a battery energy storage system for warehouse operators - both advanced into customer commissioning in the quarter ended March 2026. Management stressed this means finished product shipped to real customers, not an engineering or 'soft' launch and not an early sample. Even so, meaningful revenue is not expected until fiscal 2028. Two gating steps remain: handoffs with the large UPS makers ('all the large primary providers'), and separate validation processes that the large hyperscalers run on the product themselves. The hyperscalers appear only as validation counterparties - none is named as a customer. Management noted the lithium data-center battery did not exist a year earlier.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: ENS company page / AI datacenter product roadmap

section_title: Lead-acid data-center demand, and where power is the real limit
body_text: Data-center demand for EnerSys's incumbent lead-acid batteries has been strong: data-center orders were up 36% year-on-year, and lead-acid data-center revenue grew high-teens in fiscal 2026, a market where the company describes itself as holding a leading position. Quarterly figures are lumpy - Q4 data-center revenue was flat against a strong prior-year comp - because management calls it a project business that will not be linear quarter to quarter. The company's thin-plate pure lead (TPPL) technology is positioned for the high-rate, short-duration discharges data centers increasingly need (sub-five-minute, and in some cases sub-one-minute rates) that can exceed traditional lead-acid designs. While most greenfield data centers are adopting lithium, management frames its coming data-center lithium battery as incremental 'share of wallet,' not a replacement for the lead-acid business. Management also noted power availability is one of the biggest gating factors for new data centers - which it argues strengthens the case for its storage systems.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: ENS company page / Energy Systems + TPPL datacenter positioning

section_title: Where the lithium cells come from
body_text: For its commercial data-center and warehouse-storage products, EnerSys plans to keep sourcing lithium cells externally rather than making them, running a make-or-source analysis for each application. Management noted those commercial cells still originate in Asian supply chains, in places like China, and that roughly 99% of the constituent raw-material supply chain for lithium iron phosphate is either in or owned by China - so even a cell finished elsewhere traces back there. The company's planned Greenville, South Carolina lithium-cell factory (tied to a $199 million Department of Energy award) has been re-scoped toward customers that value secure, domestic supply chains free of foreign entities of concern - particularly aerospace and defense - using more established, commercially proven cell technology to de-risk the program. Management says its DOE discussions are in the final stages of the grant process, but it will not disclose further facility detail until the award completes.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: ENS company page / lithium sourcing + Greenville plant

section_title: No single customer even 10%
body_text: EnerSys describes a deliberately diversified customer base: no single customer accounts for more than 10% of its revenue, and it serves over 10,000 customers in over 100 countries. Its Energy Systems customers span data centers, telecommunications and broadband, electric utilities, emergency lighting, renewable energy, and industrial markets. This diversification is why - even for its new AI-data-center products - hyperscalers appear in its disclosures only as validation counterparties running their own qualification processes, and no individual hyperscaler is named as a customer. The company frames the data-center and communications markets as less sensitive to tariff policy than its forklift and transportation markets, describing data centers as in a growth cycle driven by AI and increasing digitization.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 annual filing and earnings call
vault_ref: ENS company page / customer concentration + tariff-defensive framing

section_title: Fiscal 2026: a fourth straight record year
body_text: Modine's fiscal 2026 (year ended March 31, 2026) was its fourth consecutive year of record revenue and adjusted EBITDA. Net sales were about $3.2 billion, up 23% year-over-year, driven by the Climate Solutions segment - hyperscale and colocation data-center demand plus roughly $119 million of contribution from three acquisitions. In the fourth quarter, total sales rose 47%, Climate Solutions sales grew 87%, and within it data-center revenue grew $246 million, or 158%. Climate Solutions adjusted EBITDA grew 63% in the quarter. Margins were down year-over-year on higher material costs and new aluminum tariffs, which management expects to recover through pricing and surcharges over a typical three-to-six-month lag. The Performance Technologies segment declined about 3% on weak North American vehicular markets.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 10-K and the Q4 earnings call
vault_ref: MOD company page / FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: The more-than-$4 billion capacity agreement
body_text: Modine disclosed a multi-year agreement under which it expects to supply more than $4 billion of data-center cooling products to a single customer during calendar years 2027 through 2029; the disclosure carries explicit no-assurance language, and the customer is not named. It is a major piece of forward demand visibility and, at the same time, a new customer-concentration vector: top-ten customers rose to 49% of net sales in fiscal 2026 from 43% the year before, and one customer at more than $4 billion over three calendar years materially concentrates the forward data-center book. No single customer exceeded 10% of net sales in fiscal 2026. Whether this customer crosses the 10%-of-net-sales disclosure threshold as the 2027 through 2029 volumes ramp is an open monitoring item.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 10-K and the Q4 earnings call
vault_ref: MOD company page / customer concentration + $4B agreement

section_title: Spinning off Performance Technologies to become a pure-play
body_text: Modine is transforming from a two-segment company - Climate Solutions and Performance Technologies - into a pure-play climate-solutions company. Performance Technologies (industrial heat transfer and automotive/vehicular thermal management) is being spun off and combined with Gentherm in a Reverse Morris Trust; definitive agreements were signed in January 2026, and the milestones of a Gentherm S-4 filing, shareholder approval, and an IRS determination letter on the tax treatment have been completed. The deal is on track to close by the end of calendar 2026, at which point Performance Technologies becomes a discontinued operation. Modine receives about $210 million in cash and its shareholders receive stock equal to 40% of the combined ownership. Beginning in fiscal 2027, Climate Solutions itself splits into two reportable segments - Data Centers and Commercial HVAC - giving the data-center business its own reportable segment for the first time.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 10-K and the Q4 earnings call
vault_ref: MOD company page / Performance Technologies spin-off + Gentherm combination

section_title: What Modine actually makes for data centers
body_text: Modine's data-center cooling line runs across the full range of cooling approaches: precision air conditioning units, computer-room air conditioning and air handler (CRAC/CRAH) units, chillers, condensers and condensing units, hybrid fan coils and fan walls, and liquid-cooling solutions for high-density computing - including coolant distribution units (CDUs) and immersion. A flagship 3 MW chiller delivers a 50% increase in cooling capacity for only a 9% increase in footprint, aimed at higher chip densities that need more cooling within the same space. The portfolio is run on an 80/20 discipline, and the data-center business is in a major capacity expansion with new chiller lines coming online across U.S. plants.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2025 and 2026 10-Ks and the earnings calls
vault_ref: MOD company page / Data Center Cooling product portfolio

section_title: Serving the hyperscalers, without naming them
body_text: Modine's data-center customers include the major hyperscalers - management describes 'actively working with all the hyperscalers, but at different degrees and different levels,' plus some neocloud providers - though most are unnamed, consistent with 'two global technology customers with confidentiality agreements' in its filings. Its broader top-customer list, disclosed alphabetically, spans data-center developer Ascent alongside industrial and vehicular names such as Carrier, Caterpillar, Deere, Stellantis, Trane, Volkswagen and Volvo. That mix leaves Modine diversified across data-center and automotive/industrial end markets, with no single customer above 10% of net sales.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2025 10-K and the Q3 earnings call
vault_ref: MOD company page / customer concentration + multi-hyperscaler engagement

section_title: A record quarter, held to the same margin
body_text: Monolithic Power Systems reported record revenue of $804 million for the March 2026 quarter, up 26% year over year and 7% from the prior quarter. Communications grew 33% sequentially on power solutions for optical modules and switches. Gross margin was 55.5% - flat for a fourth consecutive quarter and at the low end of the company's stated 55%-to-upper-50s% model - and management guided the following quarter to a small incremental margin step-up, tied to better visibility into backlog rather than to any change in the business. The CEO drew a deliberate line against over-promising on margin: he did not want to give 'false hope that we're gonna jump very high... That's not MPS.' For the full year 2025, revenue was $2.79 billion, up 26.4%, with gross margin around 55.2%. Management remained cautious about potential headwinds in the second half of the year even while raising near-term expectations.
qualifier: from the earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: MPWR company page / Q1 FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: Enterprise Data: a named segment, floor raised to 85%
body_text: Monolithic Power Systems breaks out Enterprise Data as its own named reportable segment - defined as cloud-based and on-premises CPU servers and workstations, and AI systems. In FY2025 the segment was $701.8 million, about 25.2% of total revenue, essentially flat in dollars versus $716.3 million a year earlier (down about 2%). The company frames that flatness as a composition effect rather than a decline: five of its six segments grew between 26% and 46% in 2025 (storage and computing up 46%, automotive up 43%, communications up 37%), so Enterprise Data's share of the mix fell even though its dollars held. On the latest call management raised the segment's growth floor to 85% year over year, attributing the confidence to stronger and more extended order patterns already visible in backlog. It declined to split the growth between higher volume and higher content per system, saying the two are hard to separate.
qualifier: from the earnings call and annual filing
vault_ref: MPWR company page / Enterprise Data named reportable segment

section_title: The Enterprise Data snap-back, per outside research
body_text: Independent research reads Monolithic Power Systems' Enterprise Data segment at about $263 million in the March 2026 quarter, up roughly 98% year over year and back to about 32.7% of revenue - above where the mix sat in 2024 and ahead of the company's own stated 85% growth floor. That figure is an outside estimate, not a number the company itself broke out in its filing. Taken together with the raised floor, it points the same direction: the segment that looked flat in 2025 dollars resumed material growth going into 2026. The estimate should be held as an estimate, and the durability of that pace across the rest of the year is not established.
qualifier: independent research estimate, not a company-disclosed figure
vault_ref: MPWR company page / Enterprise Data Q1 2026 actual

section_title: The socket-loss reading, answered flatly
body_text: An industry channel-check reading circulated that Monolithic Power Systems had lost roughly 60-70% of an NVIDIA Blackwell power socket to competitors Infineon and Renesas. The company's own primary record points the other way: its annual and quarterly SEC filings contain no mention of NVIDIA, of Blackwell, or of any socket loss. Asked about it on the call, the CEO answered directly - 'I don't see we lose any socket, okay? The major socket at least. We keep winning. Those business, those design wins will turn into revenues' - and closed the call with 'we winning socket and we're expanding our market shares.' The outside estimate and the company's own account are in direct conflict, and the filings so far side with the company. Whether any socket-loss detail surfaces in a later disclosure is something to watch.
qualifier: channel-check estimate contradicted by the company's filings and call
vault_ref: MPWR company page / socket-loss channel-check vs primary contradiction

section_title: Concentrated at the top, diversified underneath
body_text: Monolithic Power Systems sells largely through distributors, and the customer base is concentrated at the top: in FY2025 three distributors were each more than 10% of revenue - about 26%, 18% and 10%, roughly 54% of revenue from the top three - and receivables were even more concentrated, around 60%. The company does not disclose the distributors' names. Sitting under that concentration is a deliberately diversified end-market base across six segments: storage and computing, enterprise data, automotive, communications, consumer, and industrial. Five of the six grew between 26% and 46% in 2025, and management describes its approach as landing a design, then winning the other sockets available in the same application to widen its served market. The strategy is to win sockets broadly and let customers set the ramp timing, which the company says it cannot control.
qualifier: from the annual filing and earnings call
vault_ref: MPWR company page / customer concentration + segment diversification

section_title: The numbers, and the raised guidance
body_text: BWX Technologies reported FY2025 revenue of roughly $3.2 billion, up about 18%, with Adjusted EBITDA up around 15% and free cash flow up 16%. Year-end backlog was $7.3 billion, up 15% year-over-year (not more) across both segments. The first quarter of 2026 was stronger: revenue of $860 million, up 26% year-over-year with 11% organic growth, Adjusted EBITDA of $148 million (up 14%), and backlog of $8.7 billion - up 77% year-over-year and 19% sequentially, with Government Operations backlog alone near $7 billion (up 93% year-over-year). On the strength of the quarter, FY2026 guidance was raised: revenue of at least $3.75 billion (up high-teens versus 2025), Adjusted EBITDA of $650-665 million, and free cash flow of $315-330 million. Management notes this guidance does not include any contribution from the announced Precision Components Group acquisition, which is expected to close in the second half of the year.
qualifier: from the earnings calls and quarterly filings
vault_ref: BWXT company page / FY2025 + Q1 2026 financial snapshot and raised guidance

section_title: TRISO: the only line running today
body_text: BWXT frames itself, in management's words, as 'the only producer of TRISO at any scale at this point' - producing a few hundred kilograms of the advanced reactor fuel per year, which is about the limit of its current capacity. It delivered the full first core of TRISO fuel for the Department of Defense's Project Pele microreactor to Idaho National Lab in November 2025, and is manufacturing TRISO for Antares, which aims to achieve reactor criticality by July 2026. It holds a collaboration agreement with Kairos on TRISO; Kairos recently began construction of its Hermes 2 reactor for Google in Oak Ridge, Tennessee - the company's first disclosed link to a hyperscaler end-customer, through the reactor developer rather than directly. The competitive line management uses: 'While others are planning to produce and manufacture advanced nuclear fuel, we are delivering today.' To scale beyond the current few-hundred-kilogram limit, it is evaluating a larger plant, publicly discussed for Wyoming - described as 'what the market needs' to drive down TRISO cost, though no build decision is confirmed.
qualifier: from the earnings call
vault_ref: BWXT company page / TRISO sole-commercial-scale position

section_title: The naval franchise, and the concentration under it
body_text: BWXT has been the sole-source supplier of naval nuclear reactor components, reactors, and fuel to the U.S. Navy's Naval Nuclear Propulsion Program for more than 100 years. In the fourth quarter of 2025 it shipped two large steam generators for CVN-81, a Ford-class aircraft carrier, from its Mount Vernon, Indiana facility. The planning base case is a steady cadence of two Virginia-class submarines a year and one Columbia-class a year, with AUKUS-driven capacity providing some added growth in 2026; how much second-source work ultimately follows is, in the CEO's words, 'maybe a little hard to say.' The counterweight to this franchise is concentration: 68% of FY2025 revenue came from the U.S. Government. Naval reactors are sole-source on national-security grounds, so the concentration is structural and durable - and it is still a single-counterparty exposure. The Commercial Operations segment, guided to roughly 30% growth in 2026, is the de-concentration path, but government mix is expected to remain well over half of revenue for the next few years.
qualifier: from the 10-K and earnings calls
vault_ref: BWXT company page / naval reactor sole-source franchise + customer concentration honest-verdict

section_title: Rebuilding U.S. enrichment: Defense Fuels, HALEU, HPDU
body_text: BWXT is a participant in the U.S. effort to rebuild a domestic uranium supply chain. It was one of six awardees under the Department of Energy's October 2024 HALEU deconversion program (a $2.7 billion ceiling, with a $2 million minimum each). Separately, for the National Nuclear Security Administration's Defense Fuels Program - described by the CEO as 'reestablishing a domestic uranium enrichment capability for national security purposes' - it stood up a Centrifuge Manufacturing Development Facility in just seven months and, in April 2026, engaged the Nuclear Regulatory Commission on plans to build a high-enriched-uranium enrichment facility in Erwin, Tennessee, an early step in the approval process. It also won a $1.6 billion contract to expand high-purity depleted uranium production, with a new facility planned for Jonesborough, Tennessee. Management is candid that these newer programs begin at lower initial profit recognition and expand as milestones are met - in the CFO's words, 'we have some lower margin components associated with those initial costs,' with the opportunity to outperform as the programs ramp.
qualifier: from the earnings calls and 10-K
vault_ref: BWXT company page / HALEU deconversion + Defense Fuels + HPDU

section_title: Nuclear for AI: in the windshield
body_text: Asked directly about the AI-datacenter story, BWXT's management declines to lean into it. In the CEO's words: 'there's an expectation that nuclear power will power the data centers of the future, and I think that's a reasonable expectation, but that's all in the windshield for us. Certainly, that's not part of the current business mix.' The company separates an 'outside story' (nuclear powering future data centers, treated as reasonable but distant optionality) from an 'inside story' (using machine learning and factory automation to improve its own manufacturing). This is a notably restrained posture for a nuclear-exposed company: the AI-power demand thesis is acknowledged as plausible, but explicitly not counted as current revenue, and whether it turns into real revenue is left genuinely open.
qualifier: management framing from the earnings call; treated as future optionality, not current revenue
vault_ref: BWXT company page / AI datacenter exposure honest-verdict disclaimer

section_title: Three segments, one fuel cycle - the 2025 results
body_text: Cameco reports across three segments: Uranium, Fuel Services, and Westinghouse. For 2025, consolidated revenue was about CAD $3.5 billion, up 11% year over year. Uranium segment EBITDA rose about 7%; Fuel Services EBITDA rose about 22%, making it the fastest-growing operating segment; and Cameco's share of Westinghouse net earnings swung up by about CAD $276 million for the full year. Consolidated uranium production was 21.0 million pounds, which exceeded the company's revised annual guidance. Port Hope set a production record of 11.2 million kilograms of UF6. The figures are the company's own year-end results.
qualifier: from the year-end results and earnings call
vault_ref: CCJ company page / FY2025 financial snapshot + segments

section_title: Discipline and the 230-million-pound book
body_text: Cameco ended 2025 with roughly 230 million pounds of uranium committed under long-term contracts - a distributed portfolio, with no top-customer or related-party concentration disclosed. Management frames its restraint as intentional strategy: industry-wide long-term contracting in 2025 stayed below replacement-rate levels, so the company deliberately placed limited volume and kept uncommitted pounds in reserve, arguing it does not front-run demand with supply and that undelivered pounds are worth more later than today. The backdrop it cites is a thinning of secondary supply and long lead times for new production, which favor established Western producers. The U.S. Prohibiting Russian Uranium Imports Act, with a full import ban from January 1, 2028, is part of that Western-supply shift. Every one of these framings is the company's own market view, hedged as such - it does not guide beyond the current year or past a final investment decision.
qualifier: from the earnings call and year-end filing
vault_ref: CCJ company page / long-term contract portfolio + Russia ban context

section_title: The Westinghouse stake and the $80B government partnership
body_text: Cameco owns 49% of Westinghouse; Brookfield owns 51%, and the deal closed in November 2023 on an equity-method basis. During 2025 Westinghouse paid Cameco about US$220 million in cash distributions - roughly US$49 million in February, US$171.5 million in October tied to the Dukovany nuclear project, and a further US$49 million in early 2026 - which management notes it does not expect to repeat at that level, since one distribution was a one-time project payment. In October 2025 Cameco, Brookfield, Westinghouse, and the U.S. government announced a strategic partnership backed by at least US$80 billion in planned government investment to accelerate reactor deployment; once vested, the U.S. Government receives a participation interest entitling it to 20% of any cash distributions above US$17.5 billion from Westinghouse. A term sheet was signed in October and a definitive agreement is still being negotiated. Guidance for Cameco's 2026 share of Westinghouse adjusted EBITDA is US$370-430 million, described as strong but lower than 2025, with new-build earnings expected to be lumpy year to year.
qualifier: from the earnings call and year-end filing
vault_ref: CCJ company page / Westinghouse 49% equity + October 2025 partnership

section_title: Tier-one mines, the only Canadian converter, and an enrichment option
body_text: Cameco's uranium comes from Cigar Lake (about 50% owned), the McArthur River joint venture with Orano (about 70/30), and the Inkai joint venture with KazAtomProm in Kazakhstan (40/60). From Inkai in 2025 it took delivery of 3.7 million pounds for its share, plus 900,000 pounds carried over from 2024. McArthur River has a design capacity of 18 million pounds and is licensed to 25, and management notes it has produced at both 18 and 20 before; 2026 output there is guided somewhat below design as the company paces development to demand rather than accelerating. Port Hope is Canada's sole-source UF6 converter and one of only two Western converters alongside Orano's French plant, and it set a UF6 production record in 2025. Cameco also holds a 49% interest in Global Laser Enrichment, where the technology has reached readiness level 6 and is aimed first at re-enriching depleted tails; it is not yet operational, with commercial deployment framed as post-2030.
qualifier: from the earnings call and year-end filing
vault_ref: CCJ company page / uranium mines + Port Hope conversion + GLE option

section_title: The open questions in the fine print
body_text: A few threads around the Westinghouse investment remain unresolved on the record. A research report cited an equity-conversion clause that would apply if Westinghouse were to go public above US$30 billion by 2029, but that clause does not appear in Cameco's own filings or on its earnings call - so it stays unconfirmed rather than denied. The exact 2025 figure for Cameco's share of Westinghouse EBITDA is not separately broken out in the disclosures reviewed. And 2026 Westinghouse guidance is lower than 2025 even though the US$80 billion partnership is meant to accelerate deployment; management attributes the step-down partly to the one-time Dukovany distribution booked in 2025 and to project-timing lumpiness, and does not resolve it further. These are genuine open items, not settled facts.
qualifier: flagged open questions
vault_ref: CCJ company page / open questions

section_title: The 2025 year, and the two segments
body_text: Centrus reported $448.7 million of revenue for 2025, an increase of $6.7 million or 1.5% over 2024. The business runs on two segments: the commercial enrichment (LEU) segment at $346.2 million - roughly 77% of the total and relatively flat versus $349.9 million the prior year - and the government Technical Solutions segment at $102.5 million, about 23%, up 11% on higher HALEU Operations Contract revenue. Total gross profit was $117.5 million and net income $77.8 million. Within the LEU segment, SWU revenue rose 21% on a 23% increase in volume sold while average price slipped about 1%; uranium revenue fell 54% to $55.6 million, largely because of a big one-time uranium sale in late 2024 that did not repeat. Management notes there can be significant quarter-to-quarter variability and points to annual and trailing-twelve-month results as the better read.
qualifier: from the FY2025 earnings call and 10-K
vault_ref: LEU company page / FY2025 financial snapshot + segments

section_title: The HALEU cascade at Piketon
body_text: Centrus runs the only NRC-licensed Western facility producing HALEU, the high-assay low-enriched uranium that advanced reactors need - the AC100M centrifuge cascade at Piketon, Ohio. It completed Phase II of its HALEU Operations Contract by contractually delivering 900 kilograms of HALEU to the Department of Energy in June 2025, and by the first quarter of 2026 had produced over 1.6 metric tons cumulatively for the government (up from over 1 metric ton at the end of 2025). The base case buildout is described as 12 metric tons of HALEU per year, with the base case expected to be sufficient to reach the company's nth-of-a-kind cost; further capacity additions are called progressive and tied to offtake demand and capital resources. The first new cascade of centrifuges is expected to come online in 2029, with subsequent cascades to follow - a first-of-a-kind industrial buildout, in management's framing, with the timeline hedged throughout.
qualifier: from the FY2025 and Q1 2026 earnings calls
vault_ref: LEU company page / HALEU enrichment position

section_title: The federal funding, and the parts still being negotiated
body_text: In January 2026 the Department of Energy selected Centrus for a $900 million HALEU Enrichment Award. Management says it has the potential to exceed $1 billion - up to roughly $1.07 billion with $170 million of additional options - but stresses it still needs to be finalized through negotiations and will be paid against milestones rather than upfront. The company frames the award as another pool of low-cost capital that comes through a procurement and involves neither debt nor equity, supporting the 12-metric-ton HALEU buildout. Separately, in late 2025 the National Nuclear Security Administration notified Centrus of its intent to sole-source certain uranium enrichment activities; as of the first quarter of 2026 the company had submitted its response and was in a procurement cycle, declining to disclose scope or quantities and saying it would let the government drive those announcements.
qualifier: from the FY2025 and Q1 2026 earnings calls; the award terms and NNSA scope are not yet final
vault_ref: LEU company page / DOE award + NNSA sole-source intent

section_title: The backlog, and what 'contingent' means
body_text: At the end of the first quarter of 2026 Centrus reported total backlog of about $3.9 billion, extending through 2040 - $3.1 billion in the LEU segment and $0.8 billion in Technical Solutions. The LEU segment backlog breaks down into roughly $700 million of broker-dealer backlog and about $2.4 billion of contingent LEU enrichment sales that are all under definitive agreement. Management emphasizes the word contingent: these are commitments the company is working to convert to firm, and it declines to discuss the contractual details. The Technical Solutions backlog includes funded amounts, unfunded amounts, and unexercised options tied to the HALEU Operations Contract.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and 10-Q
vault_ref: LEU company page / backlog

section_title: The buildout: raised guidance, partners, and an Oklo deconversion JV
body_text: On commercial and operational progress, Centrus raised its 2026 revenue guidance to $450-500 million from $425-475 million and lifted its Piketon workforce target from over 50 to over 100 net new hires, while reaffirming capital deployment of $350-500 million. In late January 2026 it launched a $560 million investment to expand and accelerate its Oak Ridge centrifuge manufacturing plant, and signed three partners for the buildout: Fluor as primary EPC, Palantir as a strategic partner whose Foundry and AI platform the company credits with roughly $300 million of identified potential cost savings (with more expected), and Geiger Brothers to lead on-the-ground construction in Ohio. Separately, Centrus said it is exploring a joint venture with Oklo focused on HALEU deconversion - a step in the fuel cycle that, in management's words, does not exist commercially today - in which Oklo is described as a potential partner as well as a large off-taker and customer. Management frames the JV as exploratory and says it will update the market when it has more to share.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call; guidance is management's own framing and the Oklo JV is exploratory
vault_ref: LEU company page / FY2026 guidance + buildout partners + Oklo JV

section_title: The quarter: records off a loss-making build-out
body_text: MP Materials' first quarter of 2026: consolidated revenue plus price-protection income of $132.9 million, a record, with total revenue of $90.6 million, up 49% year over year. The net loss narrowed to $8.0 million from $22.6 million in the same quarter a year earlier. Production and sales at Mountain Pass set records - 917 metric tons of NdPr oxide produced, 1,006 metric tons of NdPr sold, and 12,983 metric tons of rare-earth oxide. The Materials segment contributed $36.7 million of adjusted earnings and the Magnetics segment $9.6 million. For context, full-year 2025 was $224.4 million of revenue against an $85.9 million net loss with material operating cash burn - so the company is improving off a loss-making, capital-heavy build-out, not yet from steady profit.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: MP company page / Q1 FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: The Pentagon stake, the price floor, the offtake
body_text: In July 2025 MP Materials signed definitive agreements with the US Department of War, formerly the Department of Defense, to accelerate an end-to-end domestic rare-earth magnet supply chain and reduce foreign dependency. The department took 400,000 shares of Series A cumulative perpetual convertible preferred stock for $400 million in cash (recorded at about $413.6 million net of issuance costs), convertible into roughly 13.3 million common shares. Alongside the equity, the deal set a ten-year floor under the price of the company's NdPr, added a ten-year magnet offtake commitment, and included a separate samarium project loan - all under Defense Production Act Title III authority. The company's own risk disclosures call this an unconventional use of that authority and warn that some contractually agreed funding is contingent on future appropriations that Congress still has to pass. It is a government-backstopped commercial position, and the filing is candid that the backstop carries legislative-funding risk.
qualifier: from the 10-K and quarterly filing
vault_ref: MP company page / Pentagon partnership scope

section_title: A new line: price-protection income
body_text: Starting in the fourth quarter of 2025, a new revenue category appeared on MP Materials' statements: Price Protection Agreement income, which was $42.3 million in the first quarter of 2026. It flows from the Department of War's ten-year NdPr price floor and is accounted for as a mark-to-market derivative: it records a gain when the market NdPr price falls below the agreed floor for the company's NdPr produced at Mountain Pass. In plain terms, it is a cash-flow backstop that pays most when prices are weak, rather than an operating sale of product. It is a genuinely new and sizeable line for the quarter, but by its own design it is tied to periods when prices sit below the floor.
qualifier: from the earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: MP company page / Price Protection Agreement

section_title: Walking away from China sales
body_text: In July 2025, to align with the Department of War agreements and support its domestic supply-chain goals, MP Materials stopped all sales of its products to China. Before that, it had sold into China through an offtake arrangement with a related party, Shenghe Resources. The company's full-year 2025 revenue of $224.4 million, down from $253.4 million in 2023, partly reflects the timing of that cutoff. The cleaner read on the post-China business shows up afterward: first-quarter 2026 revenue rose 49% year over year as the ramp and US and allied demand took hold. It is a deliberate strategic choice - giving up a market to rebuild a Western supply chain - and the company is candid that it reshaped its revenue base.
qualifier: from the 10-K and quarterly filing
vault_ref: MP company page / China sales cessation

section_title: How much of this is really defense?
body_text: One open question sits under the whole story: how much of MP Materials' magnet business will end up defense-driven versus commercial. The Department of War offtake and price floor de-risk the economics, but defense is only one end market alongside electric vehicles, wind power, and emerging robotics. It is not disclosed how the company will split its Independence and future 10X magnet capacity between Department of War offtake and merchant customers, nor how quickly the Texas magnet ramp converts into revenue. The magnet business is contracted with anchor customers, but the balance between strategic and commercial demand - and the pace of the ramp - is still being proven, not yet reported.
qualifier: an open question the company itself flags
vault_ref: MP company page / open questions

section_title: Growing fast, losing money - and where the loss comes from
body_text: Revenue grew 168% in FY2025, to $5.1 billion from $1.9 billion, and kept climbing in Q1 2026 to $2.1 billion, up 112% year-over-year and 32% sequentially. The company still runs a net loss, and the reason sits below the operating line. In Q1 2026 the operating loss was $144 million, but the net loss was $740 million - the roughly $536 million gap is almost entirely interest expense, the cost of the debt funding the build. For the full year 2025 the operating result was a $46 million loss while the net loss was $1.17 billion, with interest expense of $1.23 billion the single largest driver. Management also reports an adjusted, non-GAAP net loss - $589 million for Q1 2026 - alongside the $740 million GAAP figure. Revenue is growing explosively; the loss is a financing cost, not an operating one.
qualifier: from the filings and the earnings call
vault_ref: CRWV company page / financial snapshot

section_title: CapEx shows up before revenue
body_text: The spending runs ahead of the income it is meant to generate. Full-year 2026 capex is guided to $31-35 billion, against revenue guidance of just $12-13 billion - capex is roughly 2.6 to 2.9 times revenue, one of the most extreme ratios among large AI-capacity spenders. Q1 2026 capex was $6.8 billion, described by management as on schedule. The CFO put the timing plainly: capex shows up before revenue and cash flow. The low end of the 2026 capex range was raised, with management attributing the increase to higher component pricing - the same memory and GPU cost-push cited across the space. The bet is that the contracted backlog converts to revenue fast enough to service the debt the capex is built on; the capex flows into construction in progress and is not depreciated until the capacity comes online.
qualifier: from the earnings call guidance
vault_ref: CRWV company page / capex-vs-revenue gap

section_title: Financed with debt, and proud of the structure
body_text: Unlike the cash-generating hyperscalers, this build is funded with debt rather than operating cash flow. Total debt principal reached about $25.1 billion in Q1 2026, up from $21.6 billion at the end of 2025 and $8.0 billion a year earlier. FY2025 interest expense was $1.23 billion and rising. In January 2026 NVIDIA - the company's main GPU supplier - invested $2.0 billion in its Class A common stock. Management's own emphasis is on the financing engine: a roughly $8.5 billion facility backed by the GPU infrastructure earned an investment-grade rating and priced at an implied cost of less than 6%, structured non-recourse to the parent. On the call, management said the company had no debt maturities until 2029 other than self-amortizing contract-backed debt and vendor financing. The debt is large and its cost is falling; both facts sit side by side.
qualifier: from the filings and the earnings call
vault_ref: CRWV company page / financing and debt

section_title: The unresolved race: backlog against debt
body_text: The contracted revenue backlog reached about $99.4 billion in Q1 2026, up nearly 50% in one quarter, and management points to it as forward visibility into 2026 and beyond. The open question is whether that backlog converts to revenue and cash fast enough to service more than $25 billion of rising-cost debt before maturities come due. Because the company spends roughly 2.6 to 2.9 times its revenue and its capex lands before the revenue does, it is the layer of the AI-capex chain that would feel a demand slowdown first - quarters before a cash-funded hyperscaler would. Independent estimates, not company figures, put the 2031 bonds near junk-level yields and a debt-to-equity load around four and a half times; the primary debt figures stand where they differ. How exposed the company is to a tightening in credit markets, and when it turns cash-flow positive, are genuinely open.
qualifier: open question
vault_ref: CRWV company page / open questions

section_title: One customer, 100% of colocation
body_text: Core Scientific discloses that one customer, CoreWeave, currently accounts for 100% of its colocation segment revenue. The contracted capacity was expanded through a series of options - from 16 to 216 to roughly 590 megawatts - on twelve-year leases. At the end of March 2026, 243 megawatts were billable to CoreWeave, including the full turnover of the Marble, North Carolina site (65 megawatts) and the first phase at Dalton, Georgia (30 megawatts). Management expects to reach more than 450 billable megawatts by the end of summer 2026 and the full 590 megawatts by early 2027. The thesis and the central risk here are the same fact: the company owns power-secured, interconnected sites, but every dollar of colocation revenue comes from a single tenant.
qualifier: from the annual and quarterly filings and the earnings call
vault_ref: CORZ company page / CoreWeave concentration + delivery curve

section_title: The customer that tried to acquire the landlord
body_text: There is an unusual fact at the center of Core Scientific's independence: CoreWeave - the customer that is 100% of its colocation revenue - signed an all-stock agreement to acquire the whole company in July 2025. That agreement was terminated in October 2025 after Core Scientific's own stockholders rejected it at a special meeting. There was no termination fee, and roughly $21.6 million of advisory and legal costs landed in the 2025 results. The standalone company described here exists because its own shareholders refused to be absorbed by the tenant they depend on - a genuinely unusual host-and-tenant dynamic.
qualifier: from the annual filing
vault_ref: CORZ company page / terminated CoreWeave merger

section_title: The project bond and its lockbox
body_text: Core Scientific funds its build with debt against contracted demand. In May 2026 it closed a $3.3 billion CoreWeave project bond at a 7.75% coupon, with net proceeds of roughly $2.9 billion after closing costs and funding a required debt-service-reserve account. The bond carries a lockbox structure: CoreWeave contract revenues flow into a designated account and are applied through a defined cash waterfall - first to operating expenses, then to debt service, then to other permitted uses. Unlike a traditional project-finance lockbox tied to one project under development, this structure lets the majority of the proceeds be released up to the corporate level to fund non-CoreWeave projects. The company also has two publicly traded convertible notes outstanding - $460 million at 3.00% due 2029 and $625 million at 0.00% due 2031. The final indenture terms of the bond are still to be confirmed in the next quarterly filing.
qualifier: from the earnings call and annual filing
vault_ref: CORZ company page / financing + project bond

section_title: Grid buyer today, behind-the-meter gas tomorrow
body_text: Today Core Scientific is a buyer of grid power, not a generator: its annual filing describes firm and interruptible supply agreements with eleven utilities delivered to dedicated substations, with no on-site generation in the picture. The forward strategy is a pivot toward behind-the-meter natural-gas generation to expand where the grid-interconnection queue would otherwise bind - a linear gas pipeline to the Pecos campus described as low-emission generation, and behind-the-meter infrastructure combined with utility supply at Muskogee, helped by Oklahoma's behind-the-meter legislation. Management puts the lead time at about 12 to 14 months and is explicit that the ownership model is undecided: some generation the company might own, some it might contract through a third-party power-purchase agreement, with air-quality permits still in study. The durable-asset case strengthens if that generation comes online and is owned, and weakens if it stays a pass-through power-purchase agreement where the company captures no generation economics. This is forward and in development, not an operating position today.
qualifier: from the earnings call and annual filing
vault_ref: CORZ company page / power strategy + behind-the-meter pivot

section_title: The capex-versus-revenue gap
body_text: Nebius raised its 2026 capital-spending guidance to $20-25 billion, up from a prior $16-20 billion range. That sits against roughly $3 billion of revenue for the year - capital spending of about seven to eight times revenue - an unusually wide gap between what the company spends and what it currently earns. First-quarter capital spending was $2.47 billion; property and equipment grew from $5.55 billion to $7.13 billion in the quarter, with depreciation and amortization of $212 million. Management frames the raise as funding 2027 capacity that comes online early next year, which it expects to contribute to revenue in the first half of 2027 against customer commitments already in place. The company's own annual filing states plainly that the core business is capital-intensive and currently not profitable - the spend is being deployed years ahead of the revenue it is meant to generate.
qualifier: from the earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: NBIS company page / capex-vs-revenue gap

section_title: The core cloud is profitable; the group is not
body_text: Two things are true of Nebius at once. For the 2025 full year, total revenue was $529.8 million, up roughly 480%, and the core Nebius cloud segment turned adjusted-EBITDA-positive at about +$59.0 million, from a $(128.5) million loss the year before. But the group's adjusted EBITDA was still negative at about $(64.9) million, dragged there entirely by two early-stage non-core businesses the holding company carries - Avride in autonomous driving, at about $(82.7) million, and TripleTen in edtech, at about $(41.2) million. Adjusted EBITDA also excludes the large depreciation of the GPU fleet, so even the core is loss-making on a bottom-line basis - the first-quarter 2026 group net loss was $(113.5) million. A third non-core business, Toloka, was deconsolidated mid-2025. The AI-cloud story reads stronger than the consolidated numbers, but the group's losses are real.
qualifier: from the annual filing and quarterly filing
vault_ref: NBIS company page / segment split + holding-company structure

section_title: How big is the loss, really
body_text: The size of the quarterly loss depends on which reading you take, and the two have not yet been reconciled. A reading of the quarterly filing shows a net loss of about $(113.5) million. A separate outside read of the same quarter shows a positive net income near $621 million - but that figure is flattered by a roughly $780.6 million one-time, non-cash gain from revaluing a stake in ClickHouse, which nets to an adjusted loss near $(100.3) million once the one-timer is stripped out. Exactly what the reported net-income line includes or excludes is a to-verify item for the next filing. Either way the shape is the same: a core cloud that is adjusted-EBITDA-positive sitting inside a group that still loses money once heavy GPU-fleet depreciation is counted.
qualifier: two readings of the same quarter, pending reconciliation
vault_ref: NBIS company page / net-income reconciliation

section_title: Funded ahead of the revenue: the cash cushion
body_text: Nebius entered its build with a large liquidity buffer: about $9.3 billion of cash at March 31 2026, up from $3.7 billion at year-end, built from early-2026 financings. Those included roughly $2 billion from NVIDIA - the company's annual filing records this as about $2 billion of proceeds plus a pre-funded Class A share warrant of about 21 million shares - alongside convertible debt. The NVIDIA piece is the supplier-funds-its-customer circularity, confirmed here from Nebius's own filing. For the incremental capacity in the raised guidance, the CFO said it will be funded through further financing, specifically flagging asset-backed financing against the Microsoft contract. So the model is debt raised against contracted demand - carried, for now, on a large cash cushion.
qualifier: from the earnings call and annual filing
vault_ref: NBIS company page / cash buffer + financing

section_title: Two anchor customers, and the concentration question
body_text: Microsoft and Meta are Nebius's anchor customers, on multi-year, multi-billion-dollar agreements the company describes as carrying attractive economics and financing terms. Meta committed to take up to $15 billion of capacity, at Nebius's option, over a five-year contract; a separate five-year agreement framed at more than $27 billion is also referenced, and which of the two anchors it belongs to is still being confirmed against the filing. Concentration is the fragility here: an outside read of the filing puts Microsoft and Meta together at more than 90% of backlog, which would make the contracted demand narrow even as the dollar commitments are large. That figure is an inference - no exact customer split is disclosed, and the precise share is an open question until the next annual filing confirms which customers exceed the disclosure threshold.
qualifier: an open question; no exact split disclosed
vault_ref: NBIS company page / customer concentration

section_title: The record year, and the backlog behind it
body_text: AeroVironment's fiscal year ended April 30, 2026 was a record. Full-year revenue reached about $1.98 billion, up 30% on an organic basis and 141% as reported, the reported figure lifted by the BlueHalo acquisition that closed at the start of the year. The fourth quarter set a company record at $642 million, up 31% organically. Adjusted EBITDA for the year was $286 million, a 14% margin, above the high end of the company's most recent guidance range; the fourth quarter alone contributed $140 million of that, a 22% margin. Total backlog stood at $2.7 billion, with funded backlog of $1,183.0 million at year end, up 63% from a year earlier. The fourth-quarter book-to-bill was 0.9 times on $572 million of bookings, while the trailing-twelve-month book-to-bill was 1.4 times. The award slate spanned the portfolio - a $168 million Army task order for next-generation Switchblade, an $874 million five-year sole-source contract vehicle for allied sales, a $117 million P550 reconnaissance award, a roughly $240 million long-haul laser-communications award, and a $96 million Freedom Eagle-1 interceptor contract framed as a market opportunity of about $1 billion over several years.
qualifier: from the Q4 and full-year fiscal 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: AVAV company page / fiscal 2026 financial snapshot + award slate

section_title: Why the GAAP loss and the positive EBITDA sit in the same year
body_text: AeroVironment reported a GAAP net loss of $265.1 million for fiscal 2026 even as adjusted EBITDA stayed positive at $286 million. The gap is mostly one item: a $240.7 million non-cash goodwill impairment on the Space reporting unit, taken after the US Space Force terminated the company's SCAR program for convenience; a January stop-work order had been the trigger. On top of that sit purchase-accounting intangible amortization and integration costs from the BlueHalo deal, none of which touch adjusted EBITDA. GAAP gross margin was 25% for the year against an adjusted gross margin of 30%, in line with original guidance but still below a legacy level near 40%. The two operating segments diverged: Autonomous Systems, about 69% of revenue, ran a 21% adjusted-EBITDA margin, while Space, Cyber & Directed Energy ran roughly a $3 million adjusted-EBITDA loss and is not yet profitable. SCAR itself contributed $121 million of revenue in fiscal 2026 and $31 million in the fourth quarter, leaving an approximately $30 million gap in the first quarter of fiscal 2027; about $291 million of Space goodwill remains after the write-off and is exposed to further impairment if forecasts weaken.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 annual report
vault_ref: AVAV company page / goodwill impairment + GAAP-to-adjusted bridge

section_title: Three governance flags, all tracing to the acquisition
body_text: Three developments landed alongside AeroVironment's fiscal 2026 annual report, all tied to the BlueHalo integration. First, the company restated its unaudited third-quarter fiscal 2026 financials in an amendment filed June 22, 2026, after identifying a material weakness in the design of controls over its goodwill-impairment analysis. Second, management concluded that disclosure controls were not effective as of April 30, 2026, citing three material weaknesses in internal control: BlueHalo IT general controls, the goodwill-impairment reconciliation control, and the BlueHalo financial-close and reporting process; remediation is ongoing, with the new controls still to be tested over additional quarters. Third, a securities class action, Norrell v. AeroVironment, was filed in the Eastern District of Virginia, alleging misleading statements about the company's work on the Space Force SCAR program, with a class period running June 25, 2025 to March 10, 2026; it seeks unspecified damages and is at an early stage. The CFO also changed during the year, from Kevin McDonnell to Sean Woodward. Management disclosed the weakness proactively on the earnings call.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 annual report and Q4 earnings call
vault_ref: AVAV company page / restatement, material weakness & litigation

section_title: The fiscal 2027 guide, and the budget-timing bet inside it
body_text: AeroVironment guided fiscal 2027 revenue to $2.125-2.225 billion, about 10% growth at the midpoint, with adjusted EBITDA of $305-325 million. Management framed the roughly 10% figure as deliberate budget-timing conservatism rather than a demand slowdown: the guide assumes a continuing resolution, meaning it does not assume the full defense budget passes on time. Management's own estimate is a budget approved around December or January, with cash not reaching the military services until roughly March. Until then, the company expects to run on its $2.7 billion backlog and on funding already approved. The guide excludes any SCAR-related revenue. Capital spending is guided to 12-14% of revenue, aimed mainly at production-capacity expansion across sites including Salt Lake City - which could produce more than $2 billion of Switchblades or other products per year, starting spring of calendar 2027 - plus Huntsville, Albuquerque and Dayton. Because of that spending, management does not expect fiscal 2027 to be free-cash-flow positive. A separate reconciliation bill before Congress could add funding, but management says its timing is uncertain and it is not assumed in the guide.
qualifier: from the Q4 and full-year fiscal 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: AVAV company page / fiscal 2027 guidance + CR-timing dependence

section_title: The rare-earth dependence, in its own filing
body_text: AeroVironment's own annual report flags that its products - including motors, batteries and other advanced components - rely on rare-earth metals, a significant majority of which are sourced from China, alongside semiconductor and component sourcing risk from China. In March 2025, China placed AeroVironment on its export-control list; this language is unchanged in the fiscal 2026 filing, with no escalation or relief disclosed. The company describes its edge as production capacity, breadth across small drones, loitering munitions and counter-drone systems, and incumbency on programs of record - not ownership of the upstream materials and secure components it depends on. Its closest-to-scarce position is Titan, an AI-enabled radio-frequency counter-drone system whose sales more than doubled in fiscal 2026 on a pro forma basis, with demand expected to rise through 2027 and beyond. US government customers were about 85% of fiscal 2026 revenue, with the Department of Defense about 63%; the international end-market was about 28%.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 annual report risk factors and earnings call
vault_ref: AVAV company page / supply-chain dependence + customer mix

section_title: The quarter, and the raised guide
body_text: Kratos's first quarter of fiscal 2026 was a beat-and-raise. Revenue was $371 million, up 22.6% in total and 15.8% organically, with the largest contributions from unmanned systems, defense and rocket support, turbine technologies, and microwave products. Backlog set a record at about $2 billion, on a 1.6-to-1 book-to-bill. Adjusted EBITDA, a non-GAAP figure from the call, was $38.7 million - above the $25-30 million range management had guided. On the strength of the start, full-year 2026 revenue guidance was raised to $1.70-1.76 billion, an estimated 15-19% organic growth rate. The company ended the quarter with $1.46 billion of cash and no debt.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: KTOS company page / Q1 FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: How the build is being financed
body_text: The beat-and-raise is real, and so is how it is paid for. Operating cash flow was negative $27.4 million in the quarter and free cash flow was about negative $43 million, as capacity is being built ahead of orders - Valkyrie units produced before a contract award, plus new propulsion, radar, and hypersonic facilities. Full-year capital spending is guided to roughly $160 million. The funding mechanism has been equity: two offerings in eight months raised about $1.9 billion net, lifting share count roughly 11% in a single quarter and about 44% since 2023, and leaving the balance sheet debt-free with $1.46 billion of cash. GAAP profitability is thin - operating income was $4.7 million, a 1.3% margin - and net income of $11.9 million was bridged up from operating income by interest income on the cash pile plus a tax benefit. The bet is that the demand converts before the cash and the dilution run out.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 quarterly filing and call
vault_ref: KTOS company page / capital structure and free cash flow

section_title: The plane and the engine, and affordable mass
body_text: Kratos's stated edge is vertical integration plus affordability. Management describes it as the only company that builds both the airframe and the engine under one organization - the Tactical Firejet now flies on Kratos's own jet engine. The strategy is framed as a 'Family of Affordable Mass': more than 30,000 low-cost missiles and drones targeted over the next several years, built to deliver, in the customer's words, 85% of the capability that can be fielded today rather than a 100% solution that may or may not arrive. Small jet-engine low-rate production is expected to begin late 2026, ramping toward several thousand engines in 2027 at $40,000-60,000 each, tied to specific programs. The Prometheus solid-rocket-motor joint venture with Rafael - $50 million from each partner plus a $100 million Defense Department facility commitment - is an attempt to add a second source to a strained munitions supply base, with a first motor firing expected in 2027.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: KTOS company page / propulsion and affordable-mass programs

section_title: Hypersonics and space, the growth drivers
body_text: Two franchises carry the forward story. Hypersonics is guided from about $400 million of revenue in 2026 to about $700 million in 2027, anchored by MACH-TB 2.0 - a test program with a $1.45 billion ceiling over five years, the largest award in company history. Near-term hypersonic revenue is described as primarily solid rocket motors, with air-breathing engines a 2027 story. In space, Kratos won a $447 million US Space Force prime contract for the Resilient Missile Warning and Tracking program, a medium-earth-orbit constellation for detecting and tracking missile launches - management frames it as part of the broader Golden Dome architecture, with Kratos providing the ground system and software to operate the satellites. The satellite business ran a 3-to-1 book-to-bill in the quarter. The $700 million hypersonics figure is a forward target, not a booked result.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: KTOS company page / hypersonics and space programs

section_title: Verbal, pre-contract, and the obligation backlog
body_text: A recurring hedge runs under the wins: many are not yet obligated contracts. Valkyrie was pre-low-rate-production, with the first low-rate contract in negotiation and a plan to reach about 40 drones a year by early 2028 - and units were partly built as company-owned assets ahead of any award, so revenue recognition depends on the customer's final configuration. The '$1 billion-plus' sole-source hypersonic expansion was described as a verbal award the company believed it would receive shortly, not a signed contract. Management tied its confidence to the 2025 defense reconciliation now being put on contract, but flagged that only about $30 billion of the $156 billion had been obligated by April, with the rest still flowing. The longer-dated part of the outlook - a projected fiscal 2027 national-security spend near $1.5 trillion - rests partly on a requested reconciliation figure that depends on future appropriations, not only on enacted law. The demand signal is described as real; the timing of conversion is the open variable.
qualifier: an open question the company itself flags on the call
vault_ref: KTOS company page / verbal-award and budget-timing risk

section_title: The quarter, and the raise
body_text: Mercury Systems' quarter ending March 2026 was a beat-and-raise on the operational lines. Revenue was $235.8 million, up 11.5% organically year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA was $36.1 million - a 15.3% margin, up 46% and 360 basis points from a year earlier - and gross margin was 29.3%, up 230 basis points as lower-margin legacy development backlog converts out and new bookings come in at target margins. Bookings set a record at $348.3 million, a 1.48 book-to-bill, against a record backlog approaching $1.6 billion; domestic revenue was about 88% of the total and grew 17%. Coming out of the quarter, management raised full-year fiscal 2026 guidance to mid-single-digit revenue growth (from low-single-digit) and a mid-teens adjusted EBITDA margin, with full-year free cash flow expected positive. The longer-term target profile management is converging on is above-market top-line growth, a low-to-mid-20s adjusted EBITDA margin, and 50% free-cash-flow conversion.
qualifier: from the quarterly filing and earnings call
vault_ref: MRCY company page / Q3 FY2026 snapshot + raised guidance

section_title: The turnaround, in adjusted terms
body_text: The turnaround is real and inflecting, but it lives in the adjusted lines. Adjusted EBITDA went from $9.4 million in fiscal 2024 to $119.4 million in fiscal 2025, while revenue grew about 9% to $912.0 million and gross margin widened 440 basis points to 27.9%. Even so, the company stayed a GAAP net loss - about $37.9 million for fiscal 2025 (narrowed from about $137.6 million) and about $30.5 million across the first nine months of fiscal 2026 - held down by roughly $33 million a year of interest on a drawn revolver plus heavy intangible amortization. There is no going concern, no goodwill impairment, and no material weakness; a fiscal 2024 restructuring is largely behind it, with roughly 220 positions cut across fiscal 2025 and 2026. The straight read: the turnaround is credible in adjusted terms, but GAAP profitability and a clean run-rate free cash flow are still the open items, on a mid-cap balance sheet.
qualifier: from the annual and quarterly filings
vault_ref: MRCY company page / turnaround read (Framework D6)

section_title: The trusted, onshore secure-processing moat
body_text: Mercury's differentiated moat is trusted, secure, onshore microelectronics: certified trusted design and manufacturing (secure system-on-chip design, assembly, packaging and test), plus reverse-engineering-mitigation and safety-certifiable security IP, and secure chiplets that management flags as a large addressable market. On the call the CEO said there are certain security standards 'we're the only ones that can meet - we've got a nice moat around this business.' Products are designed and manufactured through an onshore trusted supply chain, with no explicit China, Taiwan or foundry manufacturing dependence disclosed (tariff risk only). That positions Mercury as the trusted-domestic pole of the secure-microelectronics node - the structural beneficiary of the push to de-risk secure-microelectronics supply away from China.
qualifier: from the annual filing and earnings call
vault_ref: MRCY company page / trusted-domestic-microelectronics moat

section_title: Swinging the pendulum: the working-capital story
body_text: Roughly three years ago management 'swung the pendulum hard' to a just-in-time material model, largely to address a multi-year buildup in net working capital. That cut net working capital about $225 million from its peak (roughly $659 million down to about $434 million, a 34% reduction) and drove fiscal 2025's strong free cash flow of about $119 million. Management is candid that this number was largely a one-time working-capital unwind, so it is not a clean run-rate - and that the hard swing 'really did introduce some constraints in being able to accelerate our backlog conversion.' The current fix is to 'pull the supply base to the left' - bringing material due-dates ahead of the need-date to convert backlog faster and improve revenue linearity. Fiscal 2026 free cash flow is lumpier (a roughly $2 million outflow in the March quarter versus a $24 million inflow a year earlier), with the full year guided positive; net debt was about $260 million and improving after a $150 million revolver paydown in late April 2026.
qualifier: from the earnings call and filings
vault_ref: MRCY company page / net-working-capital turnaround

section_title: The tailwinds that aren't in the bookings
body_text: Management ties potential upside to increased defense budgets globally, Golden Dome, munitions acceleration, and global rearmament - and is careful that none of it is in the current numbers. As the CEO put it, those tailwinds are 'not reflected in any of our bookings or our outlook,' and management views them as 'additive' to the target profile it is converging on, only if funding is allocated to the right program priorities. On a specific example, management noted it is publicly known there are conversations around increased quantities on the LTAMDS air-defense program, but 'that really hasn't factored into any of our bookings to date.' The CEO's own hedge on timing: those tailwinds 'could start to manifest likely by the end of calendar 2026, but potentially as early as our fourth quarter.' Whether and when the potential converts into bookings is an open question management is watching, not counting on.
qualifier: management's framing; flagged as not yet in bookings or outlook
vault_ref: MRCY company page / defense-budget tailwinds (open question)

section_title: Where the sensor sits inside a diversified conglomerate
body_text: Teledyne is a diversified instruments and imaging company, and its defense exposure - which management sizes at roughly 30 to 35% of the company, close to $2 billion of about $6.1 billion in annual revenue - spans drones, counter-drone systems, electronic warfare, missiles and munitions. The highest-value piece, the cooled and uncooled infrared detectors and seekers, sits inside the Digital Imaging segment, which is just over half of total sales. But much of Digital Imaging is commercial and broad-cycle: machine-vision cameras for semiconductor inspection, medical X-ray, and space-based imaging. The four reportable segments by weight are Digital Imaging (about 52%), Instrumentation (24%), Aerospace and Defense Electronics (17%), and Engineered Systems (7%). Aerospace and Defense Electronics, the formal defense-electronics block, grew about 36% year-over-year in fiscal 2025, helped by acquisitions. So the sensor position the defense story prizes is a high-quality slice of a diversified industrial-technology company, not a pure-play.
qualifier: from the annual filing and earnings call
vault_ref: TDY company page / segment structure

section_title: Picks and shovels: infrared detectors to every drone maker
body_text: On its own call, management made the structural point plainly: Teledyne supplies 'cooled, visible, and more importantly, infrared detectors, not only to our own drone manufacturers, but also to everyone else across the world that's making drones.' That means the company wins at the sensor and seeker layer largely regardless of which drone airframe ultimately wins - the inverse of a platform prime's exposure. As airframes are pushed toward a low-cost commodity floor, value tends to migrate up to the payload and sensor, which is Teledyne's layer. FLIR Defense, the unit that holds this position, was described as growing about 9%, with essentially all its product lines growing. The company frames this as a picks-and-shovels position: strong, but not proof against pricing pressure on high-volume, cheap counter-drone sensors - a point management did not directly address.
qualifier: from the earnings call
vault_ref: TDY company page / picks-and-shovels sensor supply

section_title: The unmanned franchise - air, ground, and sea
body_text: Teledyne makes unmanned systems across air, ground, and sea. In the air, the Black Hornet nano-drone - Black Hornet 3 into the newer Black Hornet 4 - is expected to generate about $500 million of cumulative revenue over the period, with orders in the United States, Europe, and the Middle East; management said it would 'remain with the $500 million for now,' noting some pockets are growing faster than 10%. Rogue 1, an armed loitering drone, is in full-rate production, with first contracts management expects to 'increase substantially with time.' On the sea, unmanned subsea vehicles - including Gavia mine-countermeasure vehicles and long-endurance gliders - grew more than 20% in the first quarter, with orders in Europe. Counter-drone demand is elevated: in the first quarter and early second quarter the company booked orders for infrared cameras and subsystems totaling tens of millions of dollars. Management cautioned that some of the larger defense orders are yet to come and that timing is lumpy.
qualifier: from the earnings call
vault_ref: TDY company page / unmanned air-ground-sea franchise

section_title: The two-sided China squeeze
body_text: Teledyne owns a hard-to-copy infrared-sensor position, but its own filings show it is squeezed from above by the same China concentration. The annual report discloses that China has restricted the export of certain rare-earth minerals and permanent magnets used in its products, which 'has in the past delayed' and could in the future limit its ability to ship products that rely on those components. The filing separately names gallium and germanium alongside permanent magnets as having 'impacted the production and pricing of some of our digital imaging and aerospace and defense products.' Germanium is the load-bearing infrared-window material for thermal optics, so the controls bite the imaging line directly. The structural irony is that the same country whose military threat drives Teledyne's sensor and counter-drone demand also controls key inputs those sensors require. On the call, management said it is carrying extra inventory and investing in its own germanium machining as a cautious hedge; whether the restrictions materially constrain 2026 shipments or pricing is not yet resolved.
qualifier: from the annual filing and earnings call
vault_ref: TDY company page / two-sided China exposure

section_title: The disciplined acquirer, and the tempered surge
body_text: Teledyne has grown by disciplined acquisition - about 75 acquisitions and roughly $12.8 billion over 25 years, mostly funded with cash the company generated; roughly $800 to $900 million was spent in the most recent 12 to 13 months. It generates over $1 billion of free cash flow a year and is guiding toward roughly $1.1 billion. Unusually for this point in the year, it is currently favoring capital expenditure (up about 35% year-over-year) and R&D over deals, because 'our demand is larger than our capacity in certain areas.' Management actively tempers the defense-surge narrative that some peers lean into: first-quarter book-to-bill was 1.16 (a tenth straight quarter above one) and backlog was about $4.6 billion, and it raised full-year revenue guidance to about $6.415 billion - but it declined to promise 20%-a-year growth 'like others do,' warned that 'government cycles are tedious, even when there's urgent need,' and on the Golden Dome missile-defense program said 'we'll see how much budget goes in there in reality... asking for increased budgets is one thing, getting it is another.'
qualifier: from the earnings call
vault_ref: TDY company page / capital allocation and tempered defense outlook

section_title: The quarter, and the balance sheet behind the losses
body_text: Ouster's first quarter of 2026: revenue of $48.6 million, up 49% year over year, which included roughly seven weeks of the Stereolabs camera business acquired in February. Gross margin was 43%, up about 200 basis points from a year earlier. The company shipped more than 12,600 sensors - over 8,300 of them lidar, a quarterly record, plus more than 4,300 cameras - the thirteenth straight quarter of product-revenue growth. It is not profitable: the net loss narrowed to $17.5 million and the adjusted EBITDA loss to $7 million, from $8 million a year earlier. The offset is the balance sheet - about $174 million of cash and short-term investments and no debt. Smart Infrastructure was the largest vertical in the quarter, followed by Industrial; for full-year 2025, revenue was $169.4 million, up 52%, with a net loss of $60.4 million that narrowed from $97.0 million.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: OUST company page / Q1 2026 + FY2025 financial snapshot

section_title: One customer, about 31% - the base is still narrow
body_text: Alongside the growth sits a concentration fact worth naming: one customer was roughly 31% of Ouster's first-quarter 2026 revenue. The top line is expanding fast off a small base - 49% year-over-year growth, a record lidar-shipment quarter - but a single customer at nearly a third of revenue means the base is real and still narrow at the same time. The company does not name the customer. Diversification as the platform scales is the thing to watch, not a resolved fact.
qualifier: from the quarterly filing's concentration disclosure
vault_ref: OUST company page / customer concentration

section_title: The path to profitability, stated as a framework
body_text: Management frames the road to profitability as a long-term financial model rather than a dated promise: revenue growth of 30-50%, GAAP gross margin of 35-40%, and operating-expense growth of 5-8% off 2025 levels. Run those together, in its telling, and the company reaches profitability 'somewhere within 2027.' For the second quarter of 2026 it guided revenue to a range of $49.5 million to $52.5 million. The company describes this as a model it keeps hitting quarter after quarter, and says it will stay diligent on operating expenses - including the added costs from the Stereolabs acquisition - to get there. It is a target and a framework, not a reported result.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call; the 2027 timing is a management framework
