vault_ref: OUST company page / path to profitability + long-term framework

section_title: Read the gross margin carefully
body_text: The reported gross margin flatters the underlying business, and it is worth stripping apart. Ouster's full-year 2025 gross margin of 49.3% included $22.8 million of new IP-license royalties that cost the company almost nothing to earn. Take those out and an outside read puts the underlying product-only margin closer to 41%. Those royalties are not expected to repeat at that scale: management guided total 2026 royalty revenue to less than $5 million, most of it in the back half of the year, and royalty revenue in the first quarter of 2026 was not material. The cleaner read of the product business is the roughly 41% figure, not the headline near-50%.
qualifier: outside read that strips the royalty line from filed figures
vault_ref: OUST company page / royalty-adjusted gross margin

section_title: The open question: lidar versus vision-only
body_text: The central open question over Ouster - and over the whole camera-and-lidar layer of physical AI - is whether lidar keeps a durable role, or whether a camera-only, vision-first approach shrinks the market for it. Outside estimates already rank this sensing layer near the bottom of the value-capture ladder as a commoditizing one. Ouster's own hedge is not to bet on one modality but to fuse both: native-color lidar built on its in-house silicon, the Stereolabs stereo cameras and neural-depth acquired in February 2026, and edge compute, combined into a single perception stack. Whether that fusion strategy defends the lidar market or the market erodes underneath it is genuinely unresolved.
qualifier: an open question over the sensing layer
vault_ref: OUST company page / vision-vs-lidar route risk

section_title: The quarter, and the largest-ever raise
body_text: Palantir's first quarter of 2026: total revenue of $1.633 billion, up 85% year over year and 16% sequentially - the company's highest reported year-over-year growth rate as a public company. The US business, now 79% of total revenue, grew 104% year over year - passing 100% for the first time since the company's direct public offering - with US commercial up 133% and US government up 84%. Customer count reached 1,007, up 31%. The Rule of 40 score, which adds revenue growth to adjusted operating margin, was 145, and net dollar retention was 150%. On the strength of the US results, management raised full-year 2026 revenue guidance to a range of $7.650-7.662 billion, about 71% growth, which it called its largest-ever full-year guidance raise; the implied full-year Rule of 40 guide is 129.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: PLTR company page / Q1 2026 results and raised guidance

section_title: How it bills - outcomes, not seats
body_text: Palantir bills for multi-year outcomes rather than per-seat licenses or metered token consumption: customers commit contract value tied to deployments of its AI platform. In the first quarter of 2026 it closed $2.4 billion of total contract value bookings, up 61% year over year, including $1.2 billion in US commercial bookings - its third straight quarter above $1 billion there - while trailing-twelve-month US commercial bookings were $4.7 billion, up 115%. Remaining performance obligations were $4.5 billion, up 134%, and total remaining deal value was $11.8 billion, up 98%. One caveat the company flags: remaining performance obligations are mostly its commercial business, because they exclude contracts shorter than 12 months and amounts beyond termination-for-convenience clauses that are common in its government work. There is no separately disclosed AI or AIP revenue line - the AI contribution shows up only through the growth rates and these booking figures, not as its own number.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: PLTR company page / billed-unit lens and bookings

section_title: The ontology, and the no-slop zone
body_text: Palantir describes its defensibility as ontology depth plus agentic execution rather than a passive data-network effect. The ontology is a semantic model of an enterprise's data, processes and decisions - the company's chief technology officer called it 'the body of the AI brains' - and its AI platform orchestrates software agents across that ontology with governance the company frames as a 'no-slop zone,' meaning every agent action is authorized, cost-attributed and auditable. Management contrasts this 'operational AI' with generic large-language-model output, arguing that the value comes from work that was not previously feasible, not from automating tasks a company already does. Its own framing is that this is defensible but execution-dependent - the forward-deployed-engineer motion that builds the ontology does not commoditize, but it is talent-intensive, so the moat rests on continued execution rather than on a self-reinforcing data flywheel.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call; the execution-dependent read is the company's own framing
vault_ref: PLTR company page / ontology + agentic-execution moat

section_title: The parts that cut the other way
body_text: Set against the growth are several counterweights, all facts about Palantir's own business and none of them changing what the growth is. Government work is a large and slower-growing share: US government was about 42% of revenue in fiscal 2025 and rose to roughly 53% in the first quarter of 2026, and it grows more slowly than the commercial business; US-government budget and appropriations risk is a named risk factor in the company's annual filing. There is a wide gap between the company's GAAP operating margin - about 46% in the quarter - and its adjusted operating margin of about 60%; that gap is stock-based compensation, and the diluted share count of roughly 2.57 billion is rising. And the forward-deployed-engineer model that builds the ontology moat is talent-intensive and structurally hard to scale. These are the execution-dependent side of an otherwise fast-growing business, stated plainly.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2025 annual filing and Q1 2026 quarterly filing
vault_ref: PLTR company page / execution-dependent caveats

section_title: Named deployments, not demos
body_text: Palantir points to named customer deployments as evidence that its 'operational AI' produces real results rather than demos. Its examples include AIG using the platform for agentic underwriting and claims, GE Aerospace deepening a partnership to deploy agentic AI across production, and work with Moder and Freedom Mortgage on the mortgage process and with Ondas and World View. On the government side, it disclosed a US Department of Agriculture contract of up to $300 million. These are named examples the company put forward as evidence of its operational-AI thesis, not audited aggregate results - the argument it makes is that this kind of governed, ontology-based deployment is what generic AI models cannot do on their own.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: PLTR company page / customer deployment evidence

section_title: Three divisions, one workpiece
body_text: MKS runs three divisions. Vacuum Solutions covers vacuum, pressure, flow, gas and vapor delivery, gas-composition analysis, and plasma and reactive-gas products (remote plasma and ozone generators used in deposition, etch, and cleaning); it was about 40% of FY2025 revenue and the largest of the three. Photonics Solutions covers continuous-wave and pulsed lasers, optics, and precision motion and vibration control under the Newport brand (including the ULTRAlign fiber-alignment stage), serving lithography, metrology, inspection, and back-end semiconductor work; about 26% of FY2025 revenue. Materials Solutions supplies plating chemistry and equipment for electronics and packaging - plating, surface finishing, high-density interconnect, and flex-PCB processing - and carries the highest gross margin of the three at 54.1% in FY2025 on about 34% of revenue. Total FY2025 revenue was $3,931 million, up 9.6% year-over-year.
qualifier: from the annual and quarterly filings
vault_ref: MKSI company page / three-division structure

section_title: The quarter and the guide
body_text: First-quarter 2026 revenue was $1,078 million, up 15% year-over-year and 4% sequentially - at or above every guidance range. Gross margin was 47%, the high end of guidance; operating margin was 21.8%; adjusted EBITDA was $277 million, a 25.7% margin. By end market: semiconductor $466 million (up 13% year-over-year), electronics and packaging $321 million (up 27%), and specialty industrial $291 million (up 8% on a constant-currency basis). For the second quarter the company guided revenue of $1.2 billion plus or minus $40 million - semiconductor $550 million, electronics and packaging $350 million, specialty industrial $300 million - with gross margin of 47% plus or minus 100 basis points and adjusted EBITDA of $328 million plus or minus $26 million. Capital spending is guided to 4-5% of revenue for the year.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 quarterly filing and earnings call
vault_ref: MKSI company page / Q1 2026 financials + Q2 guidance

section_title: One step below the tool maker
body_text: MKS describes its largest customers as concentrated in the semiconductor industry, with a limited number accounting for a significant portion of revenue, but it does not name them. Its OEM customers - Applied Materials, Lam Research, KLA, and ASML - are inferable from the filing's language ('semiconductor capital equipment manufacturers') but are not disclosed by name, in line with the industry's usual non-disclosure. The practical consequence: MKS sits one step below those equipment makers, so its AI exposure is indirect - it supplies the tool builders, who integrate MKS subsystems into fab equipment that goes to chip makers, who then build for the datacenter. No direct chip-vendor reference-architecture partnership - with a GPU or accelerator vendor - is disclosed.
qualifier: an independent read of the disclosure; the specific OEM names are inferred, not disclosed
vault_ref: MKSI company page / customer concentration + sub-tier-1 OEM positioning

section_title: The Atotech ledger, in three columns
body_text: The Materials Solutions division was built from the Atotech acquisition, which closed in August 2022, and its outcome splits three ways. Market position: MKS calls itself one of the market leaders in the chemistry needed to bond circuit-board layers to each other - its highest-margin division at 54.1% in FY2025 - with AI now roughly 15% of chemistry revenue and AI-related advanced-PCB chemistry up 22% year-over-year on a constant-currency basis. Acquisition cost: a $1.9 billion goodwill and intangible impairment was recorded in 2023, tied to Atotech together with the earlier 2019 ESI acquisition. Capital structure: net debt was $3.6 billion at the end of the first quarter, about 3.5 times trailing-twelve-month EBITDA of over $1 billion - the company is paying it down, with a $100 million term-loan prepayment, net interest expense down to $37 million from $45 million a year earlier, and the dividend raised 14%.
qualifier: from the annual report and earnings call
vault_ref: MKSI company page / Atotech acquisition mixed-outcome + capital structure

section_title: The quarter, and the raised outlook
body_text: Onto Innovation's quarter ended March 31, 2026: revenue of $292 million, a record and up nearly 10% from the prior quarter, coming in above the company's own guidance range. Gross margin improved 110 basis points to 55.7% and operating margin rose 150 basis points to 26.7%. The quarter was helped by adoption of the Atlas G6 metrology platform and the first commercial shipments of the Dragonfly G5 inspection system. Management raised its full-year 2026 view: total revenue growth of more than 30% and revenue above $1.3 billion, with advanced packaging now expected to grow more than 50% and advanced nodes about 25% - the latter ahead of industry equipment growth in the low 20% range. Second-quarter revenue is guided to $320-330 million, a midpoint about 28% above a year earlier, and the company expects to exit the fourth quarter with an operating margin above 30%. These are management's forward figures, and they carry the company's own caveats about material-cost, fuel, and shipping headwinds.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: ONTO company page / Q1 2026 results and raised outlook

section_title: What it measures, and the $240 million HBM commitment
body_text: Onto Innovation makes process-control tools - metrology, inspection, and lithography software - that measure and inspect wafers rather than build them: bump height, film thickness, alignment, and defects across advanced-packaging steps like 2.5D/3D integration and HBM stack inspection. About half of last year's revenue came from advanced packaging and specialty devices, roughly $504 million. The largest single demand signal in its disclosures is a volume purchase agreement from one HBM customer, valued at over $240 million, covering Dragonfly 2D and 3D bump metrology through 2027, including more than $60 million of 3D bump inspection systems. The next-generation Dragonfly G5 inspection system has been qualified at a leading 2.5D logic customer, following earlier wins in high-bandwidth memory for both 2D inspection and 3D metrology, with a pipeline of over 15 applications across more than 10 customers. The precision bar keeps rising: two years ago the most advanced bumps were about 15 to 25 microns tall, and the company is now sampling bumps below 6 microns. The inspection is technology-generic - it checks the physical package whether the interconnect is electrical or optical.
qualifier: from the earnings calls
vault_ref: ONTO company page / advanced packaging + HBM exposure

section_title: Three customers, and the names on them
body_text: Onto Innovation's three largest customers were about 20%, 15%, and 14% of full-year revenue - roughly half the total. Its filings tag them, through the structured data in the filing, as TSMC, Samsung, and SK Hynix, and the company is unusually explicit for its industry: its annual report names 'Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Ltd.' and 'Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd.' directly as customers in its risk factors. Taiwan and Korea together are about 60% of revenue, which follows directly from those three accounts. More than 190 customers across 25-plus countries bought tools or software during the year, so the base is broad even as the top is concentrated. One disclosure wrinkle worth noting: a Chinese NAND maker, YMTC, showed up at 14% of revenue through the first nine months but dropped below 10% for the full year as China sales kept falling - YMTC is under U.S. export restrictions, which makes its earlier size a regulatory point to watch.
qualifier: from the annual report and quarterly filings
vault_ref: ONTO company page / customer concentration

section_title: The one thing in the record that doesn't line up
body_text: There is a genuine divergence inside Onto Innovation's own disclosures that the record keeps unresolved. Its annual report states that in 2025 the number of inspection units shipped for AI advanced-packaging chips actually decreased from the year before - a reported, backward-looking fact. Its earnings calls, over the same window, guide advanced-packaging growth of more than 30% and later more than 50%, with backlog nearly doubling in three months. Both can be true at once, and the company presents them side by side without picking one. The possible explanations it offers: a mix shift from inspection tools toward metrology tools (the Atlas G6 for the newest logic and DRAM), a digestion cycle between the first AI-packaging wave and the next, and the ordinary gap between what a filing reports and what a call forecasts. As of the March 2026 quarter, management's read is that both the metrology-mix-shift and the inspection-recovery explanations are operative at the same time - not a resolution so much as both threads still running.
qualifier: conflicting signals across the company's own filing and calls
vault_ref: ONTO company page / AI inspection tension

section_title: The quarter, and the year it's holding to
body_text: Veeco's first quarter of 2026 brought revenue of $158.3 million, down about 5% from a year earlier, at 36% non-GAAP gross margin. That came in a touch below the midpoint of guidance and below the gross-margin range, and management named the reason plainly: one laser-annealing system bound for a China customer needed an export license the company was informed of by U.S. authorities, an issue worth about $8 million of first-quarter revenue. For the second quarter the company guided revenue of $170-190 million and gross margin of 38-40%. It reiterated a full-year 2026 range of $740-800 million, with growth weighted to the second half of the year. Backlog ended 2025 at $555 million, up about 35% year over year, which management points to as the visibility behind that second-half acceleration. That is the base it is growing from: full-year 2025 revenue of $664 million, down about 7%, with the semiconductor segment nearly flat at $477 million while compound semiconductor and data storage fell much more sharply.
qualifier: from the quarterly filing and earnings call
vault_ref: VECO company page / Q1 FY2026 financials + FY2026 guidance

section_title: The $250 million laser order, and the shift behind it
body_text: Veeco disclosed orders of more than $250 million from multiple customers for the tools that build indium phosphide lasers - its MOCVD epitaxy platform, its wet-processing systems, and its ion-beam deposition tool for coating the laser facets - with deliveries starting in 2026 and accelerating significantly in 2027. Management framed the driver as data centers moving from copper interconnect toward optics for more speed and bandwidth, and pointed to recent investments by a major AI-chip company into optical-networking suppliers as the industry inflection point it sees behind the demand. A large portion of the order is for the facet-coating tool, sold to suppliers of next-generation 800-gigabit and 1.6-terabit optical transceivers for hyperscale customers. To meet it, the company plans to raise capacity on that tool line roughly tenfold from its base level, reaching that level in early 2027, with the possibility of doubling again. Veeco sizes the served market for indium phosphide laser manufacturing at about $700 million by 2030 - a forward framing, not a booked result.
qualifier: from the earnings call; forward market-size figures are management's framing
vault_ref: VECO company page / $250M+ indium phosphide laser orders

section_title: Three steps in the laser, and where Veeco stands on each
body_text: Asked directly whether it was a primary or a second source on the big laser order, Veeco's management split the answer by process step rather than dressing it up. There are three pieces it serves in indium phosphide laser making: the epitaxy step that grows the active layer, the wet-processing and etch steps, and the ion-beam coating of the laser facets. On facet coating it calls itself a strong incumbent - though not everywhere, only at a number of key customers. On the epitaxy step it says plainly that a competitor holds a good incumbent position and that Veeco is today the second source, having built up its products over recent years to compete. On wet processing it says it holds a strong position with several of the leaders. So the more than $250 million of orders spans all three lines, but the strength is uneven, and the company says so.
qualifier: from the earnings call Q&A
vault_ref: VECO company page / competitive positioning by process step

section_title: One customer getting bigger, and a two-sided China line
body_text: Two facts sit in Veeco's filings at the same time. First, its largest customer keeps growing as a share of revenue: under 10% two years ago, 11% in 2024, and 17% in 2025, with no other customer above 10%. The company does not name that customer; the pattern - Taiwanese, semiconductor, advanced packaging - is consistent with a large foundry, but that is inference, not disclosure. Second, China revenue fell about $80 million, or 31%, in 2025 to roughly a quarter of the total, which the company ties to U.S. export controls on semiconductor equipment. Because Veeco's manufacturing is about 99% based in the United States, it sits on the export-control side of the U.S.-China split - restricted in what it can ship into China rather than restricted in what it can ship out. The rising single-customer share is a demand floor and a concentration risk in the same disclosure; how high it can go before it reads as a risk is an open question.
qualifier: from the annual filing; the customer's identity is not disclosed
vault_ref: VECO company page / customer concentration + geography

section_title: The split quarter: display up, chips down
body_text: Photronics' fiscal second quarter, ended May 3, 2026: total revenue of $210 million, essentially flat year-over-year and below the company's own $212-220 million guide. IC mask revenue fell about 5% year-over-year to $148 million on delayed design releases, while the display (FPD) mask business rose 13% to $62 million - one of the strongest quarters in the history of that business, with high-end display up 21%. Gross margin was 31%, down from about 37% a year earlier, as a mostly fixed-cost model deleveraged on a softer, lower-mix quarter; operating margin was 20%. Guidance for the next quarter was set flat-to-down again: revenue of $207-215 million and operating margin of 18-20%. The two legs of the business moved in opposite directions in the same quarter.
qualifier: from the Q2 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: PLAB company page / Q2 FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: Why the AI boom can be a near-term headwind
body_text: A counterpoint worth stating plainly: as a photomask maker, Photronics can be squeezed by an AI boom in the near term, because its demand follows semiconductor design releases, not wafer starts. In this quarter three things delayed those releases at once. Fabs were running so full on AI logic and memory that they could not accommodate additional design releases from some customers. A surge in memory prices and related supply constraints pushed device makers to delay new consumer-product launches while they secured memory. And geopolitical uncertainty, including the U.S.-Iran conflict, added to the caution. The result: IC mask revenue fell about 5% even as demand for leading-edge AI chips stayed strong. A broadly cyclical supplier can sit inside the AI supply chain and still be held back by the boom for a time.
qualifier: from the Q2 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: PLAB company page / AI crowd-out mechanism

section_title: High-end masks, and how little a single quarter tells you
body_text: High-end IC masks are Photronics' margin engine, and they also show how variable this business is. High-end IC revenue set a record at $71 million in the first fiscal quarter, up 19% year-over-year, then fell to about $57 million in the second - down roughly 5% year-over-year and about 20% sequentially - on the delayed design releases. Management's standing reminder frames why: demand is inherently variable, visibility is limited with a typical backlog of only one to three weeks, and high-end mask sets carry much higher prices, so even a small number of orders can materially move revenue and earnings. The secular pull toward high-end masks - advanced logic nodes, advanced DRAM, and AI-driven advanced chip packaging - is presented as intact; the near-term timing is not.
qualifier: from the Q1 and Q2 FY2026 earnings calls and filings
vault_ref: PLAB company page / high-end IC mask + visibility caveat

section_title: Broad-cycle, not a photonics play
body_text: An honest placement note: despite sitting in the AI supply chain, Photronics reads as a broadly cyclical semiconductor supplier, not a photonics story. Across its annual filing, its quarterly filings, and its earnings calls, it names zero silicon-photonics or photonic-foundry customers; silicon photonics appears once, listed among broad end-markets. Its AI exposure runs through high-end IC photomasks for advanced chip packaging and leading-edge logic - upstream of many technologies, but not specifically photonic. This is an independent read of the disclosure record rather than a company claim, and it is why the business is best understood as broad-cycle with AI as one sub-segment driver, structurally adjacent to the photonics theme rather than central to it.
qualifier: independent analysis of the disclosure record
vault_ref: PLAB company page / photonics-fit placement

section_title: A broad-cycle book, no customer over 10%
body_text: Cohu is a broad-cycle semiconductor test and inspection company - test handlers, testers, and interface products - serving mobile, automotive, industrial, and computing end markets. Its customer base is about as diffuse as they come: no single customer exceeded 10% of revenue in either 2025 or 2024, and the top ten customers together were roughly 60% of the total. Only STMicroelectronics ever crossed the 10% line historically, at 12% in 2023 - an automotive and industrial chipmaker, not an AI-adjacent account. On a quarterly basis the picture gets lumpy: in one 2025 quarter three customers each topped 10%, the largest at 34%, which reflects episodic handler-system deliveries to specific customers rather than any sustained dependency. Roughly 90% of sales are international. This spread across markets and customers is the opposite of AI-thesis concentration.
qualifier: from the 10-K and quarterly filings
vault_ref: COHU company page / customer concentration + product position

section_title: AI language, aspirational and not yet quantified
body_text: Cohu's filings say the company is 'capitalizing on high-growth opportunities in HBM inspection, high performance processor test in AI applications,' and credit stronger AI-computing demand for 2025 revenue growth - but they never put a number on AI-specific revenue, name an AI customer above 10%, or break out end markets in a way that would let AI revenue be identified. The most concrete AI-specific investment is Tignis, an AI process-control software company acquired for $34.9 million in January 2025 and integrated into the defect-inspection platform for HBM applications; the company discloses that Tignis revenue was 'not material' in 2025. Meanwhile the broad-cycle signals dominate: mobile revenue fell three years running, and automotive and industrial swung down hard before recovering. The picture is a broad semiconductor-cycle company with real but bounded AI exposure - not an AI-concentrated one.
qualifier: from the 10-K
vault_ref: COHU company page / the AI-adjacent question + Tignis

section_title: The HPC pipeline, bounded and staged
body_text: In the first quarter of 2026 Cohu reported revenue of $125.1 million, up about 29% year over year, with orders up 57% and semiconductor-test orders up 163%, driven mainly by computing demand; recurring revenue held at about 60% and non-GAAP gross margin was 46.5%. Management framed a computing-segment pipeline of roughly $750 million - about $650 million in test handlers and $100 million from HBM inspection - across 12 customers, five in qualification and seven in early engagement, and raised the 2026 high-performance-compute revenue outlook to about $80 to $100 million (roughly 17-22% of 2025 revenue) plus full-year growth guidance to 20-25%. Two Eclipse handler orders landed at U.S. fabless customers, one supporting AI data center work with an estimated $100 million of incremental revenue at that account over three years. Management is deliberate that the $750 million is a defined serviceable market it can tally, not the total market, which it declines to guess. A first silicon-photonics product engagement began as a beachhead and is not included in the $750 million.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 quarterly filing and earnings call
vault_ref: COHU company page / Q1 FY2026 HPC pipeline + Eclipse

section_title: The lumpy rotation quarter
body_text: Aehr's most recent quarter, ending February 2026, brought revenue of $10.3 million - down 44% from $18.3 million a year earlier - because the silicon-carbide and electric-vehicle burn-in business that was 92% of fiscal 2024 revenue contracted sharply. In the same quarter, bookings jumped to $37.2 million, about six times the prior quarter, for a book-to-bill above 3.5x, and effective backlog reached a company record of $50.9 million. Those orders were driven mostly by AI and silicon photonics, including a $14 million follow-on order from the lead AI-accelerator customer for wafer-level burn-in systems and a new hyperscaler win on the package-level Sonoma platform. The company guided full fiscal-2026 revenue to the high side of a $45-50 million range and expects to return to a non-GAAP profit in the fourth quarter - but the order book has turned well ahead of the revenue, which is characteristic of a lumpy equipment company entering an up-cycle. Gross margin was 36.5% on a non-GAAP basis, down from 42.7% a year earlier, as the mix shifted toward lower-margin package-level products.
qualifier: from the latest earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: AEHR company page / Q3 FY2026 quarter + bookings inflection

section_title: One customer, forty-two percent
body_text: Customer concentration at Aehr is the highest of any company tracked here, and the filings show it moving fast. In the most recent quarter one customer was 42.1% of revenue - a customer that was under 10% a year earlier - reflecting a new AI-dominant account emerging as an older silicon-carbide customer faded. For full fiscal 2025 the top customer was 38.6% of revenue, down from 78.8% two years earlier, and the top five were 77%, down from 97%. Every customer is anonymous in the filings, and Aehr even uses different anonymous labels in its annual and quarterly disclosures, so tracking a single account across periods is difficult. On its earnings call management referred to TSMC, NVIDIA, Intel, and AMD by name - unusually specific for a small equipment company - but those were market references about the roadmap, not confirmations of who its customers are. The revenue is small and lumpy and the customer list is narrow at the same time; both facts sit in the same filings.
qualifier: from the annual and quarterly concentration disclosures
vault_ref: AEHR company page / customer concentration

section_title: Why the AI burn-in market exists
body_text: Aehr's pitch is simple: burn-in screens out chips that would fail early, and catching a bad die at the wafer is far cheaper than discovering it after the die is stacked into an expensive multi-chip package. As management puts it, a die that fails after being packaged into a module with high-bandwidth memory and other processors can cost ten or even a hundred times more to scrap. The opportunity is that most AI chips are not burned in yet - management estimates only roughly 5-20% of ASICs and about half of AI accelerators go through production burn-in today, and it says the common direction is toward more. Its package-level Sonoma system is built for high-power AI processors, up to 2,000 watts per device, and a contract manufacturer is adding capacity of more than 20 systems a month. Management estimates the package-level market at multiple hundreds of millions of dollars, with the wafer-level opportunity even higher - these are its own estimates, not booked revenue. Whether that penetration actually rises with packaging complexity is the open part of the story.
qualifier: from the latest earnings call; the market-size figures are management's own estimates
vault_ref: AEHR company page / KGD value proposition + burn-in penetration

section_title: The trough, and where the margin pain actually landed
body_text: onsemi's FY2025 was a deep cyclical trough. Net revenue was $5,995.4 million, down about 15% from $7,082.3 million the prior year, and total gross margin fell to 33.1% from 45.4%. The important detail is that the compression was concentrated, not spread evenly. Power Solutions, which carries the silicon carbide and power portfolio, saw gross margin fall roughly 2,250 basis points over two years to 24.5%, driven by silicon-carbide and power end-market cycle pressure (autos and industrial slowing, silicon-carbide oversupply). Intelligent Sensing fell even harder, about 3,360 basis points over two years to 15.1%. Meanwhile Analog and Mixed-Signal held up and was actually up about 460 basis points to 51.1%. So the headline of a broad structural margin collapse does not survive the segment detail - two segments carried the pain while the analog business stayed durable through the cycle.
qualifier: from the FY2025 annual filing's segment disclosures
vault_ref: onsemi company page / FY2025 segment revenue and gross-margin disaggregation

section_title: Q1 rebound: utilization to 77%, margin up a third quarter
body_text: For the quarter ended in early April 2026, onsemi reported revenue of about $1.51 billion, up 5% year-over-year and down 1% sequentially. Non-GAAP gross margin expanded for the third consecutive quarter to 38.5%, and manufacturing utilization increased to 77% as production was ramped to meet stronger demand signals. In the CFO's own words on the call, the gross-margin improvement is a result of structural manufacturing changes made over the prior several years, and sequential gross-margin expansion is expected throughout the year. Guidance for the following quarter is revenue of $1.535 to $1.635 billion, with capital expenditures guided to $25 to $35 million. The company also flagged roughly $50 million of planned non-core revenue exits in the quarter, with $30 to $40 million more expected the next quarter. The framing is a company moving off the bottom of the cycle onto a recovery path - stated as an expectation, not a certainty.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 quarterly filing and earnings call
vault_ref: onsemi company page / Q1 FY2026 financial snapshot and Q2 guidance

section_title: AI datacenter: the growth driver, and the hedge on the number
body_text: onsemi frames AI datacenter as its forward growth driver, and the number needs a careful hedge. Management said on the earnings call that AI datacenter revenue was about $250 million in FY2025 and that it now expects to roughly double that in FY2026, with first-quarter AI datacenter revenue up more than 30% sequentially - nearly double the growth rate expected entering the quarter - across multiple XPU vendors and, in the generic phrasing used, all the leading hyperscalers. The critical caveat: that $250 million figure and the doubling framing come only from the earnings call. The AI datacenter revenue is not disaggregated as a quantified line in onsemi's SEC filings, and no hyperscaler customer is named there. The company positions itself as the only broad-based U.S. power-semiconductor supplier spanning the full power tree from high-voltage conversion to the point of load, but the specific AI dollar figures live in management commentary, not in the filings.
qualifier: from the earnings call; the AI figure is management's framing, not disaggregated in filings
vault_ref: onsemi company page / AI revenue trajectory and cross-venue disclosure gap

section_title: Capital discipline: buybacks well above free cash flow, capex near a floor
body_text: Through the trough, onsemi ran a lean capital model. In the first quarter of FY2026, capital expenditures were $22 million, or about 1.4% of revenue, and the company guided capital intensity to a mid-single-digit percentage of revenue for the foreseeable future. Share repurchases were $346 million in the quarter, described as opportunistic and representing nearly 160% of free cash flow. There is no dividend. The company also began exiting non-core revenue - about $50 million in the first quarter with $30 to $40 million more expected the next quarter - as part of a portfolio cleanup. The posture is a scale incumbent choosing to repurchase its own shares and rationalize its portfolio rather than expand capacity while volumes recover.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 quarterly filing and earnings call
vault_ref: onsemi company page / capital discipline and cycle recovery

section_title: One distributor, 11%, and not named
body_text: onsemi's customer concentration sits in a single distributor. Per the FY2025 annual filing, one customer - a distributor - accounted for about 11% of total revenue, up from 10% in FY2024 and under 10% in FY2023, with sales spread across all three reportable segments rather than one product line. The distributor is not named. On accounts receivable, a single distributor represented about 10% of the balance, down from 13% a year earlier. The concentration is modest but has ticked up two years running, which is worth putting on the table plainly. Separately, an independent research view places onsemi as roughly the number-two maker of power discretes and modules globally at about 8.5% share, behind Infineon - but that share figure is an outside estimate reproduced from a third party, not a company disclosure, and should be read as an estimate.
qualifier: concentration from the FY2025 filing; the market-share figure is an independent estimate
vault_ref: onsemi company page / customer concentration and power-discretes market share

section_title: The full year, stated flat - profitable, but compressing
body_text: Power Integrations' FY2025: net revenue of $443.5 million, up 5.9% year-over-year, with GAAP gross margin of 54.5%, up about 90 basis points. The company stayed profitable through the cycle at $22.1 million of net income - but that was down 31% from the prior year, and operating income has slid three years running, from $35 million to $18 million to $10 million, a sustained margin compression. In the first quarter of 2026, revenue was $108.3 million, up 2.6% year-over-year, and the company took a roughly 7% workforce reduction, recording $6.2 million of restructuring charges, described as aligning expenses with revenue and creating flexibility to invest in the products, people, and markets expected to drive long-term growth. The concentration and the compression sit in the same set of results as the profit - all three are true at once.
qualifier: from the annual filing and the Q1 2026 quarterly filing
vault_ref: POWI company page / FY2025 financial snapshot + Q1 2026 restructuring

section_title: The only volume GaN at 1,250 and 1,700 volts
body_text: Power Integrations describes its PowiGaN as a proprietary gallium-nitride transistor technology with substantially higher active-mode efficiency than traditional silicon switches, integrated into its InnoSwitch family. The differentiated part: it is the only vendor with volume production of 1,250-volt and 1,700-volt GaN - other GaN suppliers all produce 650-volt GaN. That higher voltage class matches an engineering rule of thumb for 800-volt-DC systems, where a margin over 800 volts plus turn-off spikes pushes designers to either 1,200-volt silicon carbide or 1,250-to-1,700-volt GaN for primary-side switching. PowiGaN revenue grew more than 40% in FY2025. Independent analysis, not a filing, is the basis for the only-vendor claim; the company's own filing carries the efficiency description.
qualifier: independent analysis on the only-vendor claim; the efficiency description is from the filing
vault_ref: POWI company page / PowiGaN high-voltage positioning

section_title: Everybody's favorite topic, sized honestly
body_text: Data center is a real but modest end-market participation here, not a central position. It is not a named category in the annual filing, which enumerates only four end markets - Communications 12%, Computer 13%, Consumer 37%, and Industrial 38% - and the entire annual filing mentions 'data center' just once. Any datacenter participation is embedded inside Computer (servers) and Industrial (high-voltage transmission and backup power). On the earnings call the CEO placed the higher-voltage datacenter revenue 'a couple years out,' framed the near-term wins as auxiliary power supplies for today's datacenters, and noted a collaboration with NVIDIA on sockets using the 1,250- and 1,700-volt GaN for coming 800-volt-DC architectures. Management sizes the combined rack-and-grid datacenter addressable market at over $1 billion by 2030 - a forward estimate it says it is still sizing quarter to quarter, not a booked result. Notably, the quarterly filing mentions AI datacenter zero times, so the AI framing lives in company commentary while the formal filings stay silent on it as a category.
qualifier: from the annual filing and the earnings call; the 2030 figure is management's forward estimate
vault_ref: POWI company page / AI datacenter end-market scope + cross-venue gap

section_title: One distributor, 32%, and rising
body_text: Customer concentration is high and moving the wrong way. One distributor, Avnet, was 32% of revenue in 2025, up from 30% in 2024 and 27% in 2023 - a steady climb. In 2025 three customers, all distributors, each accounted for more than 10% of revenue. The channel itself is distributor-heavy: 81% of sales ran through distributors against 19% direct. Geography concentrates too, with roughly 98% of net revenue outside the United States and China cited as a material exposure - the annual filing states plainly that the company expects competition from Chinese semiconductor vendors to intensify over time, reflecting China's stated aim to build its domestic semiconductor industry. Whether the single-distributor share crosses higher from here is an open trajectory the company flags itself.
qualifier: from the annual filing's customer and geographic concentration disclosures
vault_ref: POWI company page / customer + geographic concentration

section_title: Navitas 2.0: the whole company, pointed at high power
body_text: Navitas is a power-semiconductor pure-play with two technologies - gallium nitride power ICs and high-voltage silicon carbide MOSFETs - and its strategic transition, called 'Navitas 2.0,' de-emphasizes its historical mobile and consumer business to refocus the entire organization on four high-power markets: AI datacenters, energy and grid infrastructure, performance computing, and industrial electrification. The dual-technology argument is that gallium nitride and silicon carbide play complementary roles in the 800V DC power chain, so one supplier can address more of that chain and capture more content per system than a single-technology peer. Management frames a $3.5 billion serviceable market by 2030 growing at over 60% a year, split roughly evenly between the two technologies - but this is a forward-looking company framing, not a result reported in the filings. The company also names an explicit risk: it may not successfully execute the transition to these new markets and applications, against incumbent competitors with established resources and long-standing customer relationships.
qualifier: from the annual report and the earnings call; the 2030 market figure is management's framing
vault_ref: NVTS company page / Navitas 2.0 strategic transition + served-market framing

section_title: The numbers, said plainly: recovery on a shrinking base
body_text: The execution picture carries hedges in both directions. Full-year 2025 revenue fell 45% to $45.9 million from $83.3 million, and the net loss widened 38% to $117 million from $84.6 million the year before - the loss got bigger as revenue got smaller. First-quarter 2026 revenue was $8.6 million: up 18% from the prior quarter, but still down 39% from the same quarter a year earlier, which was $14.0 million. So it is a sequential recovery on a small and shrinking base, not yet a year-over-year turn; the second-quarter outlook of about $10 million would extend the sequential recovery but still sits well below the prior-year level. Reported gross margin remains negative on a standard basis, though it improved sequentially. Management's own words are 'too early to declare victory.'
qualifier: from the annual report, the quarterly filing, and the earnings call
vault_ref: NVTS company page / financial snapshot FY2025 + Q1 2026

section_title: The NVIDIA question: a conference venue, not booked revenue
body_text: Here is the NVIDIA relationship exactly as disclosed, with nothing added. The annual report and the quarterly filing name NVIDIA zero times - across the business description, the risk factors, and the financial discussion. NVIDIA appears only in the first-quarter earnings press release, and both mentions are about a venue: participation at NVIDIA's GTC conference in March, and the debut there of a 20-kilowatt 800V-to-6V power delivery board built on Navitas gallium nitride. There is no disclosed design win, no disclosed revenue, and no disclosed joint development - the relationship is conference participation and a demonstration board, not a booked commercial partnership. Separately, cash fell to $221 million at the end of the first quarter of 2026 from $237 million, a roughly $16 million sequential burn, with no debt on the balance sheet; no going-concern flag was raised in the annual report. So the biggest name attached to the story is a trade-show relationship, while the base underneath it is small and burning cash - both facts sit side by side. Whether NVIDIA ever names Navitas in its own filings or on its own call is an open question the company does not control.
qualifier: the NVIDIA mentions are venue-only in the earnings press release and absent from the filings
vault_ref: NVTS company page / NVIDIA disclosure asymmetry + cash position

section_title: The backlog inflection - and the hedge built into guidance
body_text: Vicor's most striking recent number is the order book. One-year backlog rose 70% in a single quarter, closing at $300.6 million, with book-to-bill above 2.0. Quarterly revenue was $113 million, up 5.3% sequentially and 20.2% year over year, with gross margin expanding 800 basis points from a year earlier to 55.2%. Management guides the next quarter to nearly $126 million and full-year 2026 to nearly $570 million. The important hedge travels with that guidance: it is built on conservative assumptions about licensing - specifically that no new licensing agreements are signed before the company's second trade case reaches its final determination in 2027. Management notes a deal could close ahead of that, but chose to leave any such upside out of the number.
qualifier: from the quarterly filing and the earnings call
vault_ref: VICR company page / Q1 FY2026 backlog + guidance

section_title: One customer, 11.1% - and a name that is inferred, not disclosed
body_text: Vicor's customer concentration is real and only partly visible. The filings disclose that one customer was 11.1% of last year's revenue (12.1% and 10.7% in the two prior years) without naming it. On the earnings call, sales leadership described the 'lead computing customer' as continuing a steep production ramp of its wafer-scale engine, and separately referenced Cerebras and the wafer-scale companies. Reading those together, analysts infer the lead customer is Cerebras - but that is an inference from the wafer-scale description, not a name the filings disclose. If that inference holds, the concentration is on a single AI-accelerator vendor, which sharpens the risk. Management declined on the call to break out how much of the backlog that lead customer represents, saying only that it is an important customer but not the only major one.
qualifier: inferred from the call's wafer-scale framing; the filings do not name the customer
vault_ref: VICR company page / customer concentration + lead-customer inference

section_title: Why last year's net income overstates the earning power
body_text: Last year's headline net income of $118.6 million looks like an enormous jump, but two one-time items flatter it. First, a $45 million patent-litigation settlement that Vicor PAID OUT - it was the defendant in that case, so the money went out, not in. Second, a $24 million tax benefit from reversing a valuation allowance. Strip both and underlying operating income was $81.8 million, against a small operating loss the year before. So the recovery is real - revenue up 13.5% to $407.7 million, gross margin restored - but the right way to read the profit line is as a cyclical recovery off a weak base, not steady-state earning power.
qualifier: from the annual and quarterly filings
vault_ref: VICR company page / FY2025 quality-of-earnings framing

section_title: The physics of vertical power delivery, and who else sells it
body_text: The technical argument runs on physics. At an AI processor drawing 2,000-plus amps at roughly 0.6 volts, even a single milliohm of trace resistance dissipates about a kilowatt of waste heat - which forces the power converter to sit underneath the processor package, with current flowing up vertically through the board rather than sideways. Vicor's second-generation vertical-power-delivery part is described with 3 amps per square millimeter of current density, a current-multiplication factor up to 40, in a package 1.5 mm thin, and management argues you have to judge a solution on all three of those together, not any one alone. The honest bound: vertical-power modules are not Vicor-only - Empower, Infineon, ADI, TDK and Flex all supply them - and the claimed advantage is specifically at the highest current-density processor loads, where management says its approach is the one that meets the requirement. Whether rivals close that gap at the frontier is an open question.
qualifier: from the earnings call plus independent technical analysis on the multi-supplier landscape
vault_ref: VICR company page / VPD Gen-5 physics + multi-supplier landscape

section_title: The licensing leg rests on trade cases still in flight
body_text: Vicor runs a combinatorial model: it makes power modules and it licenses the patents behind them, and the two are meant to reinforce each other. The licensing leg rests on trade cases the company brought as the plaintiff. A first case was carried to a first exclusion order, with a customs action still pending on it. A second case is under investigation, with a final determination expected in 2027 that management expects will produce a second exclusion order - and the CEO hinted at a third, in his words 'there is no two without three.' Royalties are described as very high margin, and the CEO's stated vision is that in time hyperscalers and equipment makers will largely become licensees. All of that is forward-looking and contingent: full-year guidance is deliberately built to assume no new licensing agreements until that 2027 determination. Whether the enforcement campaign keeps succeeding is the unresolved question the whole licensing story turns on.
qualifier: forward outcomes are unresolved; guidance assumes no new deals before 2027
vault_ref: VICR company page / ITC enforcement + royalty licensing strategy

section_title: The year the copper ramp scaled
body_text: Credo's fiscal 2026 revenue was $1,335.1 million, up 206% year-over-year, with GAAP net income of $472.3 million against $52.2 million the prior year - the accumulated deficit was wiped out. The fourth quarter reached a record $437 million and by itself exceeded the entire fiscal 2025. Gross margin was about 68%, and operating margin rose to 33.3% from 8.5% as research and general costs fell sharply as a share of revenue - the operating leverage was the story. The filing states that over 99% of the revenue increase came from the active-electrical-cable ramp at hyperscale data-center customers, so this was a copper-led year. One comparability note carried plainly: fiscal 2026 was a 52-week year against fiscal 2025's 53 weeks, so per-week growth is slightly higher than the 206% headline.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 annual filing and the Q4 earnings call
vault_ref: CRDO company page / FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: The concentration, read straight
body_text: Credo's customer base is broadening but not yet de-risked, and the numbers are read out in full. The fiscal 2026 annual filing shows two customers at 10% or more of revenue - Customer A at 49% and Customer B at 32% - versus a single customer at 67% a year earlier. On an end-customer basis in the fourth quarter, management reported four customers each at or above 10% of revenue, at 34%, 27%, 16%, and 10%, with the fourth newly crossing the threshold; the three largest were the same as the prior quarter. The two lenses differ in basis (the filing's largest customer is consistent with a contract manufacturer aggregating several hyperscalers), but they point the same way: the top-of-book fell from 67% toward 34%, while the top four together are still roughly 87% of revenue. The honest framing is that it is genuinely broadening yet still extreme - and the new optical ramp could re-concentrate revenue by product even as the customer list diversifies. One end customer, xAI, was named as the single account where the company describes itself as fully deployed.
qualifier: from the annual filing's concentration note and the Q4 earnings call
vault_ref: CRDO company page / customer concentration

section_title: The optical bet the +80% guide leans on
body_text: The fiscal 2027 outlook is more than 80% revenue growth, and about half of the added dollars are expected to come from a brand-new optical ramp of more than $600 million. It has three legs - discrete optical DSPs, silicon-photonics chips, and ZeroFlap optical transceivers - each expected to contribute more than $100 million, with the ZeroFlap leg the largest and the ramp weighted to the second half of the year (mid-single-digit sequential growth in the first half, an optical inflection in the second). This is forward guidance, not a realized result, and it is a fresh single-vector dependency: the de-risking that customer-broadening was meant to provide could be offset if this optical ramp becomes the new concentration. Management declined to rank the three legs precisely, giving the aggregate instead and saying each is growing faster than the full-year rate. Gross margin is guided broadly consistent with the prior year (around 68%) and net margin in the vicinity of 50%. The guide is stated on the current tariff regime, which remains fluid.
qualifier: from the Q4 earnings call; the optical figures are forward guidance
vault_ref: CRDO company page / FY2027 guidance + optical pivot

section_title: One foundry, four nodes, a tight supply chain
body_text: All of Credo's integrated-circuit manufacturing is outsourced to a single foundry - the filing states it currently outsources all IC manufacturing to one supplier - across four process nodes: 12-nanometer as the workhorse for today's 100-gig-per-lane copper parts, 7-nanometer for optical DSPs, a 5-nanometer program in flight, and 3-nanometer for all 200-gig-per-lane products and the bulk of 1.6-terabit. Assembly and test run through several outside partners, and the filing calls out capacity-shortage, price, and yield risk explicitly. There is significant industry-wide supply tightness; the stated way of managing it is owning the entire bill of materials, sourcing it directly, and committing to capacity early - with management noting that clusters do not get built without the small complementary connectivity chips it makes. The confidence is grounded in a multi-year investment in those supplier relationships rather than any guarantee.
qualifier: from the annual filing's risk factors and the Q4 earnings call
vault_ref: CRDO company page / single-foundry dependency + supply

section_title: The trough, then the inflection - both halves
body_text: Lattice's recent story is a cyclical recovery off a weak base, and the company narrates both halves. FY2025, the fiscal year ended January 3, 2026, was a trough and rebuild year: total revenue was $523.3 million, up just 2.7%, roughly flat off the FY2023 peak of $737.2 million, and GAAP net income collapsed to $3.1 million as research and operating spending rose faster than the recovering top line. Then Q1 FY2026, the quarter ended April 4, 2026, inflected: total revenue was $170.9 million, up 42% year-over-year and 17% sequentially, with GAAP net income of $21.8 million. The driver was the Compute and Communications end market at $106.6 million, up 86% year-over-year and 62% of revenue, on data-center AI and hyperscaler demand. Channel inventory came down from about three months to close to two, which management describes as a headwind clearing rather than a new tailwind. Guidance for the following quarter is $175 million to $195 million, a midpoint near 50% year-over-year growth.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: LSCC company page / FY2025 trough + Q1 FY2026 inflection

section_title: About 64% Greater China, and fabbed in Taiwan
body_text: The honest counter-signal sits in the same quarter as the growth. Greater China was about 64% of Q1 FY2026 revenue, up sharply from 48% a year earlier - so the AI and hyperscaler growth is disproportionately a China-channel story - versus 52% for full-year FY2025. On top of that, roughly 94% of quarterly revenue runs through distributors, with two distributors accounting for about 69% of FY2025 revenue: concentration on top of concentration. Lattice is fabless, with its silicon built by TSMC, Samsung, and UMC in Taiwan and Korea, plus Seiko Epson in Japan, and assembled and tested by outsourced partners in Malaysia, Taiwan, and Japan. Its own filing flags Taiwan-fab exposure, China-Taiwan geopolitical risk, and an additional Section 301 tariff on Chinese semiconductors set to begin June 23, 2027. For a company whose secure-control chips could serve a trusted domestic supply chain, the 64% China revenue and Taiwan fabrication cut directly against that framing - and the company states both facts side by side.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 quarterly filing and the FY2025 annual filing
vault_ref: LSCC company page / Greater China concentration + fabless foundry dependence

section_title: Secure FPGAs: real capability, not yet defense revenue
body_text: Lattice's Control and Security FPGA line is a genuine trusted-microelectronics capability. The MachXO5D-NX carries crypto-agile algorithms and a hardware root of trust with integrated flash and secure remote field updates; the MachXO5-NX TDQ is described as the industry's first secure control FPGA with full CNSA 2.0-compliant post-quantum cryptography support; and Lattice Sentry is an integrated system-level hardware-security stack. That capability is exactly the kind of secure silicon a trusted-microelectronics thesis prizes. But the honest read is that it is pitched broadly - across communications, computing, industrial, automotive, and consumer - not as defense procurement. The company does not cite any defense-budget tailwind, carries no NDAA, DMEA, or Trusted-Foundry framing, does not break out defense and aerospace revenue (it is folded into Industrial and Embedded, mentioned only as aerospace customers in a recovery comment), and makes zero mention of drones or unmanned systems in its filings. Whether the capability ever converts into material defense revenue is an open question the filings do not yet answer.
qualifier: from the FY2025 annual filing; the defense-revenue absence is stated, not inferred
vault_ref: LSCC company page / secure-microelectronics capability vs unquantified defense revenue

section_title: What a specialty foundry actually is
body_text: Tower Semiconductor is a specialty analog foundry, deliberately not a leading-edge one - it competes on specialty process rather than on the smallest transistor node. Its platforms span analog, RF SOI, silicon-germanium BiCMOS, mixed-signal, power-management BCD, CMOS image sensors, and a silicon-photonics process, all at mature process nodes on 200mm and selected 300mm wafers. It runs seven manufacturing fabs across four geographies: Israel, California, Texas, Japan, and Italy - the Italian fab a shared cleanroom with STMicroelectronics. It reports as a single segment, analog foundry operations, with no breakdown by end-market in its financials. Its customers are described only as fabless semiconductor companies and integrated device makers; no hyperscaler is named as a customer. This is a foundry that positions early in the supply chain, at the wafer tier, and it explains itself from the fab floor.
qualifier: from the FY2025 annual filing
vault_ref: TSEM company page / specialty foundry commercial structure

section_title: The numbers, in the CFO's model register
body_text: For full-year 2025, Tower reported revenue of about $1.57 billion, up 9% year-over-year, with gross margin near 23% and operating margin around 12%. In the first quarter of 2026, revenue was $414 million, up 15% year-over-year, with gross profit up 52% and operating profit up 96%; gross margin rose to 27% from 20% a year earlier, and net margin was 16% versus 11%. Management guided second-quarter 2026 revenue to about $455 million plus or minus 5% - characterized as the highest quarterly revenue in the company's history, up 22% year-over-year - and reiterated a target of sequential revenue and margin growth through 2026. The margin path is framed against a published model: incremental revenue is assumed to come in at 59% to gross profit over a 20% baseline, progressing toward 39% at full build-out. The Q2 figure is forward guidance, not a reported result.
qualifier: from the FY2025 filing and the Q1 2026 earnings call; the Q2 figure is guidance
vault_ref: TSEM company page / financial snapshot + FY model targets

section_title: Silicon photonics: the contract, not the forecast
body_text: Silicon photonics is the fastest-growing part of the business, up about three times year-over-year in the first quarter of 2026, with first silicon-photonics revenue shipments from the Israeli Fab 2 and the Japanese Fab 7, the latter reporting 95% yield on its first photonics wafers. Long-term customer commitments represent about $1.3 billion of revenue in 2027, with larger contracts for 2028, backed by roughly $290 million in prepayments already received from the largest silicon-photonics customers. Management is careful to draw a line the numbers alone would blur: the $1.3 billion is a contractual commitment, not the full forecast, which it says is higher - and it is not even the full expressed demand of those customers. Against a 2025 photonics baseline of about $230 million, this is roughly a six-fold step over two years. The company operates two photonics platforms it describes as leading in the 400 gigabit-to-1.6 terabit market, and runs more than 50 active photonics customers. The additional 2028 prepayments are due by January 2027 - a dated checkpoint for whether those commitments are hardening on schedule.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and a related filing
vault_ref: TSEM company page / silicon photonics PDK foundry sub-segment

section_title: NVIDIA and TSMC: named as partner, careful about the rest
body_text: NVIDIA publicly described Tower as a development partner, and Tower's own prepared remarks tied that announcement to the 1.6-terabit silicon-photonics node, stating it is by far the majority supplier of 1.6T silicon photonic integrated circuits. Beyond the joint announcement, management declines to discuss program specifics, saying it does not have the freedom to talk about specific customer programs. Tower does not ship photonics directly to NVIDIA - its chips are designed by and shipped through other module makers and integrators; its role is the output parameters of the photonics, the drivers, and the amplifiers within a customer's module, not building the transceiver. On TSMC, whose one-stop-shop scale it grants openly ('nobody can compete with TSMC on the extreme deep digital content'), management frames the relationship as complementary rather than head-to-head: it aims to be the reference design for the major integrators, meaning its photonics content could be part of what those integrators use with TSMC's advanced packaging. Whether either relationship ever reaches a top-tier disclosure - a named customer concentration or a filed agreement - is not yet known.
qualifier: from the Q4 2025 and Q1 2026 earnings calls; some elements are management framing
vault_ref: TSEM company page / NVIDIA reciprocal-confirmation + TSMC reference-design complementarity

section_title: Israel risk and the Intel obligation - the live uncertainties
body_text: Tower's primary manufacturing facilities sit in Israel, and its FY2025 filing carries a substantial disclosure on regional conflict: it states that hostilities that escalated in early 2026 prevented vendors from arriving at its fab to install equipment, which may affect the timely execution of its roughly $920 million silicon-photonics capacity plan. Temporary ceasefires reached in April 2026 were noted as in effect. Notably, none of the five analysts on the following earnings call probed this geopolitical risk, despite the length of the written disclosure. Separately, a legacy of Tower's terminated 2022 merger with Intel is a $300 million equipment commitment tied to an Intel facility in New Mexico; as of year-end 2025, Intel had expressed an intention not to perform under the agreement, and the two were in mediation. Both the capacity-plan execution risk and the mediation outcome are unresolved. This is a company that plans around real uncertainties in its own manufacturing base and discloses them plainly.
qualifier: from the FY2025 annual filing
vault_ref: TSEM company page / Israeli geopolitical risk + Intel New Mexico mediation

section_title: The quarter InP crossed half the business
body_text: AXT's quarter ending March 2026 was the profitability inflection. Total revenue was $26.9 million, up 39% year-over-year from $19.4 million. Non-GAAP gross margin rebounded to 29.9%, from negative territory a year earlier, driven mainly by two things: volume up, and a mix now rich toward indium phosphide. InP came in at $13.6 million - a tad north of 50% of total revenue - primarily from datacenter applications, alongside gallium arsenide at $5.4 million and germanium at $0.2 million. The InP backlog reached a new high of over $100 million, and the next quarter is guided to be the largest InP quarter in the company's history, past the roughly $17 million pandemic-era record. The company also guided the June quarter to profitability on both a GAAP and non-GAAP basis - built, though, on the roughly $34 million of revenue for which it already holds permits or needs none.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: AXTI company page / Q1 FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: Three suppliers, or two - both on the record
body_text: There is a divergence in AXT's own disclosures about how many InP substrate suppliers exist, and it is worth holding both halves. The annual filing states there are three primary suppliers of InP substrates worldwide, naming two Japanese competitors, Sumitomo Electric and JX Nippon, as the principal ones. On the earnings call, the CFO said instead: 'Today there are two InP substrate suppliers in the world, and we're one of them.' The filing acknowledges a third competitor that the call leaves out. The open question is whether that third supplier is commercially marginal - making the 'two' directionally right - or whether the call's framing narrows the field to assert more dominance than the filing supports. Both statements stand as management's; neither has been reconciled.
qualifier: filing says three, the call says two - unreconciled
vault_ref: AXTI company page / competitive landscape

section_title: The export permit is the gating factor
body_text: All of AXT's manufacturing is in China, and that makes the export permit the single largest factor in its growth. In February 2025 China added InP substrates to its export-control list, so every non-China shipment now needs a permit. The pattern splits three ways: permits for Europe, Japan, the UK and Canada continue to come readily; shipments within China need no permit at all, which is why the China leg runs unconstrained and its InP-laser revenue more than doubled quarter over quarter; and US-destined InP permits remain pending - reframed from earlier denials to still-pending, with China's Ministry of Commerce requesting more data, which management calls an encouraging sign rather than a permit in hand. The consequence is stark: only about 2% of last year's revenue reached North America. Management stresses that permit timing is 'not predictable, nor in our control, and doesn't align with our quarterly reporting.'
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 call and filing; the US permit is pending, not granted
vault_ref: AXTI company page / export controls and geography

section_title: The raise, the roadmap, and the 10x claim
body_text: In April 2026 AXT completed an offering raising roughly $632.5 million gross. The proceeds fund a multi-year capacity roadmap for indium phosphide: doubling to about $35 million per quarter by the end of 2026 in a repurposed Beijing site; doubling again to $65-70 million per quarter by the end of 2027 or early 2028 in a facility next door; and a further greenfield step planned for 2028, possibly outside China. The honest framing management itself gives: the 2026 doubling is funded and committed, the 2027 step is planned and funded but not yet contracted, and 2028 is direction. Management characterizes underlying demand as roughly 10x the planned doubling - stated as a management claim - and cautions repeatedly that 'adding capacity versus able to deliver wafers is two different things.' The long-term supply agreements discussed with larger customers and hyperscalers are, as disclosed, not yet signed, and no customer is named.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 call and filing; forward phases and the 10x demand figure are management's framing
vault_ref: AXTI company page / capital raise and capacity roadmap

section_title: Most of the company is not an AI story - and it is in a trough
body_text: Soitec makes engineered substrates: the specialized wafers that chips are built on. For the year ended March 2026 the company sat at a cyclical bottom - revenue fell 34% to 592 million euros, gross margin compressed to 16.3% from 32.1% a year earlier on low fab loading, and the group posted a net loss of 220 million euros, including roughly 105 million of non-recurring impairments. Roughly 52% of revenue is RF-SOI for mobile - the substrate under nearly every 4G and 5G phone - and that part is mid-way through a customer inventory correction. The balance sheet, though, held: free cash flow was positive at plus 63 million euros, and net debt to EBITDA was 0.4x. The company should not be read as an AI pure-play; most of it is mobile and cyclical, with a smaller structural AI leg alongside.
qualifier: from the full-year results for the period ended March 2026
vault_ref: Soitec company page / FY2026 financial snapshot + honest framing

section_title: The AI leg: the substrate under silicon photonics
body_text: Soitec's AI-datacenter exposure is one leg, not the whole company: Photonics-SOI, the SOI wafer that silicon-photonics optical parts are fabricated on - whether they end up in a pluggable transceiver or in a co-packaged optics engine. It passed 100 million dollars in revenue in the latest fiscal year, earlier than management had anticipated, and is supply-constrained; it is about 16% of total revenue. Management describes more than five customers engaged in its silicon-photonics solutions with 'more and more coming,' and sizes the market at a 20 to 30% yearly growth rate. Co-packaged optics inserts the interconnect at the substrate level, which management expects to pull more content per unit than today's pluggables; the CPO timeline has moved forward, with 'first designs commercially available by end of this calendar year.' The content uplift and pull-forward are a forward view, not yet a booked result.
qualifier: from the full-year results and the Q3 FY2026 earnings call; the content-uplift and pull-forward are management's forward framing
vault_ref: Soitec company page / Photonics-SOI and CPO exposure

section_title: The mobile inventory walk, and the Smart Cut moat
body_text: The near-term earnings swing sits in mobile RF-SOI, and management quantified the bottom unusually precisely: customer inventories of about 2.5 million 8-inch-equivalent wafers fell to 2.3 million in September and around 2 million in December, with Soitec undershipping 200 to 300 thousand wafers a quarter and aiming for roughly 1 million - the pre-COVID normal - by the end of calendar 2026. RF growth 'should' resume in calendar 2027, in management's words, 'but we don't know exactly when,' and fab loading stays below normal into fiscal 2027, keeping margins pressured. The structural moat is the Smart Cut process - atomic-layer wafer transfer. Soitec has exactly one licensee, Shin-Etsu, and terminated GlobalWafers' license in 2024; GlobalWafers loses the right to use any Soitec IP by July 2027, even as it builds US SOI capacity. Management says it sees 'no big changes in segment shares' and has doubled yearly R&D from about 60 to 120 million euros over four years.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call; recovery timing is management's stated assumption
vault_ref: Soitec company page / RF-SOI inventory correction + Smart Cut franchise

section_title: What we are, and where the moat actually sits
body_text: X-FAB is a pure-play analog and mixed-signal specialty foundry - process nodes from 1.0 micron down to 110nm, on 150mm and 200mm wafers, across six fabs in four countries: Erfurt, Dresden and Itzehoe in Germany, Corbeil-Essonnes in France, Kuching in Malaysia, and Lubbock, Texas. It serves automotive, industrial and medical end markets, which together were 94% of revenue. Its position rests on requalification friction, not on contracts: automotive product lifecycles run more than 15 years and are costly and slow to move to another foundry, and X-FAB is sole-source on most of its products. But no customer is contractually barred from buying from another foundry, and customers have switched with little or no notice historically. The moat therefore works at the product level, not the contractual one - and the low-60s-percent utilization shows it is not currently translating into pricing power. The portfolio spans Smart CMOS and SOI, microsystems and photonics, and wide-bandgap silicon carbide and gallium nitride, and the company recently reorganized into three business units built around those technology families.
qualifier: from the annual report and the earnings call
vault_ref: XFAB company page / specialty foundry positioning + customer concentration

section_title: The largest customer, and how the decline reads two ways
body_text: X-FAB's largest customer, Melexis, was 43% of FY2025 revenue - about $377 million; the top three were 53% and the top five 58%. One customer exceeded 10% of consolidated revenue. Into Q1 2026 the largest customer's quarterly share fell from 41% to 35%. Management framed that as strategically important diversification, pointing to customers ranked two through twenty growing their combined revenue 21% quarter-over-quarter. The fuller reading is that the decline is driven in large part by involuntary automotive destocking at that customer - automotive revenue fell 10% year-over-year in the quarter - not purely by deliberate diversification. Both readings hold at once: the growth in the rest of the top twenty is genuine, while the largest-customer drop is destocking-led. It is a real concentration risk that partly showed up as diversification progress, and the annual report's risk factors disclose the concentration plainly.
qualifier: from the annual report risk factors and the earnings call
vault_ref: XFAB company page / customer concentration + framing gap

section_title: The AI-datacenter slice: real, but thin - and off a soft base
body_text: X-FAB's AI-datacenter exposure is a thin but growing slice of an automotive-led business. Wide-bandgap revenue reached $15.1 million in Q1 2026, up 152% year-over-year, with silicon-carbide wafer shipments up 195% to 14,300 units - but that is off a depressed base: FY2025 silicon-carbide revenue was $33.8 million, down 34% after an exceptionally strong prior-year first quarter, so the high growth rate is a low-base rebound as much as a new trend. Wide-bandgap was about 4% of FY2025 revenue. The data-center link is concrete on the technology, not the mix: silicon carbide is used for solid-state breakers and for energy conversion from 800 volts down to system voltages of 48, 12 or 6 volts, Navitas is a publicly named silicon-carbide customer, and a chip built on X-FAB technology is used for atomic-clock-precision timing to synchronize GPUs in racks - though that timing-chip customer is not disclosed. Capacity is built to run up to 10,000 silicon-carbide wafers a month; about 6,000 are loaded today, roughly 60%, leaving headroom for growth. Whether the data-center-specific share climbs is still an open question.
qualifier: from the earnings call and quarterly release; the datacenter-specific mix is not disclosed
vault_ref: XFAB company page / datacenter exposure (wide-bandgap)

section_title: Two unrelated businesses, and the AI leg is the smaller one
body_text: Sivers runs two unrelated segments. Wireless - roughly 70% of revenue and up 53% in 2025 - makes millimeter-wave beamforming chips for satellite communications, defense and fixed-wireless. Photonics - roughly 30%, up 17% - makes semiconductor lasers. Only Photonics touches the AI datacenter story; the Wireless majority is satellite, defense and RF. Full-year 2025 net sales were about 307 million Swedish kronor, up 40% year over year, which at roughly ten kronor to the dollar is about $30 million total, with Photonics near 92 million kronor (about $9 million). Photonics' fourth quarter was up 52% year over year, but the base is small and weighted toward engineering fees, automotive LiDAR and bio-sensing rather than datacenter lasers. A reader should not mistake this for a datacenter pure-play: it is a satellite-and-defense RF chip company with an early-stage datacenter-laser bet attached.
qualifier: from the audited FY2025 annual report
vault_ref: SIVE company page / segment split and FY2025 revenue

section_title: The datacenter-laser leg is pre-revenue
body_text: The Photonics segment designs and makes high-power DFB lasers, continuous-wave DFB laser arrays and semiconductor optical amplifiers - the external light source that feeds pluggable transceivers and co-packaged optics. AI-datacenter laser revenue today is zero. Production readiness is targeted for the end of 2026, and the company expects a subset of the transceiver makers it is sampling to commit to production plans with its lasers in 2027 and beyond. Management frames the opportunity around a coming continuous-wave laser supply shortage as datacenter links move from 800G to 1.6T and eventually to 3.2T - the challenger's view of the same bottleneck, seen from the outside.
