Manufacturing runs at the company's own fab in Glasgow plus a high-volume foundry partnership with WIN Semiconductors announced in March 2025. This is a real technology fit for the laser bottleneck, but the revenue timeline is 2027 at the earliest, and it is management's forward expectation, not a booked result.
qualifier: from the FY2025 annual report and earnings call; the 2027 revenue is management's forward expectation
vault_ref: SIVE company page / Photonics franchise and AIDC laser timeline

section_title: Five years of losses and a going-concern flag
body_text: Sivers has run operating losses above 140 million Swedish kronor for five straight years; the 2025 operating loss was about 178 million kronor, and net loss was about 223 million. Adjusted EBITDA for 2025 was negative 50 million, though the fourth quarter alone turned positive at plus 10.8 million. Cash was about 30 million kronor - roughly $3 million. The audited annual report discloses a material uncertainty related to going concern: the group has not yet achieved sales revenue sufficient to cover its development plans and operating costs, depends on its continued ability to raise additional capital, and states there is a material risk it may not be able to continue operations to the planned extent. A $17 million refinancing completed after year-end replaced about $11.5 million of existing loans through a three-year facility, alongside a $12 million convertible struck at a premium to the share price that converts only at a materially higher valuation - extending the runway by adding debt rather than an equity cushion. Notably, the earnings call framed this financing as a strength and did not mention the going-concern uncertainty the annual report discloses plainly.
qualifier: from the audited FY2025 annual report and the earnings call
vault_ref: SIVE company page / going concern and financing

section_title: A sub-scale challenger to the laser incumbents
body_text: Sivers positions itself as an aspiring merchant challenger to the laser incumbents Lumentum and Coherent - on its earnings call it explicitly cited Lumentum as a company it aspires to grow like - both of which a major chip buyer backed with $4 billion to secure laser supply. Its co-packaged-optics angle is the external-light-source layer: it supplies the laser while partners supply the interposer or optical engine. It names partners including POET Technologies, O-Net Technologies and Ayar Labs, plus LIGHTIUM on a thin-film lithium niobate platform. Every one of these relationships is disclosed only by Sivers and is not independently confirmed in outside primary sources. On the call the company also said an unnamed 'big household name brand' had entered discussions about its technology roadmap - but the name is undisclosed and it is discussions, not a design-win or an order, so it is management signaling rather than confirmed demand. It remains a sub-scale entrant against much larger incumbents.
qualifier: partner relationships are disclosed only by Sivers, not independently confirmed
vault_ref: SIVE company page / CPO partners and competitive positioning

section_title: The open question: does the challenger ever reach production?
body_text: The core test of the bull case is a pre-registered question: does a sub-scale merchant win co-packaged-optics or pluggable design-wins before the incumbents' capacity expansion and the transceiver integrators' own vertical integration absorb the laser shortage first? Alongside that, several quality-of-earnings items are still being worked through. The company first reported 2025 net sales of 304.1 million Swedish kronor and later restated them to 306.6 million, tied to a disclosed correction of financial figures that redistributed income between periods; internal-control material weaknesses are under remediation; and a possible New York dual listing is in play. The nearest concrete photonics revenue is not datacenter at all - it is an automotive LiDAR customer set to begin production ramps in the fourth quarter of 2026, with a non-binding revenue potential the company puts at roughly $23 million baseline to $58 million upside across 2026 to 2030. None of this is booked; it is the forward case, and it depends on execution the company does not fully control.
qualifier: open questions from the company's own disclosures; forward figures are non-binding
vault_ref: SIVE company page / open questions and restatement

section_title: The AI order book - and why the sequential dip doesn't read as a trend
body_text: Cisco's hyperscaler AI infrastructure orders were $1.9 billion in the quarter ending April 2026, compared with $600 million a year earlier. Year-to-date hyperscaler AI orders reached $5.3 billion - already past the company's prior $5 billion expectation for the full fiscal year, with a quarter still to go. On that strength, management raised the full-year hyperscaler AI order target to about $9 billion, which it frames as 4.5 times the prior year's total, and raised the AI revenue target to about $4 billion; it also gave an early signal of at least $6 billion of hyperscaler AI revenue the following year. One hedge travels with all of this: management describes the order book as nonlinear and lumpy quarter to quarter, so the meaningful comparison is year-over-year and year-to-date, not the modest sequential step down from the prior quarter's $2.1 billion. Beyond hyperscalers, roughly $300 million in AI orders came from neocloud, sovereign, and enterprise customers in the quarter, about $900 million year-to-date, against a roughly $3 billion pipeline.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: CSCO company page / AI infrastructure trajectory

section_title: The counterweight - AI demand fills the book and squeezes the margin
body_text: The same AI wave that grew the order book pressured the margin. Non-GAAP gross margin was 66.0% in the quarter, down 260 basis points year-over-year, and non-GAAP product gross margin was 64.3%, down 330 basis points. Management is explicit about the split of causes: in the CFO's words, 'the bigger factor actually is mix' - hardware revenue grew roughly 30% while software was about flat, tilting the blend toward lower-margin hardware - and on top of that sits what management called 'unprecedented' memory pricing as the market shifts from DDR4 to DDR5. The company says it is mitigating this industry-wide dynamic through about 20 programs, price increases, tightened contractual terms, and advance purchase commitments, and adds productivity improvements as a partial offset. The number is stated, the driver is named, and the offset is named alongside it - the decline is not dressed up.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: CSCO company page / Q3 FY2026 gross-margin drivers

section_title: Committing ahead on supply - the $16 billion on the balance sheet
body_text: To lock supply against the memory squeeze and the Silicon One ramp, Cisco's purchase commitments surged to about $16.0 billion, up from $7.6 billion in July 2025 - roughly $6 billion of that increase in the last 90 days alone. The commitments are explicitly tied to manufacturing Silicon One and meeting hyperscaler demand, and management frames the move as leaning in from a position of financial strength - securing long-term agreements where possible across silicon and memory - rather than as a demand signal on its own. Earlier in the fiscal year the figure had stepped from $7.6 billion to about $10.1 billion, so the jump to $16.0 billion continues a sharp, deliberate build. Most of the commitment is front-loaded to come due within a year. No specific webscale customers are named as the source of the commitments in the disclosures.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 quarterly filing and earnings call
vault_ref: CSCO company page / purchase commitments

section_title: Acacia optics and the first named Silicon One hyperscaler wins
body_text: Cisco's Acacia coherent-optics business took more than $1 billion in orders in the quarter and is described as on track to grow over 200% year-over-year for the fiscal year. The company reports shipping more than 750,000 400-gig and more than 40,000 800-gig coherent pluggable optics to date, which it believes far exceeds the next-largest supplier for both speeds. Alongside optics, Cisco disclosed its first named Silicon One hyperscaler design wins: five in the quarter - two for optics, each with a different hyperscaler, and three for systems, including the first two Silicon One P200-powered wins for scale-across use cases (two different hyperscalers) and a Silicon One G200-powered win for scale-out - plus a third P200 win landing in early the following quarter. Two hedges travel with this: Acacia has no separate revenue line (it sits inside the Networking segment), and the design wins are for Silicon One-powered Cisco systems sold to hyperscalers, not standalone merchant silicon - so the wins strengthen the capability story without yet proving out merchant switching-silicon share against the incumbent's Tomahawk line.
qualifier: from the Q3 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: CSCO company page / Acacia optics + Silicon One design wins

section_title: The quarter, and the two-segment company underneath it
body_text: Nokia's first quarter of 2026 landed at EUR 4,497 million in net sales, up about 2% reported and 4% on a constant-currency, comparable-portfolio basis. Comparable gross margin was 45.5% (up 320 basis points), comparable operating profit was EUR 281 million at a 6.2% margin (up 200 basis points), and free cash flow was EUR 629 million, helped by the usual first-quarter working-capital unwind. The gross-margin improvement was supported in part by the absence of a one-time Mobile charge in the prior year and by early Infinera integration synergies in optical. This was the first quarter reported under a new two-segment structure: Network Infrastructure (the growth segment - Optical, IP, and Fixed Networks) at EUR 1,829 million, or 40.7% of the group, and Mobile Infrastructure at EUR 2,495 million, or 55.5%. The company's own note is worth keeping: the profitability is improving off a modest base, and the mobile side is still the larger half of the business.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 interim report and earnings call
vault_ref: NOK company page / Q1 2026 financial snapshot + two-segment structure

section_title: AI and Cloud: growing fast, still a minority
body_text: Nokia reports revenue by customer type, and the split is the honest part of the story. AI and Cloud grew 49% year over year on a constant-currency basis (94% reported) to EUR 350 million, and Nokia booked roughly EUR 1 billion of new AI and Cloud orders in the quarter (EUR 2.4 billion across full-year 2025). But that AI and Cloud segment was only 7.8% of consolidated revenue. Telecommunication Providers were still the dominant customer type at EUR 3,267 million, or 72.6%, and that group declined 2%. Mission Critical Enterprise and Defense grew 19% to EUR 498 million (11.1%), and Technology Licensees grew 10%. So the AI exposure is genuine and accelerating, and it is diluted at the consolidated level by a telecom business that remains the bulk of sales - both facts sit in the same report.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 customer-type disclosures and earnings call
vault_ref: NOK company page / AI & Cloud customer-type segment + telecom dilution

section_title: Optical: the engine of the pivot, and the names it won't give
body_text: Optical Networks is where Nokia leans into the AI data-center build-out. The unit grew 20% on a constant-currency basis in the quarter (56% reported, once Infinera is included), and Nokia describes itself as holding the number-two global position in optical and the number-one global position in IP edge routing. Its headline customer figure is category-level: it states that 9 of the top 10 global hyperscalers use its optical networks - a quantified claim about the category, without disclosing which hyperscalers or their individual share. The technology behind it includes 800G ZR/ZR+ coherent pluggables shipping today, coherent DSPs, and a new indium-phosphide manufacturing fab in San Jose that is on track to begin ramping later in the year; management calls itself one of only a few manufacturers with indium-phosphide capability at scale. At the March OFC event it introduced four new DSPs and a building-block roadmap of 13 solutions that begin sampling in the first half of 2027 and ship in volume in the second half - so much of that portfolio is aimed at 2027, not the current year.
qualifier: from the FY2025 annual report and Q1 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: NOK company page / Optical Networks positioning + hyperscaler category disclosure

section_title: A raise inside an unchanged range
body_text: Nokia raised two full-year 2026 growth assumptions while holding its group profit range steady - and management guarded that distinction carefully. Network Infrastructure growth was lifted to 12-14% (from a prior 6-8%), and Optical plus IP Networks combined to 18-20% (from 10-12%), on the back of stronger AI and cloud demand and more confidence in supply, including the DSPs and optical subsystems. But the group comparable operating-profit range stayed at EUR 2.0-2.5 billion, described as tracking somewhat above the midpoint; capital expenditure guidance sits around EUR 900 million to EUR 1 billion, largely for optical manufacturing capacity. Management framed the year as investing to capture a long-term opportunity rather than to maximize near-term margin, noted that the San Jose fab is only a fraction of the 2026 ramp and more material longer term. The raise is in the growth lines; the profit range and the hedges around it are unchanged.
qualifier: forward guidance from the Q1 2026 earnings call - management's framing, not a reported result
vault_ref: NOK company page / FY2026 guidance raise + lead times

section_title: The names behind '9 of 10' - and why they stay unnamed
body_text: Nokia's strongest AI-cloud disclosure is a category number, not a customer list: 9 of the top 10 global hyperscalers use its optical networks, but the specific hyperscalers are not named at either the annual-filing or earnings-call level. Pressed on the naming gap - and specifically on whether hyperscalers hesitate to name Nokia because it is not a U.S. company - management's framing was that customers can speak about Nokia if they choose, that it 'is not my priority', and that Nokia does not carry the same concentration dynamic as some U.S. peers because the business is more diversified, with no indication of a geopolitical reason. Nokia also frames AI and cloud providers, in its own words, as 'customers, partners and potential competitors', since those same players build their own switching and silicon. So who sits behind the category figure, and how deeply exposure runs at any single hyperscaler, is left open.
qualifier: open question - the underlying customer names are not disclosed
vault_ref: NOK company page / hyperscaler non-disclosure + multi-role framing

section_title: The capex raise, and what's inside it
body_text: Alphabet raised its full-year 2026 capital-spending guidance to a range of $180-190 billion, up from $175-185 billion. Management attributed the increase to the acquisition of Intersect, which closed in March - Intersect being an energy and power asset, so part of the raise is an energy-infrastructure commitment, not only compute. The stated driver was unprecedented internal and external demand for AI compute resources. First-quarter 2026 purchases of property and equipment were $35.7 billion, up 107% year-over-year, with the overwhelming majority spent on technical infrastructure. Looking further out, management guided 2027 capex to increase significantly again versus 2026 but did not quantify it, saying more clarity would come in future calls. The finance chief also flagged that scaling this infrastructure will keep pressure on the P&L through higher depreciation and related data-center operating costs, such as energy.
qualifier: capex figures are forward guidance and management framing; the quarter's spend is filed
vault_ref: GOOGL company page / capex and demand-durability

section_title: Google Cloud passes $20 billion, and the backlog behind it
body_text: Google Cloud revenue reached $20.0 billion in the quarter ended March 2026, up 63% year-over-year, passing $20 billion for the first time. Cloud operating income tripled to $6.6 billion from $2.2 billion, lifting the segment's operating margin to about 33% from roughly 18% a year earlier - revenue acceleration and margin expansion at the same time. Enterprise AI became the primary Cloud growth driver for the first time, and revenue from products built on Alphabet's generative-AI models grew nearly 800% year-over-year. Cloud backlog nearly doubled sequentially to $462.3 billion, with just over half expected to be recognized as revenue over the next 24 months - a forward-demand marker that includes the newly added TPU hardware sales. One hedge carried alongside the results: the Wiz cloud-security acquisition, which closed in March and is reported inside Google Cloud, is expected to be a low-single-digit-percentage-point headwind to Cloud operating margin for the rest of 2026.
qualifier: from the March 2026 quarterly filing and earnings call
vault_ref: GOOGL company page / Google Cloud inflection

section_title: The multi-architecture compute lineup - and TPUs going external
body_text: Alphabet runs one of the most developed hyperscaler custom-silicon programs. It introduced 8th-generation TPUs, individually specialized: a training chip described as three times the processing power of the prior Ironwood generation, and an inference chip described as 80% better performance per dollar than the generation before it. Its Arm-based Axion CPU is now the host processor for those TPUs, replacing x86 host chips. Management framed custom TPUs, Axion CPUs, and the latest NVIDIA GPUs together as the industry's widest variety of compute options, and said Alphabet would be among the first to offer NVIDIA's Vera Rubin systems. Broadcom is Alphabet's TPU design and manufacturing partner. A strategic shift was disclosed too: Alphabet will begin delivering TPUs to a select group of customers for their own data centers - moving TPUs from captive silicon toward a hardware offering. Revenue there is expected to be a small percent in 2026 with the majority realized in 2027, and lumpy depending on when chips ship. Named external users cited include Thinking Machines Lab, Hudson River Trading, and Boston Dynamics.
qualifier: from the March 2026 earnings call; the external-sales revenue timing is management's framing
vault_ref: GOOGL company page / custom silicon (TPU + Axion)

section_title: Read the operating line, not net income
body_text: Alphabet's reported net income is materially inflated by gains that sit below the operating line - unrealized fair-value gains on equity securities that flow through other income. In the quarter ended March 2026 that was a $36.9 billion gain, versus $9.8 billion a year earlier; the finance chief attributed it to unrealized gains in the non-marketable equity-securities portfolio. Because those gains are volatile and non-operating, operating income is the cleaner read of the business. Operating income rose 30% in the quarter to $39.7 billion, with operating margin of 36.1%, while reported net income rose 81% - the gap between the two is the equity-portfolio swing. The same dynamic showed up across the full year 2025, where net income grew faster than operating income for the same reason. The reported bottom line will keep moving with the marked-to-market portfolio, so the operating line is the durable comparison.
qualifier: from the March 2026 filing and earnings call; the gain is explicitly non-operating
vault_ref: GOOGL company page / equity-securities-gain caveat

section_title: The quarter, and the margin under AI load
body_text: Microsoft's quarter ending March 2026 (its fiscal Q3): total revenue of $82.9 billion, up 18% year-over-year and 15% in constant currency. Operating income was $38.4 billion, up 20%, and net income $31.8 billion, up 23%. Microsoft Cloud revenue reached $54.5 billion, up 29%, at a 66% gross margin - down from 69% the prior fiscal year and guided to roughly 64% the following quarter, with the compression attributed to scaling AI infrastructure. Company gross margin was 68%, also down as AI-infrastructure investment and AI product usage grew. Inside the cloud, Azure grew 40% (39% in constant currency) and was described as accelerating, with demand continuing to exceed available capacity. The Intelligent Cloud segment, which carries Azure, grew 30%. The margin path under inference load is the number to watch: it is falling as the AI fleet scales, and management guides it lower again next quarter.
qualifier: from the March-2026 quarterly filing and earnings call
vault_ref: MSFT company page / Q3 FY2026 financial snapshot + cloud margin

section_title: Two legs: consumption amplified, seats contested
body_text: Microsoft's AI monetization splits cleanly along how a unit is billed. The consumption-billed leg - Azure, metered by compute - is amplified by AI, because agent activity increases usage; this is the leg the whole supply-chain buildout ultimately serves. The seat-billed leg - Microsoft 365 Copilot, a per-user add-on around $30 - is the contested one. Management reported over 20 million paid Copilot seats, seat adds up 250% year-over-year (its fastest growth since launch), the number of customers with 50,000-plus seats quadrupling, and Accenture at 740,000 seats, the largest win to date. The hedge sits right beside those numbers: Copilot penetration remains a low single-digit share of the roughly 450-million-seat commercial base, and per-seat value depends on surviving agent substitution - whether a seat holds its worth as agents do more of the work. Same company, two very different durability questions.
qualifier: from the March-2026 earnings call
vault_ref: MSFT company page / AI monetization + billed-unit lens

section_title: Capex ahead of revenue, and the CFO's answer
body_text: Microsoft is a direct primary source for hyperscaler capex, sitting at the top of the AI-buildout diagram as the spend the rest of the chain tracks downstream. Additions to property and equipment were $30.9 billion cash in the quarter (about $31.9 billion including finance leases), up roughly 85% year-over-year, and $80.1 billion over nine months. Management guided to roughly $190 billion for calendar 2026 and to above $40 billion for the next quarter. Roughly two-thirds of the spend is short-lived assets (servers and GPUs - the portion framed as correlating with revenue) and one-third long-lived (datacenters, land, power, with 15-year-plus lives). Total remaining performance obligations stood at $633 billion, with commercial RPO up 26% year-over-year excluding OpenAI - meaning OpenAI is itself a material part of that book. The CFO addressed the investor worry head-on that capex is growing faster than revenue, leaning on the short-lived-asset share and the $600-billion-plus book of business rather than disputing the concern. Whether that short-lived spend converts to revenue fast enough to close the gap is the open timing risk.
qualifier: forward capex is management guidance; RPO from the quarterly filing
vault_ref: MSFT company page / capex + demand-durability

section_title: The $37 billion that lives only in the commentary
body_text: Microsoft's most-cited AI figure comes with a disclosure caveat. Management stated the AI business surpassed a $37 billion annual run-rate, up 123% - but that number appears only in earnings commentary, not on any discrete line in the annual or quarterly filing. AI revenue is embedded inside Azure, Microsoft 365, and GitHub, with no separate reported line, and the run-rate is a non-GAAP construct spoken on the call. The same tier gap runs through the quarter's other headline AI metrics - the Copilot seat counts, the Azure growth rate, the roughly $190 billion capex guidance, and the OpenAI deal terms are all disclosed in commentary while the filings embed AI inside existing segments. Reading this company means noting which venue a fact is allowed to appear in: the striking AI numbers live in the call, and the filings keep AI folded into the segments it already reports.
qualifier: the run-rate is a call-only construct, not a filed line
vault_ref: MSFT company page / AI revenue disclosure + source audit

section_title: OpenAI: the terms disclosed, the share that isn't
body_text: Microsoft describes its restructured OpenAI arrangement in structural terms only: access to OpenAI's frontier-model IP royalty-free through 2032, a revenue-share arrangement persisting through 2030, an equity stake, and OpenAI as a large compute customer across AI accelerators and other compute. The annual filing reflects net recognized equity-method losses on the investment. OpenAI is a material part of the backlog - commercial remaining performance obligations grew 26% year-over-year when OpenAI is excluded, which is how its weight shows through. What the primary record does not disclose is any percentage: an outside claim that 'OpenAI is 45% of Azure RPO' was checked against the annual filing, the quarterly filing, and the call, and no such figure exists in any of them - the relationship is material but unquantified at that granularity. Whether Microsoft ever puts a number on OpenAI's share of the backlog is an open question.
qualifier: deal terms from the call; the '45% of RPO' figure is unverified and not in any filing
vault_ref: MSFT company page / OpenAI relationship + open questions

section_title: Capex raised - and the driver named plainly
body_text: Meta raised its full-year 2026 capital-expenditure guidance, including principal payments on finance leases, to a range of $125-145 billion, up from a prior range of $115-135 billion. Management attributed most of the increase not to more volume but to cost: higher component costs, particularly memory pricing, with a smaller part from additional data-center costs to support future capacity. At the roughly $135 billion midpoint that is about 87% above 2025's $72 billion of capital spending. First-quarter 2026 capex was $19.8 billion, driven by servers, data centers, and network infrastructure. The honest read the company itself surfaces: even a company sitting on enormous AI demand pays the memory bill - the raise is a cost-push from component pricing, not only from buying more. Full-year total expenses were held unchanged at $162-169 billion, and operating income is expected to be above 2025.
qualifier: forward capex guidance; the memory-pricing driver is management's own attribution
vault_ref: META company page / capex and AI-datacenter investment

section_title: The quarter, and the tax-benefit caveat
body_text: Meta's first quarter of 2026: total revenue of $56.3 billion, up 33% year-over-year, with income from operations of $22.9 billion at a 41% operating margin (up 30%). Reported net income was $26.8 billion, but that figure was inflated by a roughly $5 billion one-time tax benefit; management gave the normalized number itself, noting that absent the tax benefit net income would have been about $18.7 billion. Operating income, up 30%, is the clean metric to read. Family of Apps - the ads engine - carried revenue of $55.9 billion and operating income of $26.9 billion, roughly a 48% margin. For the full year 2025, revenue was $201.0 billion (up 22%), income from operations $83.3 billion, and capital spending $72.2 billion. The takeaway the company states plainly: read operating income, not the tax-noisy net income line.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 quarterly filing and earnings call; the normalized figure is management's own
vault_ref: META company page / financial snapshot

section_title: Meta Compute: three suppliers, and the metaverse bill
body_text: Meta builds its AI compute across three suppliers under an initiative it calls Meta Compute, aimed at leading the industry in the efficiency of building compute. It is rolling out more than 1 gigawatt of its own custom silicon developed with Broadcom, a significant amount of AMD chips, and NVIDIA systems - a multi-vendor mix where custom and merchant chips grow at the same time. Broadcom separately names Meta as a custom accelerator customer, so both sides confirm the program. Alongside the build, Meta is expanding its own data-center footprint and signing multi-year cloud deals that come online across 2026 and 2027 to scale more quickly. The honest counterweight the company surfaces: Reality Labs, its metaverse arm, lost $19.2 billion from operations in 2025 against about $2.2 billion of revenue, and the Family of Apps ads business - $102.5 billion of operating income - is what funds it. One business pays for the other.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and annual filing; the Broadcom-Meta confirmation is bilateral
vault_ref: META company page / custom silicon and Reality Labs drag

section_title: The quarter, and where the profit really comes from
body_text: Amazon's quarter ending March 2026: net sales of $181.5 billion, up 17% year-over-year (15% excluding a favorable foreign-exchange impact), with operating income of $23.9 billion at a record 13.1% operating margin. AWS grew 28% year-over-year to $37.6 billion - the fastest AWS growth in 15 quarters and the largest Q4-to-Q1 increase ever - reaching about a $150 billion annualized run rate. The detail worth noticing: at roughly 21% of consolidated sales, AWS generated 59% of the whole company's operating income ($14.2 billion of $23.9 billion) at a 37.7% segment margin, against 7.9% in North America and 3.6% International. The company guided the following quarter to net sales between $194 billion and $199 billion and operating income between $20 billion and $24 billion.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: AMZN company page / Q1 FY2026 financial snapshot

section_title: Capital: no clean annual number, said plainly
body_text: Amazon declined a precise annual capital-spending figure on this call. Asked directly, the CEO said: 'I don't have a new update on capital. Our plan is largely the same.' What the company did disclose: cash capex of $43.2 billion in the quarter (up about 73% year-over-year), trailing-twelve-month capex of $151 billion, and full-year 2025 capex of $131.8 billion (up 59%). A roughly $200 billion 2026 figure was reported to have been named once at the prior quarter's call, but was not restated here - so it reads as named-once, not a firm standing guide, softer than the precise annual ranges some peers give. Management's framing: 'the faster AWS grows, the more short-term CapEx we'll spend,' and AWS must lay out cash for land, power, buildings, chips, servers, and networking gear typically six to 24 months before it can bill customers.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 call and filing; the ~$200B figure is reported, not restated
vault_ref: AMZN company page / CAPEX and AI-datacenter expansion

section_title: Trainium: $225 billion committed, and sold out down the roadmap
body_text: Amazon's in-house training chip, Trainium (designed by its Annapurna team, manufactured by an outside foundry), now carries over $225 billion in revenue commitments - the largest single custom-silicon commitment figure the company discusses. It includes very large multi-year, multi-gigawatt training commitments from the two leading AI labs, Anthropic and OpenAI, plus companies like Uber. The roadmap is supply-constrained top to bottom: Trainium2, with about 30% better price-performance than comparable GPUs, is largely sold out; Trainium3, which started shipping at the start of 2026 and is 30-40% more price-performant, is nearly fully subscribed; and Trainium4, still about 18 months from broad availability, is already reserved. Management's economic rationale, in its own words: Trainium 'will save us tens of billions of dollars of CapEx each year' and provide several hundred basis points of operating-margin advantage versus relying on others' chips for inference. Amazon adds that it continues to order substantial quantities from NVIDIA and expects to remain partners.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: AMZN company page / custom silicon - Trainium

section_title: The backlog, and what it leaves out
body_text: The AWS revenue backlog stood at $364 billion at the end of the March 2026 quarter. Management noted plainly that this figure 'does not include the recent deal we announced with Anthropic for over $100 billion,' and that there is 'reasonable breadth' in it - 'not just one customer or two customers.' The company treats this as a forward-demand proxy: contracted future commitments, not booked revenue. It sits alongside a stated capex tension the CEO owned directly - in periods of very high growth, when capital spending outpaces revenue growth, early-years free cash flow is challenged until the first tranches of capacity are monetized, though the underlying data-center assets have useful lives of 30-plus years.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: AMZN company page / AWS revenue backlog

section_title: Read the operating line: the Anthropic mark-up in the numbers
body_text: Amazon's reported net income rose 77% year-over-year in the quarter, but the increase is materially inflated by a $15.6 billion non-operating 'Other income' item (versus $2.7 billion a year earlier). The filing attributes this primarily to an upward adjustment on Amazon's own nonvoting preferred stake in Anthropic as Anthropic's valuation rose - about a $12.3 billion mark-up plus a $4.5 billion reclassification on converted Anthropic notes - not an operating result. Operating income, up 30%, is the clean read. The entanglement runs deep: Amazon has invested $8.0 billion in Anthropic convertible notes and, after quarter-end, another $5.0 billion in preferred stock, and established a financing facility of up to $20.0 billion for Anthropic tied to compute-capacity milestones. Its private-company equity (mostly Anthropic and OpenAI preferred) is now carried at $48.1 billion, up from $16.2 billion - so reported net income will swing with these marks quarter to quarter.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 quarterly filing's non-operating income disclosures
vault_ref: AMZN company page / Anthropic entanglement and equity-gain caveat

section_title: Memory costs, walked through in order - and the margin guide cut
body_text: The most consequential signal in Apple's quarter is a cost signal, not a strategy one. Management said memory had a minimal impact in the December quarter, a bit more in the March quarter (partly offset by carry-in inventory), and that the June quarter would see significantly higher memory costs, also partly offset by carry-in inventory. Beyond June, where the company does not give color, management said it believes memory costs will drive an increasing impact on the business, and that it would look at a range of options. The direction is already visible in the guide: gross margin is guided down to 47.5-48.5%, from 49.3% in the March quarter, with memory named as the primary driver. This is Apple confirming the memory squeeze from the demand side. As one of the world's largest buyers of DRAM and NAND, it sits on the cost-taker side of that cycle - it does not make memory, cannot pass the full cost through in a competitive consumer market, and absorbs the margin hit. A clean cross-check that the memory cycle is real and biting even the highest-margin hardware business in the world.
qualifier: from the Q2 FY2026 earnings call and quarterly filing; the guidance is forward-looking
vault_ref: AAPL company page / memory cost exposure - the demand-side cost-taker signal

section_title: The capex-light AI counter-model
body_text: Apple's AI strategy is the structural inverse of the datacenter builders'. Where the cloud payers centralize AI in datacenters funded by very large annual capital budgets, Apple pushes intelligence on-device, powered by its own silicon, with a modest, undisclosed Private Cloud Compute tier for heavier tasks. The CEO framed Apple Intelligence as woven into the platforms, fast, personal, and private - an embedded device feature, not a standalone cloud service. No dependence on outside AI-model providers is disclosed in the quarter's sources, and a more personalized voice assistant is deferred to this year. The capital signature confirms the model: fiscal-2025 capital spending was $12.7 billion, about 3.1% of revenue, an order of magnitude below the cloud cohort. Pressed on whether Apple would build out more infrastructure as the world turns AI-centric, the CFO said AI investment would be incremental on top of what the company normally invests, and the CEO pointed to accelerating research spending rather than infrastructure. This is the honest counterpoint to the AI-demand debate: the mega-cap with the most to gain from AI in its products is choosing not to build at datacenter scale.
qualifier: from the Q2 FY2026 earnings call and fiscal-2025 filing
vault_ref: AAPL company page / the capex-light AI counter-model

section_title: The leading-edge chip buyer that never names its foundry
body_text: Apple designs its own chips - the A-series for iPhone, the M-series for Mac - and has them fabricated on the leading edge. It is the foundry's largest customer and the lead adopter of each new manufacturing node, the volume that underwrites the leading edge's economics. But, per Apple's long practice, its annual filing never names the foundry: it discloses only that new products often use custom components available from only one source, and that certain components come from single or limited sources. The advanced chip nodes are, by the company's own account, the primary supply constraint it has faced - the limit on how many devices it can build. So Apple is a heavy consumer of this manufacturing and captures none of its scarcity value; it owns none of the bottleneck it depends on. The same vertical-integration instinct shows up elsewhere in its silicon: Apple is developing its own modem to replace a supplier's, designing that vendor out over time, just as it did with its own processors.
qualifier: the foundry is inferable, not named in the filing; single-source risk language only
vault_ref: AAPL company page / supply chain and silicon - the foundry anchor + China concentration

section_title: The backlog: $638 billion, up 363%
body_text: Oracle's remaining performance obligations - contracted future revenue not yet recognized - reached $638 billion at the fiscal year ended May 31, 2026, up 363% from $137.8 billion a year earlier, and $552.6 billion a quarter earlier. Management calls it exceptional visibility, all supported by long-term contractual customer commitments. It is long-dated: about 12% is expected to convert within the next 12 months, 34% over months 13-36, 34% over months 37-60, and roughly 20% beyond - so current cloud-infrastructure revenue is only a small fraction of the backlog, and the bulk converts years out. About 12% of the backlog is now the prepaid / bring-your-own-hardware AI-infrastructure cohort. The recognized-revenue base is diversified - no single customer was 10% or more of revenue in fiscal 2026, 2025 or 2024 - but the contracted backlog surge is attributed only to certain significant contracts. Those two facts are not in tension: today's billed base is broad; tomorrow's backlog is narrow.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 annual filing and the Q4 earnings call
vault_ref: ORCL company page / RPO backlog + concentration

section_title: Capex up 162%, free cash flow at negative $23.7 billion
body_text: Oracle's capital spending is the steepest ramp among the large AI-datacenter spenders: purchases of property and equipment went from $6.9 billion in fiscal 2024 to $21.2 billion in fiscal 2025 to $55.7 billion in fiscal 2026, up 162% in the latest year. Against operating cash flow of $32.0 billion, that pushed free cash flow to negative $23.7 billion for the year. Part of the build is customer- or partner-funded through prepayments and bring-your-own-hardware structures - a cumulative $75 billion of such contracts, $67 billion of AI-infrastructure contracts signed in the fourth quarter alone, at similar or better margins than the rest - so reported capex understates the total datacenter capacity being built in Oracle's name. Fiscal 2027 capex is guided to about $70 billion of net cash outlay, which becomes roughly $90-95 billion reported once $20-25 billion of customer prepayments and third-party-manufacturer timing is added back; management's phrasing is that the sum of those is reported capex. Net income itself carries no large one-off mark - the distinctive caveat sits below the line in the financing structure and the rising debt load, not in reported earnings.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2026 annual filing and the Q4 earnings call; forward capex is management's guide
vault_ref: ORCL company page / capex ramp + free cash flow

section_title: Financing the bet - and the customer it won't name
body_text: Oracle is funding its datacenter build increasingly off its own balance sheet. In fiscal 2026 it raised about $34 billion - $28.8 billion of senior notes plus a $5 billion mandatory convertible preferred - and plans about $40 billion more in fiscal 2027, including a $20 billion at-the-market equity program, with no additional debt planned in calendar 2026 and its investment-grade rating reaffirmed. The durability question underneath it: the $638 billion backlog is attributed only to certain significant contracts, and Oracle names no counterparty. A widely reported compute contract with a single large, itself-capital-hungry AI customer is never named in the fiscal 2026 annual filing or on any earnings call, and single-customer backlog concentration is not quantified. How exposed Oracle is to that customer's own funding and compute-demand durability is the central open question. Independent analysis has also put total debt above $100 billion and $72 billion or more of partner-debt behind the build, with one lender reportedly stepping back from a $10 billion Oracle datacenter financing over the spending pace - leverage context, not figures Oracle discloses.
qualifier: financing figures from the filings and call; the total-debt, partner-debt and lender detail is independent analysis, not disclosed
vault_ref: ORCL company page / financing envelope + counterparty concentration

section_title: The merchant-silicon cloud, and where the capex flows
body_text: Oracle is the AI-datacenter spender that buys merchant silicon rather than designing a captive accelerator. Where the other large clouds build in-house AI chips to cut capex and lift margin, Oracle deploys NVIDIA and AMD GPUs and merchant ARM CPUs, and competes instead on multicloud neutrality - running the Oracle database inside Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud and AWS - plus speed of capacity build. Multicloud database revenue grew 404% year over year in the fourth quarter, on a base management calls very early days. Its spending feeds the physical build-out: management describes AI infrastructure as beginning with data centers and power generation, then compute, networking and storage that must be designed, manufactured, delivered and installed - and notes that both GPU and CPU supply continues to exceed nothing and fall short of demand. Oracle delivered more than 1.2 gigawatts of capacity in fiscal 2026, reports global GPU utilization of 97.5%, and its New Mexico site at Dona Ana County is designed around gigawatt-scale Bloom clean fuel cells - a company-side confirmation of a large cloud building on-site fuel-cell power.
qualifier: from the Q4 and Q3 earnings calls and the company filings
vault_ref: ORCL company page / merchant silicon + capex flow + power

section_title: The Apple-modem cliff, owned plainly
body_text: Qualcomm's core is two businesses: the chip segment (QCT), which was $38.4 billion of fiscal-2025 revenue, and patent licensing (QTL) at $5.6 billion on a 72% pre-tax margin - the high-margin engine. Total fiscal-2025 revenue was $44.3 billion. Apple is the largest customer at roughly 21% of revenue for modems, and simultaneously the clearest structural risk: the annual filing states Apple's shift to its own in-house modem will have a significant negative impact on chip revenue, results of operations, and cash flows. Management now models the Apple chip business at only about $2 billion in fiscal 2027, with the licensing royalty held pending renegotiation. The customer-and-threat dynamic is the single most commercially material relationship the company has, and the filing describes it without softening.
qualifier: from the fiscal-2025 annual filing and the latest earnings call
vault_ref: QCOM company page / Apple-modem cliff + QCT/QTL split

section_title: Automotive: the proven diversification line
body_text: Automotive is the diversification vector that has converted to real revenue. In the quarter ended March 2026 it crossed $5 billion annualized for the first time, up 38% year over year, and management guides to a roughly $6 billion exit run-rate for the fiscal year and about 50% year-over-year growth the following quarter. The growth runs on the Snapdragon Digital Chassis - connectivity, telematics, infotainment, and driver-assist and automated-driving - with more than 1 million cars now operating driver-assist and autonomy on its Ride processors, and named engagements with BMW, Bosch, and Wayve. Management's own framing is that as the mix shifts from cockpit chips toward driver-assist, and from chip sales toward modules and software, it still models the business in line with the corporate-average margin - a claim, not yet a settled result.
qualifier: from the latest earnings call; forward run-rate and margin are management's framing
vault_ref: QCOM company page / automotive diversification

section_title: Edge AI: real optionality, but a measured read - not a supercycle
body_text: Qualcomm is the edge counterpoint to the datacenter build-out, and its own read on edge AI is deliberately measured. It is re-entering the datacenter as a custom-silicon provider for an unnamed leading hyperscaler - a datacenter CPU plus AI-inference accelerators, built on the Alphawave connectivity acquisition, with initial shipments guided for the December-2026 quarter and the customer withheld until a June investor day. Its central framing is that agent orchestration is predominantly CPU-bound, positioned around its Oryon CPU across phone, PC, auto, and eventually datacenter, with the PC agentic ramp weighted toward late 2026. On robotics it has design wins with Figure AI and a multi-year NEURA agreement. But edge-inference silicon - the layer where Qualcomm competes - is independently ranked as the least durable and most contested part of the physical-AI value chain, and the datacenter custom-silicon is early and unproven. Whether it converts to material recurring revenue is the open question the company itself flags, and it is a weak fit to the datacenter thesis by its own placement.
qualifier: independent analysis plus forward guidance from the latest call; the datacenter customer and revenue are unproven
vault_ref: QCOM company page / edge-AI + datacenter custom-silicon optionality

section_title: The quarter, and the backlog behind it
body_text: ServiceNow's quarter ending March 2026: total revenue of $3.77 billion, up 22% year-over-year, with subscription revenue of $3.67 billion, also up 22% (and 19% in constant currency), coming in above the high end of guidance. Non-GAAP operating margin was 32%, half a point above guidance, and free cash flow margin was 44%. The backlog is the durable signal: remaining performance obligations reached $27.7 billion, up 23.5% in constant currency, and current RPO was $12.64 billion, up 21%. One number is worth reading carefully: GAAP net income rose only about 2% on that 22% revenue, because the tax provision jumped to $204 million from $95 million a year earlier - so the headline profitability the company emphasizes is the non-GAAP 32% margin, not the GAAP line. For the full prior year, revenue was $13.3 billion, up 21%, with subscription at 97% of that and a 98% renewal rate.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: NOW company page / Q1 2026 financial snapshot

section_title: From seats to tokens - the billed unit is changing
body_text: ServiceNow bills its software largely by the seat, and management says that unit is shifting: 50% of net-new business now comes from a non-seat-based pricing model, including tokens and other assets such as infrastructure and connectors. The company frames this as a hybrid model - predictable foundational seat licenses combined with usage-based scale - and announced that the entire portfolio is now 'AI native,' meaning AI, data, security, and governance are built into every product rather than sold as a separate purchase, a deliberate break from bolt-on AI. There is real evidence that AI auto-resolution reduces the human workload behind those seats: management cited one customer, Robinhood, deflecting 70% of employee requests before a human is involved and eliminating 2,200 hours of manual effort a month. That is a single customer case, though - not a portfolio-wide figure showing how many fulfiller seats the base actually sheds, and whether the usage-based revenue offsets any seat erosion is exactly the test that remains open.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call; the Robinhood figure is one customer case
vault_ref: NOW company page / billed-unit lens and AI monetization

section_title: Does the value stick here, or leak to the model layer?
body_text: ServiceNow is a clear test of whether AI value sticks at the software application layer or leaks down to the companies that make the underlying models. The moat case is a system of record: a 98% renewal rate, subscription gross margin above 80%, and a 'context engine' management says draws on more than 95 billion annual workflows and over 7 trillion transactions across 22 years - the argument being that AI agents need this governed workflow context, so the data layer is amplified rather than bypassed. It remains a claim, not yet a disclosed revenue moat. The contested side was priced in real time: the stock fell about 12% after the print despite beats. Sell-side analysts pressed on two points - how much of the AI growth is organic versus from acquisitions like Moveworks, Veza, and Armis, and whether customers negotiate down the older, seat-priced core to fund the new AI products. Management said it is 'not getting negotiated down on the core' but did not break out organic versus inorganic AI uplift, and the Now Assist bookings number counts only the incremental AI contribution, with no separate AI revenue line in the filings.
qualifier: AI story is call-only bookings; the organic-vs-inorganic split is an open question
vault_ref: NOW company page / durability moat and the contested application-layer verdict

section_title: Crane, Microsoft, and the FERC gate
body_text: The Crane Clean Energy Center is the restart of Three Mile Island Unit 1 - about 835 megawatts of emissions-free nuclear capacity dedicated to powering Microsoft's data centers in the PJM region. Management is direct that the plant won't start sooner than planned, but is pursuing full capacity credit by moving the connection timeline up from the older 2031 reference; the real open item is securing full capacity credit for the asset. Constellation has filed at FERC to transfer capacity-interconnection rights from the retired Eddystone units over to Crane, which it believes supports a 2027-deadline capacity credit, and is separately working with the utilities to shorten the transmission-interconnection timeline. A FERC response is expected in the June-to-July 2026 window. The restart therefore hinges on an external regulatory gate the company does not control, and management says it will know more once FERC responds.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and annual report; the FERC timing is pending
vault_ref: CEG company page / Crane restart + FERC capacity credit

section_title: A merchant generator, not a regulated utility
body_text: Constellation is a competitive merchant generator operating in the PJM, MISO, NYISO and ERCOT markets - explicitly not a regulated rate-base utility. Management made the distinction on the call in response to a question about a Pennsylvania governor's letter, noting the letter pertained to the regulated utilities and not to entities like Constellation. The practical consequence is two-sided: the company carries merchant power-price exposure rather than a fixed regulated return, but it can also capture premium pricing through long-term contracts for firm, clean power. Analysts flagged weakness in ERCOT and PJM forward prices even as the demand pipeline looked strong; management argued the forwards undervalue the 2028-2029-and-beyond period, said the ERCOT load 'isn't yet on the system - it's getting built,' and noted it has stayed well hedged and protected against the near-term weakness.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and the annual report
vault_ref: CEG company page / competitive-merchant status + merchant-price exposure

section_title: The data-economy demand signal, and its hedges
body_text: Management states that demand for compute, and by extension power, has not slowed from hyperscaler customers, with projected 2026 spending nearly 75% higher than the prior year and continuing to be revised upward. It cites more than 400,000 megawatts of large loads in the ERCOT interconnection queue while cautioning it does not expect anything near that figure to materialize. Its read is that the forward market beyond 2029 appears to price only about 10,000 to 15,000 megawatts of incremental load, so realized load closer to 30,000 megawatts would be expected to put upward pressure on prices. On the supply side, Constellation has submitted roughly 5,000 megawatts of new capacity - nuclear uprates, new natural gas, and battery storage - into PJM's interconnection queue, and its powered-land deal with CyrusOne at the Freestone Energy Center received Texas PUCT approval of its net-metering agreement, with the enabling substation expected energized in the fourth quarter of 2026. The recurring caveat: much of this load is not yet on the system, so timing is the open variable.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call; forward-load figures are management's framing
vault_ref: CEG company page / datacenter power exposure + demand signal

section_title: Power generation: the datacenter angle, and one slice of the whole
body_text: For the quarter ending March 2026, Caterpillar's Power Generation external sales were $2,817 million, up 41% year over year, and sales to end users grew 48% - driven by demand for large gensets and turbines used in data center applications, with an increasing mix toward prime power. Set that against the whole company: power generation is roughly 16% of consolidated sales in the quarter, and about 14% for full-year 2025. Construction Industries ($7,161 million, up 38% in the quarter) and Resource Industries ($3,797 million, up 4%) still dominate the base, alongside oil and gas and industrial engines inside the Power and Energy segment. The company's annual filing states that no single customer or dealer represented a significant concentration of credit risk. Data center power is a growing part of the story, and it is a minority of it - both facts sit in the same filings.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 quarterly filing and the annual filing
vault_ref: CAT company page / Power Generation sub-application + segment mix

section_title: The backlog: record $62 billion, orders into 2028
body_text: Caterpillar reported record consolidated backlog of $62 billion firm at the end of March 2026, up 79% year over year and about $28 billion higher than the prior quarter. The large reciprocating engine backlog has grown by more than 3.5x since the company first announced its capacity expansion plans in January 2024, and management noted that customers are committing to longer-term orders, with some running well into 2028. The Power and Energy segment is estimated to represent a substantial part of that multi-quarter backlog growth. Backlog is the demand signal here; the orders are booked, but delivery stretches across years, so the revenue lands over time rather than at once.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 quarterly filing and earnings call
vault_ref: CAT company page / backlog visibility

section_title: Behind the meter: six 1-gigawatt agreements, and the ProPower deal
body_text: Caterpillar sells engines and turbines behind the meter - on-site power that a data center runs itself, for backup or for prime (continuous) power. On the Q1 FY2026 call management described six agreements, each carrying at least 1 gigawatt of Caterpillar equipment for prime power applications, plus multiple projects below 1 gigawatt where it is also supplying customers. The most recent, announced April 29, 2026, is up to 2.1 gigawatts of large gas generator sets for ProPower, supporting data center, oil and gas, and industrial applications, with gensets delivered over about the next five years. ProPower is named as a data center developer; the underlying hyperscaler customers are not disclosed - management keeps it at 'our largest customers in the broader data center industry.' Management framed a multi-product advantage: having both turbines and reciprocating engines lets it configure a site one way, the other, or a mix.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 earnings call and the April 29, 2026 announcement
vault_ref: CAT company page / behind-the-meter prime power positioning

section_title: Raising capacity - and owning the cost of it
body_text: Caterpillar is raising large reciprocating engine capacity from 2x 2024 levels to nearly 3x 2024 levels, with the additional investment beginning as soon as possible but occurring primarily from 2027 through 2029; capital expenditures are expected to average about 4% to 5% of manufacturing-and-engine sales through 2030. The 2030 power generation sales target was raised to more than 3x the 2024 baseline, and the estimated additional capacity is another roughly 15 gigawatts annually once the installation is complete, on top of a 50-gigawatt baseline. The caution travels with the ambition: adding capacity runs accelerated depreciation, which the company said drags Power and Energy margin over the next few years; Power and Energy segment margin was 20.6% in the quarter, down 170 basis points, mainly on tariffs. Full-year 2026 tariff cost is estimated at $2.2 to $2.4 billion, versus a $2.6 billion estimate given in January, and the company describes the tariff situation as fluid, based on what it sees today.
qualifier: from the Q1 FY2026 call; the 2030 targets are management's raised guidance
vault_ref: CAT company page / capacity expansion + FY2026 guidance

section_title: The honest counterweight: a broad cyclical machine
body_text: The datacenter-power story is a slice of a diversified industrial company, and the rest of it is broadly cyclical. Construction Industries was about a third of full-year 2025 sales, driven by non-residential construction, infrastructure spending, and dealer rental - not AI. Resource Industries is mining, heavy construction, quarry and aggregates, and rail. Even inside the Power and Energy segment, oil and gas gas-compression engines and industrial engines carry substantial non-AI cyclical exposure. The company's revenue-mix shift toward power generation over 2027 to 2030 is a projection, not a reported result, and how durable the datacenter slice proves is an open question. One outside caution on framing: independent research calls Solar Turbines the world's largest industrial gas turbine maker, but the company's own annual filing does not confirm that superlative - it describes the turbine business without it. Read the AI-power angle as real but bounded, not as the whole machine.
qualifier: diversified-base reading across the annual filing and independent research
vault_ref: CAT company page / multi-segment honest-verdict + open questions

section_title: The quarter, one vertical at a time
body_text: nVent's quarter ended March 2026: net sales of $1,242 million, up 53% year-over-year and 34% organically - the third consecutive quarter above $1 billion. Acquisitions added $138 million, and foreign exchange was a two-point tailwind. Company return on sales was 20%, flat to last year; price plus productivity offset inflation of nearly $60 million, including roughly $40 million of tariff impact. Systems Protection sales were $895 million, up 76% (50% organically), with its infrastructure vertical growing more than 100% on data-center strength and segment return on sales up 220 basis points to 22.7%. Electrical Connections sales were $347 million, up 15% (8% organically), with return on sales down 390 basis points to 24.4% on higher-than-expected raw-material inflation, primarily copper - management took pricing and productivity actions and expects margins to improve through the year. Free cash flow grew 21% year-over-year. All metrics exceeded the company's guidance.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: NVT company page / Q1 FY2026 financial snapshot + segment performance

section_title: The portfolio, pointed at infrastructure on purpose
body_text: nVent describes a deliberate repositioning toward the infrastructure vertical - data centers and power utilities. Infrastructure was 12% of sales at the 2018 Pentair spin-off, expanded to about 45% last year, and was over 55% (55.8%) of consolidated revenue in the quarter ended March 2026, up 118.9% year-over-year. The shift came through organic investment and M&A: the company divested its legacy heat-tracing Thermal Management business in 2025 for proceeds of about $1,584.5 million, and acquired ECM Industries (about $1.1 billion, 2023), Trachte (about $677.7 million, 2024), and the Electrical Products Group (about $979.6 million, closed in 2025). Within infrastructure, data centers is the primary growth driver of the Systems Protection segment; management frames data centers as its greatest growth opportunity in the vertical, with power utilities next on the strength of grid-capacity demand. The infrastructure figure aggregates data centers and power utilities together - a clean standalone data-center revenue line is not broken out.
qualifier: from the FY2025 filing and Q1 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: NVT company page / infrastructure repositioning + M&A trajectory

section_title: Orders up 40%, and the honest split
body_text: In the quarter ended March 2026, nVent's organic orders were up about 40% year-over-year, primarily driven by orders for the AI Data Center build-out. Management noted that excluding data centers, organic orders still grew mid-teens, broad-based across all verticals and through distribution. Backlog grew low double digits sequentially to $2.6 billion, most of it spanning a period beyond twelve months, giving visibility into 2027. Management repeatedly cautioned that data-center orders tend to be lumpy and can vary month to month, so the pace is not framed as smooth. New products contributed over 20 points to sales growth, with 11 launched in the quarter, and forward visibility with key customers is described as several years out - the company points to industry talk of five-plus years of visibility and projects for 2030, and says it has a view to some key customers' demand for several years ahead.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: NVT company page / AI Data Center commercial momentum

section_title: One customer, about 11% - unnamed
body_text: In FY2025, one customer within nVent's Systems Protection segment represented approximately 11% of consolidated net sales; no other customer accounted for more than 10%. The identity of that customer is not disclosed - an industry-standard non-disclosure pattern. In the same vein, nVent names customer categories but not specific companies: management describes winning with a wide range of customers, from hyperscalers to neoclouds and multi-tenants, and also through distribution partners, and references working with chip manufacturers on coolant-distribution-unit roadmaps out to 2030 - without naming any of them. The picture is a concentrated tail with a single customer above 10%, sitting on top of diversified mid-tier exposure across hyperscalers, utilities, OEMs, panel builders, and contractors. Because products often move through distribution, the company notes it may not always know a product's final destination - something sold to a commercial contractor can end up in a data center.
qualifier: from the FY2025 filing and Q1 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: NVT company page / customer concentration honest-verdict

section_title: Liquid cooling, in the white space
body_text: nVent positions itself, in its own words, as an enclosures and liquid cooling leader in the U.S. and globally, citing electrification, sustainability, and digitalization - including increased use of artificial intelligence - as drivers of demand for its products. Its data-center business grew across both the gray space (engineered buildings, enclosures, power connections) and the white space, where management reported outstanding growth in liquid cooling alongside power distribution units and cable management. The disclosed liquid-cooling product scope centers on coolant distribution units, cooling solutions in both liquid and air, and enclosures with integrated cooling; management referenced roadmaps for next coolant-distribution units out to 2030 developed with some chip manufacturers. What is not spelled out at the filing level is the finer product breakdown - cold-plate, rear-door heat exchanger, and immersion-cooling scope are not separately enumerated. nVent competes here against several suppliers, not as a sole source: Vertiv's Liebert, Eaton's Boyd Thermal, Modine, and others including Schneider Electric and Stulz.
qualifier: from the FY2025 filing and Q1 2026 earnings call; product-breakdown detail is limited
vault_ref: NVT company page / specialty technology platform positioning

section_title: The quarter, in throughput terms
body_text: AAON's first quarter of 2026: net sales increased 54% year-over-year to a record $496.9 million, driven by higher production rates across both brands as recent capacity investments came online. Gross margin was 25.1%, down 170 basis points from 26.8% a year earlier - management attributes the decline to three temporary factors: an increased amount of outsourced components used to drive growth, unabsorbed fixed costs at the new Memphis facility as it ramps, and tariff-related plus general inflation pressures. SG&A fell 220 basis points to 13.7% of sales. Non-GAAP adjusted EBITDA rose 44% to $78 million, though the EBITDA margin slipped to 15.7% from 17.6%. Operating cash flow swung to positive $34 million from a $9.2 million use a year earlier, and capital expenditures were $52.9 million. Total backlog reached $2,129.5 million, more than double the $1,026.9 million a year earlier and up about 16% from year-end.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: AAON company page / Q1 2026 financial snapshot

section_title: Two brands, and the mix stated honestly
body_text: AAON reports along two brands, and the split matters. In Q1 2026, AAON-branded products were $268.4 million, or 54% of sales, and BASX-branded products were $228.6 million, or 46%. BASX-branded sales grew about 72% year-over-year - against a data center thermal management market growing at roughly 30%, which management frames as continued market share gains. The AAON-branded side is a more traditional, cyclical business: rooftop units and heat pumps for commercial buildings, where the broader unitary HVAC market grew only modestly, with industry data showing a low-single-digit volume recovery that AAON says it is outperforming. So the data center growth sits on top of a cyclical commercial-HVAC base, not in place of it. The brand line also cross-cuts the reporting segments - BASX-branded product is built in both the AAON Coil Products segment ($93.2 million of BASX-branded liquid cooling product there, up about 40%) and the BASX segment itself.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 quarterly filing's brand and segment disaggregation
vault_ref: AAON company page / brand-tier and segment-tier disaggregation

section_title: Naming the customers, quietly
body_text: AAON's annual filing does something most equipment makers avoid: it names its data center customers. The 10-K states that its top customers operate primarily in data center cooling and commercial air conditioning, and gives examples in the data center space - Microsoft, Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud, QTS and Applied Digital. Separately, the filing discloses that three customers were each above 10% of accounts receivable in both fiscal 2025 and 2024. Which of those three named companies the 10%+ concentration actually corresponds to is not disclosed - the naming and the concentration are two separate disclosures, not matched to each other. The BASX unit is described as building custom, high-performance cooling for the rapidly growing hyperscale data center market, and its cooling solutions are designed to support increasingly dense computing environments, including AI and high-performance computing workloads.
qualifier: from the 10-K's business and customer-concentration disclosures
vault_ref: AAON company page / named-hyperscaler and concentration disclosure

section_title: What the cooling touches, and what it does not
body_text: AAON's data center exposure is broad at the facility level but has a clear upstream boundary. Its BASX coolant distribution unit, launched in 2025, is described as the interface between facility water systems and the technology cooling loop - providing flow, temperature and pressure control to the server cold plates, and supporting rack densities exceeding 100 kilowatts. The portfolio also spans air handling, CRAH units, direct evaporative coolers, and a water-free free-cooling chiller platform, plus what management calls AI-centric free cooling chillers designed to run at higher fluid temperatures for AI workloads. But AAON does not manufacture the direct-to-chip cold plate itself, and it is not positioned in immersion cooling. It discloses no chip-vendor reference-architecture partnership with any accelerator maker, and no analyst on the call asked about one. So the honest frame is strong, broad data center cooling exposure at the facility scope - not a chip-tier liquid-cooling moat.
qualifier: from the 10-K product disclosures and Q1 2026 call; the boundary is stated plainly
vault_ref: AAON company page / BASX CDU scope and chip-tier boundary

section_title: The raised guide, framed as timing not reset
body_text: For full-year 2026, AAON raised its outlook to sales growth of 40-45% at a gross margin of 27-28%, with SG&A of 14-15% of sales and depreciation and amortization of $95-100 million. Management's framing is that the new top-line marker implies roughly $1 billion of BASX revenue for the year - a management framing, not a reported result. On longer-term capacity, management said the initial investments in the Longview and Memphis facilities hold more revenue potential than the original $1.5 billion baseline, describing the ceiling as above $2 billion, and noted this does not trigger a large additional investment to support that rate of growth. Full-year capital expenditures are expected to stay at about $190 million, concentrated on building out the 787,000-square-foot Memphis facility. The near-term margin compression is described as temporary - a timing issue tied to how the company is choosing to ramp and use outsourcing, not a structural reset - with margins expected to improve directionally as internal capacity absorbs fixed cost through the year.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 call; the forward figures are management's framing
vault_ref: AAON company page / FY2026 guidance and BASX capacity ceiling

section_title: Two hyperscaler agreements, and what they don't say
body_text: Between its February and May 2026 earnings calls, Fluence executed master supply agreements with two major hyperscalers, disclosed by CEO Julian Nebreda on the May call. Following the industry-standard customer non-disclosure pattern, neither is named - Microsoft, Amazon, Google, Meta and Oracle appear nowhere in the annual report, the quarterly filing, or the call. Each selection ran through multiple rounds of review against criteria specific to that customer; in one case the process began with 26 different vendors, and Fluence was the first to complete all qualifications to sign a global agreement. These agreements only establish Fluence as a qualified supplier positioned to bid on expected near-term data center projects; management said it expected to sign the initial order from one of these customers' data center projects within its third fiscal quarter, which runs April through June 2026. That order is an expectation, not a booked result. Notably, the annual report frames data centers and AI only as a macro electricity-demand driver with no commercial detail, and the quarterly filing carries zero data center content - the substance lives only in the call.
qualifier: from the Q2 FY2026 earnings call; the initial order is forward guidance
vault_ref: FLNC company page / data center commercial structure

section_title: An integrator that buys cells and sells systems - and the hard year behind it
body_text: Fluence is a grid-scale battery energy-storage systems integrator organized in three product lines: energy-storage solutions (battery hardware with integrated controls and its own energy-management software), recurring operations-and-maintenance services sold under long-term service agreements, and forecasting-and-asset-management software. It does not manufacture cells - it treats components as commodities and integrates them into finished projects for utilities, independent power producers, developers, and commercial-and-industrial customers. The financial picture is honest about the strain. Fiscal 2025 revenue fell 16.2% to about $2.26 billion, gross margin was roughly 13%, adjusted EBITDA dropped about 75% to $19.5 million, and the company swung to a $68.0 million net loss from a $30.4 million profit the year before. Management tied the decline to tax-credit uncertainty and legislative changes that delayed customer contracting, an Arizona facility ramp delay, and lower volumes. Remaining performance obligations, its backlog, stood at $5.3 billion at fiscal year-end, of which 13% was tied to founding shareholder AES.
qualifier: from the FY2025 annual report
vault_ref: FLNC company page / commercial structure + FY2025 financial snapshot

section_title: The supply-chain caveat: China cells, one U.S. plant, and the tariff rules
body_text: The systems Fluence integrates depend on lithium-ion cells that come largely from Chinese manufacturers - the baseline supplier set includes CATL, BYD, LG and Samsung SDI, with the CATL relationship confirmed in Fluence's own disclosures. Against that dependence sits a U.S. domestic-content strategy: battery modules from a Utah contract manufacturer and battery cells from a plant in Smyrna, Tennessee. That plant's ownership shifted on March 31, 2026, when AESC sold a majority interest to Fixx Energy, a subsidiary of Longroad Energy; Fluence signed a new supply agreement with the new owner covering the next few years, and the cells continue to qualify for tax credits under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. The domestic-content advantage is real but conditional: prohibited-foreign-entity restrictions apply, with implementation regulations not due until December 31, 2026, and the annual report flags that compliance as an ongoing risk and a potential driver of customer contracting delays. On competition, when CATL and BYD moved to integrate vertically, management's own read was that it had not meaningfully changed the intensity of the market.
qualifier: from the FY2025 annual report and the Q2 FY2026 earnings call
vault_ref: FLNC company page / lithium-ion cell supply chain + tax-credit eligibility

section_title: The espresso advantage - what the cells actually do
body_text: Amprius makes high-energy-density lithium-ion battery cells built on a silicon-anode design - its commercial platform is called SiCore, and an older SiMaxx line is winding down. The plain way to picture it: a standard graphite battery is like drip coffee, and these cells are the concentrated version, holding the same energy in a much smaller, lighter package. When you roughly double the energy in a given size and weight, you can roughly double a drone's flight time or a light electric vehicle's range. That energy-per-kilogram edge is the entire product and the reason the cells get designed into long-endurance drones and other weight-sensitive aircraft. It is a genuine technical advantage in a fiercely competitive lithium-ion market - not a monopoly on a scarce material, but a design edge that has to be re-won against graphite cells every design cycle.
qualifier: from the annual filing and earnings call
vault_ref: AMPX company page / silicon-anode energy-density role

section_title: Fast growth, from a very small base
body_text: Revenue grew fast off a tiny starting point. For the full year it was $73.0 million, up about 202% from $24.2 million, driven almost entirely by SiCore cell sales; last quarter it was $28.5 million, up 153% year over year and 13% sequentially. Gross margin only recently turned positive - roughly 11% for the full year and about 20% last quarter - and management targets 25% for the full year, a target it says is still in sight but not yet reached. One customer alone was about $27 million, roughly 37% of last year's revenue, and the company does not name it. This is real commercial traction, but on a base small enough that a single customer and a single margin quarter move the whole picture.
qualifier: from the annual filing and quarterly filing
vault_ref: AMPX company page / revenue growth and customer concentration

section_title: Losing money, burning cash, funded by issuing shares
body_text: Amprius is loss-making and cash-burning, and it funds itself by issuing stock rather than debt. Net loss was $44.0 million for the full year and $5.0 million last quarter. Operating cash burn was $37.3 million in the quarter alone - reflecting a receivables build as revenue landed late in the quarter, part of it already collected - which took cash from $90.5 million to $62.4 million, with no debt. The company walked away from its planned gigawatt-scale plant in Brighton, Colorado, taking a $22.5 million write-down and paying $20.0 million to settle the lease, and pivoted to an asset-light model that leans on outside contract manufacturers in Korea and the U.S. It funds the ramp partly through an at-the-market share program and a warrant exchange. Management says its cash is sufficient to cover obligations over the next twelve months and guides toward a positive adjusted profitability measure for the year, but it also cautions it may be unable to raise more money on favorable terms, or at all.
